q: A company stores sensitive customer PII in an S3 bucket. The security team requires that all data be encrypted using a key they fully control and can rotate on demand. Which solution meets this requirement?
options: Use client-side encryption with an AWS-generated key
Enable SSE-S3 on the bucket
Enable SSE-KMS using a customer-managed KMS key
Enable SSE-KMS using the AWS-managed S3 key
explain: SSE-KMS with a customer-managed key (CMK) gives the customer full control over key policy, rotation, and auditing via CloudTrail. SSE-S3 uses AWS-managed keys. The AWS-managed S3 key cannot be controlled or rotated by the customer. Client-side encryption with AWS-generated keys does not give full control.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: full key control + rotation + audit → KMS CMK (customer-managed key). 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: SSE-KMS with a customer-managed key (CMK) gives the customer full control over key policy, rotation, and auditing via CloudTrail. SSE-S3 uses AWS-managed keys. The AWS-managed S3 key cannot be controlled or rotated by the customer. Client-side encryption with AWS-generated keys does not give full control.
keywords: Amazon S3
AWS CloudTrail (API call audit log: who, what, when, from where)
AWS Organizations
AWS Secrets Manager (auto-rotation)
AWS Security Hub (central findings)
AWS Shield Standard/Advanced
AWS WAF (L7 HTTP inspection)
Client-side encryption (encrypt before upload, AWS sees only ciphertext)
EBS encryption (CMK or AWS-managed, snapshots inherit encryption)
Envelope encryption (data encrypted by DEK, DEK encrypted by KMS CMK)
IAM Policy
identity-based policy
KMS CMK automatic rotation (annual, optional for CMK)
resource-based policy
S3 Access Points (named access control per app/department/team)
S3 Event Notifications → SQS, SNS, Lambda, EventBridge
S3 MFA Delete (requires MFA to delete versions or suspend versioning)
S3 Object Lock (WORM): Compliance mode (no one can delete) vs Governance mode (admin can override)
S3 Presigned URL (time-limited access to private object, generated by owner)
S3 Replication: SRR (same-region) vs CRR (cross-region), requires versioning ON

q: A Solutions Architect needs to ensure EC2 instances in a private subnet can access AWS services without traversing the public internet. Which solution achieves this with the least operational overhead?
options: Use an internet gateway with restrictive security groups
Set up a Site-to-Site VPN to AWS
Deploy a NAT Gateway in a public subnet
Create VPC endpoints for the required AWS services
explain: VPC endpoints (Gateway or Interface) allow private connectivity to AWS services without internet access. NAT Gateway routes traffic to the public internet. VPN and internet gateway both involve internet exposure.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: private access without internet → VPC Endpoint (Gateway=free for S3/DDB, Interface=paid for others). 📋 Task 1.2 — Design secure workloads and applications: Secure workloads = protect at multiple layers. Attack type decision: SQL injection/XSS/OWASP → WAF (L7). Large-scale DDoS → Shield Advanced. Threat detection/anomaly → GuardDuty. Vulnerability scanning → Inspector. S3 sensitive data discovery → Macie. Centralized security findings → Security Hub. Secret storage with rotation → Secrets Manager. Config data (non-secret) → Parameter Store (free) or Secrets Manager (paid, auto-rotate). Network layers: NACL (stateless, subnet boundary) → SG (stateful, instance boundary) → WAF (application boundary). 💡 Multiple VPC connectivity: Few VPCs (≤3) → VPC Peering (simple, free). Many VPCs → Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke, centralised routing). On-prem to AWS: Need encryption + speed → DX + VPN over DX. Need encryption + cheap → Site-to-Site VPN. Need high bandwidth + consistent + NOT encrypted → Direct Connect alone. Expose a service to another VPC without full access → PrivateLink (not peering). DNS on-prem → VPC: Route 53 Resolver inbound/outbound endpoints. ❌ Eliminate distractors: VPC endpoints (Gateway or Interface) allow private connectivity to AWS services without internet access. NAT Gateway routes traffic to the public internet. VPN and internet gateway both involve internet exposure.
keywords: Amazon EC2
Amazon VPC
AWS Direct Connect: private dedicated circuit, 1/10/100 Gbps, NOT encrypted by default
AWS KMS CMK
AWS PrivateLink / VPC Interface Endpoint: expose service to other VPCs without peering
AWS Secrets Manager (auto-rotation)
AWS Security Hub (central findings)
AWS Shield Standard/Advanced
AWS Site-to-Site VPN
AWS WAF (L7 HTTP inspection)
Client VPN: OpenVPN, remote users access VPC
DX + VPN over DX: encrypted + consistent bandwidth (best of both)
least privilege
Route 53 Resolver: DNS between on-prem and VPC (Inbound/Outbound endpoints)
Security Groups (stateful L4)
Site-to-Site VPN: encrypted, over internet, quick to set up, variable latency
SSE-KMS with AWS-managed key (limited control)
Transit Gateway: hub-and-spoke, transitive routing, 1000s VPCs, VPN attachments, DX attachments
VPC Peering: 1:1, non-transitive (A↔B + B↔C ≠ A↔C), same/cross-region, no overlapping CIDR

q: A web application on EC2 is receiving SQL injection attacks. The security team wants to block these at the network edge with minimal application changes. What should the architect recommend?
options: Use VPC NACLs to filter HTTP traffic
Configure Security Groups to block malicious IPs
Deploy AWS WAF with SQL injection match rules in front of the Application Load Balancer
Enable AWS Shield Advanced on the EC2 instances
explain: AWS WAF inspects HTTP/HTTPS content and can block SQL injection patterns using managed rule groups or custom rules. Security Groups and NACLs operate at layer 3/4 and cannot inspect HTTP payloads. Shield Advanced protects against DDoS, not application-layer injection attacks.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: application-layer attack (L7) → WAF (not NACLs/SGs which are L3/L4) | DDoS attack → Shield Advanced (+ 24/7 DRT + cost protection). 📋 Task 1.2 — Design secure workloads and applications: Secure workloads = protect at multiple layers. Attack type decision: SQL injection/XSS/OWASP → WAF (L7). Large-scale DDoS → Shield Advanced. Threat detection/anomaly → GuardDuty. Vulnerability scanning → Inspector. S3 sensitive data discovery → Macie. Centralized security findings → Security Hub. Secret storage with rotation → Secrets Manager. Config data (non-secret) → Parameter Store (free) or Secrets Manager (paid, auto-rotate). Network layers: NACL (stateless, subnet boundary) → SG (stateful, instance boundary) → WAF (application boundary). 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. 💡 ELB type selection: Web application (HTTP/HTTPS) + routing rules + WAF → ALB. Extreme performance (millions RPS), static IP, TCP/UDP, source IP preservation → NLB. Third-party security appliances (firewall, IDS) inline → GWLB. Need PrivateLink endpoint → NLB. WebSocket → ALB. Key: ALB = smart routing at L7. NLB = dumb fast routing at L4. NLB has static IP per AZ (useful for whitelisting). ALB has dynamic IPs. ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS WAF inspects HTTP/HTTPS content and can block SQL injection patterns using managed rule groups or custom rules. Security Groups and NACLs operate at layer 3/4 and cannot inspect HTTP payloads. Shield Advanced protects against DDoS, not application-layer injection attacks.

q: An application needs to call AWS APIs from EC2 instances. The security team prohibits storing long-term credentials on instances. What is the correct approach?
options: Hardcode credentials in the application configuration file
Store IAM user access keys in environment variables
Attach an IAM role to the EC2 instances
Use AWS Secrets Manager to store and retrieve IAM keys
explain: IAM roles for EC2 provide temporary credentials via the instance metadata service (IMDS). These are automatically rotated and require no static credentials. Storing long-term keys anywhere on the instance (env vars, files, Secrets Manager) is against best practices.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: no long-term credentials → IAM Role on EC2 (IMDS provides temp credentials). 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). ❌ Eliminate distractors: IAM roles for EC2 provide temporary credentials via the instance metadata service (IMDS). These are automatically rotated and require no static credentials. Storing long-term keys anywhere on the instance (env vars, files, Secrets Manager) is against best practices.

q: A company wants to prevent anyone, including the root account, from disabling AWS CloudTrail logs in their organization. Which approach enforces this?
options: Configure CloudTrail with a dedicated AWS account
Create a Service Control Policy (SCP) that denies cloudtrail:StopLogging and attach it to the root OU
Enable MFA Delete on the CloudTrail S3 bucket
Enable CloudTrail log file validation
explain: SCPs in AWS Organizations can deny specific actions across all accounts, including member accounts with admin users. Even the root user of a member account cannot override an SCP. MFA Delete protects the S3 bucket but not the trail itself. Log validation detects tampering but doesn't prevent disabling.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: prevent across org (even root) → SCP on root OU. 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 IAM policy evaluation: deny wins always. Order: Explicit Deny > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary > session policy. Cross-account access: Role with trust policy + AssumeRole. OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. Permission boundary = limits what a role/user can do even if their policy grants more. AWS RAM = share VPCs, subnets, Transit Gateways across accounts (no peering required). 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: SCPs in AWS Organizations can deny specific actions across all accounts, including member accounts with admin users. Even the root user of a member account cannot override an SCP. MFA Delete protects the S3 bucket but not the trail itself. Log validation detects tampering but doesn't prevent disabling.

Permission Boundary: max permissions an IAM entity can have (used by delegated admins)
Resource-based policy: attached to resource (S3 bucket, KMS key, SQS), allows cross-account without AssumeRole

q: An application uses Amazon RDS. The security team requires that database passwords be automatically rotated every 30 days and never stored in code. What is the recommended solution?
options: Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store Standard tier
Encrypt the password using KMS and store in DynamoDB
Store the password in an S3 bucket with server-side encryption
Use AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation enabled
explain: AWS Secrets Manager natively integrates with RDS for automatic password rotation. It stores and retrieves secrets securely via API, eliminating hardcoded credentials. Parameter Store does not support automatic rotation for RDS. S3 and DynamoDB are not designed for secret management with automatic rotation.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: no long-term credentials → IAM Role on EC2 (IMDS provides temp credentials). 📋 Task 1.2 — Design secure workloads and applications: Secure workloads = protect at multiple layers. Attack type decision: SQL injection/XSS/OWASP → WAF (L7). Large-scale DDoS → Shield Advanced. Threat detection/anomaly → GuardDuty. Vulnerability scanning → Inspector. S3 sensitive data discovery → Macie. Centralized security findings → Security Hub. Secret storage with rotation → Secrets Manager. Config data (non-secret) → Parameter Store (free) or Secrets Manager (paid, auto-rotate). Network layers: NACL (stateless, subnet boundary) → SG (stateful, instance boundary) → WAF (application boundary). 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS Secrets Manager natively integrates with RDS for automatic password rotation. It stores and retrieves secrets securely via API, eliminating hardcoded credentials. Parameter Store does not support automatic rotation for RDS. S3 and DynamoDB are not designed for secret management with automatic rotation.

Amazon DynamoDB
Amazon GuardDuty (threat detection)

q: A company needs to federate corporate Active Directory users into AWS without creating IAM users for each person. Which solution achieves this?
options: Create an IAM group for each AD group and sync users manually
Provide all users with a shared IAM user credential
Use Amazon Cognito User Pools to mirror AD accounts
Use AWS Directory Service AD Connector to proxy authentication and assume IAM roles via SAML federation
explain: AD Connector proxies authentication requests to on-premises AD. Combined with SAML 2.0 federation, users authenticate against AD and receive temporary AWS credentials via AssumeRoleWithSAML. No IAM users are created. Shared credentials violate least-privilege principles.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. 💡 IAM policy evaluation: deny wins always. Order: Explicit Deny > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary > session policy. Cross-account access: Role with trust policy + AssumeRole. OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. Permission boundary = limits what a role/user can do even if their policy grants more. AWS RAM = share VPCs, subnets, Transit Gateways across accounts (no peering required). ❌ Eliminate distractors: AD Connector proxies authentication requests to on-premises AD. Combined with SAML 2.0 federation, users authenticate against AD and receive temporary AWS credentials via AssumeRoleWithSAML. No IAM users are created. Shared credentials violate least-privilege principles.

AWS Transfer Family (SFTP/FTPS/FTP to S3 or EFS)

q: A Solutions Architect is reviewing S3 bucket policies and finds a bucket with 's3:GetObject' allowed for principal '*'. What is the security risk and correct remediation?
options: The risk is minimal if the objects are encrypted with SSE-S3
The bucket is publicly readable; remove the wildcard principal or restrict to specific AWS accounts/roles
Add a bucket ACL to override the bucket policy
The bucket is publicly readable; add a condition for aws:SecureTransport only
explain: Principal '*' makes the bucket publicly accessible to anyone on the internet. The correct fix is to remove the wildcard or scope it to specific principals. Encryption does not prevent public read access — anyone can still retrieve and decrypt SSE-S3 objects. ACLs cannot override a permissive bucket policy.

q: A multi-account AWS environment needs centralized security findings from GuardDuty, Security Hub, and Inspector. Which service aggregates findings across accounts with the least complexity?
options: AWS Security Hub with a delegated administrator account
Amazon CloudWatch cross-account dashboards
AWS Config aggregator
AWS Trusted Advisor
explain: Security Hub aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and more across all accounts in an AWS Organization when a delegated administrator is configured. Config aggregators focus on configuration compliance. CloudWatch dashboards don't aggregate security findings. Trusted Advisor has limited cross-account capability.

Order: Explicit Deny > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary > session policy. Cross-account access: Role with trust policy + AssumeRole. OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. Permission boundary = limits what a role/user can do even if their policy grants more. AWS RAM = share VPCs, subnets, Transit Gateways across accounts (no peering required). 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Security Hub aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and more across all accounts in an AWS Organization when a delegated administrator is configured. Config aggregators focus on configuration compliance. CloudWatch dashboards don't aggregate security findings. Trusted Advisor has limited cross-account capability.

q: A company runs a public API on API Gateway. They want to allow only requests from their corporate IP range and block all others. What is the simplest way to implement this?
options: Enable API Gateway resource policies with IP conditions
Use a Lambda authorizer to check request IPs
Deploy a WAF WebACL with an IP set allow rule and associate it with API Gateway
Configure a Security Group on the API Gateway
explain: AWS WAF with an IP set allow rule attached to API Gateway is the most straightforward approach. API Gateway does not support security groups (it is a managed service, not VPC-native). Resource policies can also restrict IPs but WAF provides more flexibility and is purpose-built for this. Lambda authorizers add latency and cost for simple IP filtering.

q: An S3 bucket hosts a static website. The architect must ensure all traffic uses HTTPS and HTTP requests are redirected. Which combination achieves this?
options: Enable S3 static website hosting with HTTPS — S3 static hosting natively supports HTTPS
Use an Application Load Balancer with ACM certificate in front of S3
Attach an SSL certificate directly to the S3 bucket
Put CloudFront in front of S3, configure the CloudFront distribution to redirect HTTP to HTTPS, use an ACM certificate
explain: S3 static website hosting does not support HTTPS natively. CloudFront with an ACM certificate provides HTTPS termination and can enforce HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects via viewer protocol policy. ALB cannot serve S3 static website content directly without additional configuration.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 ELB type selection: Web application (HTTP/HTTPS) + routing rules + WAF → ALB. Extreme performance (millions RPS), static IP, TCP/UDP, source IP preservation → NLB. Third-party security appliances (firewall, IDS) inline → GWLB. Need PrivateLink endpoint → NLB. WebSocket → ALB. Key: ALB = smart routing at L7. NLB = dumb fast routing at L4. NLB has static IP per AZ (useful for whitelisting). ALB has dynamic IPs. ❌ Eliminate distractors: S3 static website hosting does not support HTTPS natively. CloudFront with an ACM certificate provides HTTPS termination and can enforce HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects via viewer protocol policy. ALB cannot serve S3 static website content directly without additional configuration.

q: A company needs to share encrypted AMIs across multiple AWS accounts. The AMI is encrypted with a customer-managed KMS key. What additional step is required?
options: Share the KMS key policy to allow the target accounts to use the key, then share the AMI
Re-encrypt the AMI with the AWS-managed EC2 key before sharing
Copy the AMI to each account without encryption
Enable cross-account AMI sharing in AWS Organizations — it handles key permissions automatically
explain: When sharing an encrypted AMI, the target account must have kms:DescribeKey, kms:ReEncrypt*, kms:CreateGrant, and kms:Decrypt permissions on the KMS key. Both the AMI and the key must be shared. AWS does not automatically handle this — explicit key policy updates are required.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 IAM policy evaluation: deny wins always. Order: Explicit Deny > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary > session policy. Cross-account access: Role with trust policy + AssumeRole. OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. Permission boundary = limits what a role/user can do even if their policy grants more. AWS RAM = share VPCs, subnets, Transit Gateways across accounts (no peering required). ❌ Eliminate distractors: When sharing an encrypted AMI, the target account must have kms:DescribeKey, kms:ReEncrypt*, kms:CreateGrant, and kms:Decrypt permissions on the KMS key. q: A Lambda function needs to read a secret from Secrets Manager. The function is also logging to CloudWatch. Using least-privilege IAM, which policy should be attached to the Lambda execution role?
options: AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole only — it includes Secrets Manager access
AdministratorAccess managed policy
A custom policy allowing secretsmanager:GetSecretValue for the specific secret ARN and logs:CreateLogGroup, logs:CreateLogStream, logs:PutLogEvents for the log group
AmazonSecretsManagerReadWrite plus CloudWatchFullAccess
explain: Least privilege requires granting only the specific actions on specific resources needed. A custom policy scoped to the exact secret ARN and log group is correct. Managed policies like ReadWrite or FullAccess grant far more permissions than needed. AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole only grants logging, not Secrets Manager access.

q: A company needs to enforce that all EBS volumes created in an AWS account are encrypted by default. What is the most efficient approach?
options: Enable EBS encryption by default in the EC2 console or via AWS Config remediation
Use an SCP to deny ec2:CreateVolume unless encryption is specified
Deploy an EventBridge rule to delete unencrypted volumes on creation
Use a CloudFormation StackSet to enforce encryption in all regions
explain: Enabling EBS default encryption is an account-level setting that automatically encrypts all new EBS volumes and snapshots. This is the simplest and most reliable approach. SCPs add complexity. CloudFormation StackSets only cover resources created via CloudFormation. EventBridge-based deletion is reactive and causes data loss risk.

SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Enabling EBS default encryption is an account-level setting that automatically encrypts all new EBS volumes and snapshots. q: An application needs to allow users to upload files directly to S3 without routing through an application server. The uploads should be secure and expire after 15 minutes. What mechanism enables this?
options: Generate a pre-signed URL with a 15-minute expiration from the application server
Make the S3 bucket public and use client-side encryption
Create a temporary IAM user and provide credentials to the client
Use an S3 Transfer Acceleration URL with a time-based token
explain: Pre-signed URLs grant temporary, time-limited access to perform a specific S3 operation (PUT in this case) without exposing AWS credentials to the client. They are generated server-side using the application's IAM credentials. Public buckets expose all objects. Temporary IAM users are operationally complex and not recommended.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 📋 Task 1.3 — Determine appropriate data security controls: Data security = encryption at rest + in transit + access control + backup. Key control spectrum (least → most): SSE-S3 → SSE-KMS (AWS key) → SSE-KMS (CMK) → Client-side. If question says 'full control', 'rotate on demand', 'audit key usage' → CMK. Encryption in transit → ACM + HTTPS/TLS. S3 public access protection → S3 Block Public Access + bucket policy. Compliance retention → Versioning + MFA Delete + S3 Object Lock (WORM). Backup strategy → AWS Backup for centralised cross-service backup. 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). q: A company's security policy requires all API calls to AWS be logged and the logs must be tamper-evident. CloudTrail is already enabled. What additional step ensures log integrity?

q: A microservices application running in ECS needs to authenticate to an RDS database. The security team wants credentials rotated automatically without restarting containers. What is the best approach?
options: Use SSM Parameter Store SecureString and restart tasks after each rotation
Store credentials in ECS task definition environment variables
Use AWS Secrets Manager with ECS integration — the ECS agent injects the secret and Secrets Manager handles rotation
Bake the database password into the Docker image
explain: Secrets Manager integrates natively with ECS task definitions to inject secrets at container startup and supports automatic rotation. Tasks don't need to be restarted when Secrets Manager rotates the RDS password because ECS can be configured to retrieve the latest version. Hardcoding or storing in environment variables are security anti-patterns.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: no long-term credentials → IAM Role on EC2 (IMDS provides temp credentials). 📋 Task 1.2 — Design secure workloads and applications: Secure workloads = protect at multiple layers. Attack type decision: SQL injection/XSS/OWASP → WAF (L7). Large-scale DDoS → Shield Advanced. Threat detection/anomaly → GuardDuty. Vulnerability scanning → Inspector. S3 sensitive data discovery → Macie. Centralized security findings → Security Hub. Secret storage with rotation → Secrets Manager. Config data (non-secret) → Parameter Store (free) or Secrets Manager (paid, auto-rotate). Network layers: NACL (stateless, subnet boundary) → SG (stateful, instance boundary) → WAF (application boundary). 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Secrets Manager integrates natively with ECS task definitions to inject secrets at container startup and supports automatic rotation. Tasks don't need to be restarted when Secrets Manager rotates the RDS password because ECS can be configured to retrieve the latest version. Hardcoding or storing in environment variables are security anti-patterns.

q: A company is using AWS Organizations and wants to prevent member accounts from leaving the organization. Which control achieves this?
options: Remove the member account's IAM billing access
Create an SCP that denies organizations:LeaveOrganization
Apply a permission boundary to all IAM users in member accounts
Enable AWS Config in all member accounts
explain: An SCP denying organizations:LeaveOrganization attached to all OUs or the root prevents member accounts from leaving. SCPs override any IAM policy in member accounts. Config tracks configuration changes but doesn't prevent organization changes. Permission boundaries apply to IAM entities, not account-level organization actions.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: prevent across org (even root) → SCP on root OU. 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 IAM policy evaluation: deny wins always. Order: Explicit Deny > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary > session policy. Cross-account access: Role with trust policy + AssumeRole. OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. Permission boundary = limits what a role/user can do even if their policy grants more. AWS RAM = share VPCs, subnets, Transit Gateways across accounts (no peering required). ❌ Eliminate distractors: An SCP denying organizations:LeaveOrganization attached to all OUs or the root prevents member accounts from leaving. SCPs override any IAM policy in member accounts. Config tracks configuration changes but doesn't prevent organization changes. Permission boundaries apply to IAM entities, not account-level organization actions.

q: A regulated company must ensure that sensitive data in S3 is never transmitted without encryption in transit. How can they enforce this?
options: Enable HTTPS on the application accessing S3
Add a bucket policy that denies any request where aws:SecureTransport is false
Configure AWS Config to detect unencrypted S3 requests
Enable S3 default encryption
explain: A bucket policy with Condition: {Bool: {aws:SecureTransport: false}} and Effect: Deny blocks any unencrypted HTTP requests to the bucket at the API level. Default encryption protects data at rest, not in transit. Application-level HTTPS doesn't protect all clients. Config detects but doesn't enforce.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: A bucket policy with Condition: {Bool: {aws:SecureTransport: false}} and Effect: Deny blocks any unencrypted HTTP requests to the bucket at the API level. Default encryption protects data at rest, not in transit. Application-level HTTPS doesn't protect all clients. Config detects but doesn't enforce.

q: A company uses multiple AWS accounts and wants to centralize DNS resolution between a shared services VPC and workload VPCs. What enables private DNS resolution across VPCs?
options: Deploy a BIND DNS server in the shared VPC and use as forwarder
Create public Route 53 hosted zones and share across accounts
Use Route 53 Resolver with inbound/outbound endpoints and Route 53 Resolver rules shared via RAM
Peer all VPCs and update each VPC's DHCP options set
explain: Route 53 Resolver with Resolver rules shared through AWS RAM allows centralized DNS forwarding between VPCs across accounts without routing all DNS through a custom server. Simple VPC peering doesn't resolve private hosted zones across accounts. BIND servers add operational overhead. Public hosted zones expose internal DNS.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 📋 Task 1.3 — Determine appropriate data security controls: Data security = encryption at rest + in transit + access control + backup. Key control spectrum (least → most): SSE-S3 → SSE-KMS (AWS key) → SSE-KMS (CMK) → Client-side. If question says 'full control', 'rotate on demand', 'audit key usage' → CMK. Encryption in transit → ACM + HTTPS/TLS. S3 public access protection → S3 Block Public Access + bucket policy. Compliance retention → Versioning + MFA Delete + S3 Object Lock (WORM). Backup strategy → AWS Backup for centralised cross-service backup. 💡 Multiple VPC connectivity: Few VPCs (≤3) → VPC Peering (simple, free). Many VPCs → Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke, centralised routing). On-prem to AWS: Need encryption + speed → DX + VPN over DX. Need encryption + cheap → Site-to-Site VPN. Need high bandwidth + consistent + NOT encrypted → Direct Connect alone. Expose a service to another VPC without full access → PrivateLink (not peering). DNS on-prem → VPC: Route 53 Resolver inbound/outbound endpoints. 💡 IAM policy evaluation: deny wins always. Order: Explicit Deny > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary > session policy. Cross-account access: Role with trust policy + AssumeRole. OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. Permission boundary = limits what a role/user can do even if their policy grants more. AWS RAM = share VPCs, subnets, Transit Gateways across accounts (no peering required). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Route 53 Resolver with Resolver rules shared through AWS RAM allows centralized DNS forwarding between VPCs across accounts without routing all DNS through a custom server. Simple VPC peering doesn't resolve private hosted zones across accounts. BIND servers add operational overhead. Public hosted zones expose internal DNS.

q: An application stores encryption keys in code. The security team requires these be removed and managed centrally with key rotation. The application currently uses AES-256. Which AWS service replaces this?

q: A company needs to ensure that developers cannot launch EC2 instances larger than t3.medium in the development account. What enforces this?

q: A company's application writes sensitive logs to CloudWatch Logs. The security team requires these logs be encrypted with a customer-managed key. How is this configured?

q: A Lambda function is being invoked by an S3 event notification. An attacker has modified the S3 event to inject unexpected data into the Lambda function. What security control reduces this risk?
options: Add input validation within the Lambda function code to sanitize and validate all incoming event data
Enable Lambda code signing
Encrypt the Lambda function with KMS
Use an S3 bucket policy to prevent external PUT operations
explain: Input validation within Lambda is the primary defense against injection via malformed events. Even trusted event sources can send unexpected data — code should never trust event data without validation. Bucket policies prevent unauthorized uploads but don't validate event content. Code signing ensures code integrity, not input safety. KMS encryption protects code at rest.

q: A company needs to meet PCI DSS requirements that mandate network segmentation of cardholder data. Which VPC design best supports this?

q: A company discovered that a former employee's IAM access key is still active and was last used 6 months ago. What is the immediate remediation?
options: Move the user to a restricted IAM group
Change the IAM user's password
Rotate the access key to generate a new one
Deactivate or delete the access key immediately and audit all API calls made with it in CloudTrail
explain: Immediately deactivating or deleting a stale credential prevents unauthorized use. CloudTrail audit of the key's usage reveals if it was accessed maliciously. Rotation generates a new key but the old key remains valid until deleted. Password changes don't affect access keys. Moving to a restricted group doesn't revoke existing key permissions immediately.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: full key control + rotation + audit → KMS CMK (customer-managed key). 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Immediately deactivating or deleting a stale credential prevents unauthorized use. CloudTrail audit of the key's usage reveals if it was accessed maliciously. Rotation generates a new key but the old key remains valid until deleted. Password changes don't affect access keys. Moving to a restricted group doesn't revoke existing key permissions immediately.

q: An application needs to access AWS services from on-premises servers without storing long-term IAM credentials. What is the recommended approach?
options: Use AWS IAM Roles Anywhere with a certificate authority to issue X.509 certificates for temporary credentials
Use an EC2 instance profile from on-premises
Hard-code root account credentials in the application configuration
Create an IAM user with an access key and store on the server
explain: IAM Roles Anywhere extends IAM roles to workloads outside AWS using X.509 certificates from a trusted CA. It provides temporary, automatically-rotated credentials without long-term access keys. Instance profiles are only available on EC2. Long-term keys and root credentials are security anti-patterns.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: no long-term credentials → IAM Role on EC2 (IMDS provides temp credentials) | on-prem file server → Storage Gateway File Gateway (NFS/SMB → S3). 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. ❌ Eliminate distractors: IAM Roles Anywhere extends IAM roles to workloads outside AWS using X.509 certificates from a trusted CA. It provides temporary, automatically-rotated credentials without long-term access keys. Instance profiles are only available on EC2. Long-term keys and root credentials are security anti-patterns.

q: A company wants to prevent accidental deletion of critical DynamoDB tables in production. What is the most effective safeguard?
options: Enable DynamoDB Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR)
Enable DynamoDB deletion protection on the table
Remove dynamodb:DeleteTable permission from all developers
Enable AWS Backup for DynamoDB
explain: DynamoDB deletion protection is a table-level setting that prevents DeleteTable API calls regardless of IAM permissions. It must be explicitly disabled before a table can be deleted. PITR enables recovery after deletion but doesn't prevent it. IAM permission removal helps but can be bypassed by admins. AWS Backup provides recovery, not prevention.

q: A financial company must ensure that data at rest in RDS PostgreSQL is encrypted and that the encryption key is dedicated to their environment. Which storage option meets this requirement?

q: A company runs multiple AWS accounts. Security team needs a single pane of glass showing all IAM users across accounts without requiring console access to each account. Which solution provides this?
options: Use AWS Security Hub with cross-account aggregation and IAM findings
Deploy AWS Config aggregator with the iam-user-no-policies-check rule across all accounts
Use IAM Access Analyzer with a delegated admin account
Use AWS Organizations console to list all IAM users
explain: AWS Config aggregator collects configuration data from multiple accounts. The iam-user-no-policies-check rule (and similar IAM rules) provides visibility into IAM user configurations. IAM Access Analyzer focuses on external access risks. Organizations doesn't enumerate IAM users. Security Hub focuses on security findings, not IAM user inventory.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 📋 Task 1.2 — Design secure workloads and applications: Secure workloads = protect at multiple layers. Attack type decision: SQL injection/XSS/OWASP → WAF (L7). Large-scale DDoS → Shield Advanced. Threat detection/anomaly → GuardDuty. Vulnerability scanning → Inspector. S3 sensitive data discovery → Macie. Centralized security findings → Security Hub. Secret storage with rotation → Secrets Manager. Config data (non-secret) → Parameter Store (free) or Secrets Manager (paid, auto-rotate). Network layers: NACL (stateless, subnet boundary) → SG (stateful, instance boundary) → WAF (application boundary). 💡 IAM policy evaluation: deny wins always. Order: Explicit Deny > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary > session policy. Cross-account access: Role with trust policy + AssumeRole. OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. Permission boundary = limits what a role/user can do even if their policy grants more. AWS RAM = share VPCs, subnets, Transit Gateways across accounts (no peering required). 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS Config aggregator collects configuration data from multiple accounts. The iam-user-no-policies-check rule (and similar IAM rules) provides visibility into IAM user configurations. IAM Access Analyzer focuses on external access risks. Organizations doesn't enumerate IAM users. Security Hub focuses on security findings, not IAM user inventory.

q: A company wants to enforce that all Lambda functions use VPC networking and that no Lambda function has internet access. Which control achieves this across all accounts?
options: Create an IAM policy denying lambda:CreateFunction without VPC config
Use AWS Config rule lambda-inside-vpc and lambda-function-public-access-prohibited with auto-remediation
Deploy an SCP denying lambda:CreateFunction — developers must request exceptions
Set a VPC as default for Lambda in each account's settings
explain: AWS Config rules can detect Lambda functions not in a VPC or with public access, and auto-remediation can invoke an SSM Automation or Lambda function to remediate. This is detective + corrective. Lambda doesn't have an account-level VPC default setting. An SCP completely blocking Lambda creation is too restrictive for normal operations.

OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. q: A company hosts a REST API on Amazon API Gateway. They need to authenticate API consumers using JSON Web Tokens (JWT) issued by their own identity provider. What is the most appropriate authentication method?
options: API keys with usage plans
API Gateway Lambda authorizer that calls the identity provider
API Gateway JWT authorizer configured with the identity provider's JWKS URI
IAM authorization with SigV4 signing
explain: API Gateway HTTP APIs support native JWT authorizers that validate tokens using the identity provider's JWKS endpoint without requiring Lambda. This provides low-latency, low-cost token validation. Lambda authorizers add latency and cost. IAM auth requires AWS credentials, not JWT tokens. API keys are for throttling/metering, not authentication.

q: A company's security team wants to receive alerts when root account login occurs. Which combination of services provides this?
options: Use IAM credential reports sent to SNS on a schedule

Enable CloudTrail, create a CloudWatch Logs metric filter for root login events, and create a CloudWatch Alarm with SNS notification

explain: CloudTrail captures all API and console events including root logins. q: A web application needs to authenticate users through social identity providers (Google, Facebook). The application is hosted on EC2. Which AWS service handles this with minimal custom code?
options: Amazon Cognito User Pools with social identity provider federation
Create IAM users for each social identity
AWS STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity called directly from the front end
AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO)
explain: Cognito User Pools natively federate with social identity providers and handles OIDC/OAuth flows, token management, and user directory. It abstracts the complexity of integrating with each IdP. IAM Identity Center is for workforce identity (employees). Direct STS calls require custom federation code. Creating IAM users for social identities is not scalable.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: decoupling → SQS (queue), SNS (pub/sub), EventBridge (events). 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 IAM policy evaluation: deny wins always. Order: Explicit Deny > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary > session policy. Cross-account access: Role with trust policy + AssumeRole. OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. Permission boundary = limits what a role/user can do even if their policy grants more. AWS RAM = share VPCs, subnets, Transit Gateways across accounts (no peering required). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Cognito User Pools natively federate with social identity providers and handles OIDC/OAuth flows, token management, and user directory. It abstracts the complexity of integrating with each IdP. IAM Identity Center is for workforce identity (employees). Direct STS calls require custom federation code. Creating IAM users for social identities is not scalable.

q: A company needs a hardware security module (HSM) to perform cryptographic operations for a PKI implementation requiring FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validation. Which AWS service meets this?
options: ACM Private CA with hardware backing
AWS CloudHSM
AWS Secrets Manager with RSA encryption
AWS KMS with a custom key store
explain: AWS CloudHSM provides dedicated FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated HSM hardware. KMS custom key stores integrate with CloudHSM but CloudHSM itself provides the hardware. KMS alone is FIPS 140-2 Level 2. ACM Private CA uses KMS-backed keys, not dedicated hardware. Secrets Manager doesn't perform HSM-grade cryptographic operations.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: no long-term credentials → IAM Role on EC2 (IMDS provides temp credentials). 📋 Task 1.2 — Design secure workloads and applications: Secure workloads = protect at multiple layers. Attack type decision: SQL injection/XSS/OWASP → WAF (L7). Large-scale DDoS → Shield Advanced. Threat detection/anomaly → GuardDuty. Vulnerability scanning → Inspector. S3 sensitive data discovery → Macie. Centralized security findings → Security Hub. Secret storage with rotation → Secrets Manager. Config data (non-secret) → Parameter Store (free) or Secrets Manager (paid, auto-rotate). Network layers: NACL (stateless, subnet boundary) → SG (stateful, instance boundary) → WAF (application boundary). 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS CloudHSM provides dedicated FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated HSM hardware. KMS custom key stores integrate with CloudHSM but CloudHSM itself provides the hardware. KMS alone is FIPS 140-2 Level 2. ACM Private CA uses KMS-backed keys, not dedicated hardware. Secrets Manager doesn't perform HSM-grade cryptographic operations.

q: A company stores API keys for third-party services in SSM Parameter Store as SecureString parameters. Multiple Lambda functions access these. The security team wants automatic rotation. What is the recommended upgrade?

q: A company needs to prove to auditors that their AWS infrastructure configuration has not drifted from approved baselines over the past 12 months. Which AWS service provides this configuration history?
options: Amazon CloudWatch — it stores metrics and log data for 15 months
AWS Config — it records configuration snapshots and change history for all supported resources
AWS CloudTrail — it records all API calls made over time
AWS Systems Manager Inventory — it collects instance configuration data
explain: AWS Config maintains a continuous record of resource configuration changes and provides a timeline view of how configurations changed. It can also evaluate compliance against rules over time. CloudTrail records API calls but not the resulting resource configuration state. CloudWatch stores metrics and logs, not resource configuration history. Systems Manager Inventory covers EC2 instance metadata, not all AWS resources.

DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. q: An EC2-based application is transmitting sensitive data to another EC2 instance in the same VPC. The security team requires encryption in transit between the instances. What is the recommended approach?
options: Use an encrypted EBS volume on both instances
VPC traffic is encrypted by default — no additional steps are needed

Enable VPC Flow Logs encryption
explain: Traffic within a VPC is not encrypted by default at the network layer. Application-level TLS/mTLS provides encryption in transit between instances. q: A company wants to restrict access to an internal web application hosted on EC2 to only users connected to their corporate VPN. The VPN terminates on-premises and connects to AWS via Direct Connect. How should access be restricted?
options: Configure security groups to allow traffic only from the on-premises VPN CIDR range
Deploy AWS Client VPN and require authentication for all users
Use an internet-facing ALB with a WAF IP allow rule
Implement Cognito authentication on the application
explain: Since traffic from on-premises arrives via Direct Connect with a known CIDR range, security groups can restrict access to only that IP range at no extra cost with no additional services. WAF IP rules also work but add cost and complexity. Cognito adds authentication but doesn't restrict network access. AWS Client VPN is for users connecting without an existing corporate VPN/Direct Connect.

q: A company using AWS Organizations wants to ensure that all new accounts created automatically have GuardDuty enabled. What is the most automated approach?

q: A company has a strict requirement that no AWS service should be able to access S3 bucket objects unless explicitly granted. Currently, the bucket has no bucket policy. What is the default behavior?
options: By default, only the bucket owner (the AWS account) has access — no other principal has access unless granted

AWS-managed services (RDS, Lambda) get read access to all S3 buckets by default

explain: S3 follows a deny-by-default model. Without explicit grants via bucket policy, ACL, or IAM policy, no principal other than the bucket owner account has access. q: A company uses AWS KMS and wants to ensure that if a key is accidentally deleted, they have a recovery window. What feature provides this?
options: Enable KMS key replication to another region before deletion
Enable KMS key rotation — it keeps previous versions of the key
Export the key material before deletion
Schedule key deletion with a waiting period of 7–30 days — the key can be cancelled during this period
explain: KMS key deletion must be scheduled with a minimum 7-day and maximum 30-day waiting period. During this time, the deletion can be cancelled. This provides a safety net against accidental deletion. Key rotation creates new key versions but doesn't prevent deletion. Multi-region key replication requires setup before deletion. Exporting key material is only for imported key material keys.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 📋 Task 1.3 — Determine appropriate data security controls: Data security = encryption at rest + in transit + access control + backup. Key control spectrum (least → most): SSE-S3 → SSE-KMS (AWS key) → SSE-KMS (CMK) → Client-side. If question says 'full control', 'rotate on demand', 'audit key usage' → CMK. Encryption in transit → ACM + HTTPS/TLS. S3 public access protection → S3 Block Public Access + bucket policy. Compliance retention → Versioning + MFA Delete + S3 Object Lock (WORM). Backup strategy → AWS Backup for centralised cross-service backup. 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: KMS key deletion must be scheduled with a minimum 7-day and maximum 30-day waiting period. During this time, the deletion can be cancelled. This provides a safety net against accidental deletion. Key rotation creates new key versions but doesn't prevent deletion. Multi-region key replication requires setup before deletion. Exporting key material is only for imported key material keys.

explain: CloudFormation StackSets with service-managed permissions (Organizations integration) can deploy Config enablement across all accounts, including accounts created in the future (via automatic deployment). SCPs don't deploy resources. Conformance packs are for compliance rules, not Config enablement itself. Manual setup doesn't scale.

Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: prevent across org (even root) → SCP on root OU. 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. q: A company stores database connection strings in EC2 user data. A security review flags this as a risk. What is the correct remediation?
options: Store connection strings in S3 and download during instance launch
Encrypt the user data field using KMS
Remove connection strings from user data and retrieve them at runtime from AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store using the instance IAM role
Base64-encode the connection string in user data
explain: EC2 user data is accessible to any code running on the instance and is not encrypted at rest. The correct approach is to retrieve secrets at runtime from Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store using the instance's IAM role — the instance never stores long-term secrets. Base64 is not encryption. User data field cannot be encrypted with KMS. S3 storage without strict access controls moves but doesn't solve the problem.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: no long-term credentials → IAM Role on EC2 (IMDS provides temp credentials). 📋 Task 1.1 — Design secure access to AWS resources: Secure access = WHO can do WHAT on WHICH resource. Decision path: Is it a service/app? → IAM Role (not user). Is it cross-account? → Role + AssumeRole. Is it a human user? → IAM Identity Center (SSO) + federation. Need to restrict across entire AWS Org? → SCP (even overrides admin). Permission boundary = max permissions a role can have (use for delegated admin). NEVER use root account for daily tasks. NEVER store long-term keys on EC2 (use role). 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: EC2 user data is accessible to any code running on the instance and is not encrypted at rest. The correct approach is to retrieve secrets at runtime from Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store using the instance's IAM role — the instance never stores long-term secrets. Base64 is not encryption. User data field cannot be encrypted with KMS. S3 storage without strict access controls moves but doesn't solve the problem.

q: A company is using AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) to manage TLS certificates for their ALB. The certificate is expiring in 30 days. What happens if no action is taken?
options: ACM sends an SNS notification and renews after manual approval
The ALB will stop serving HTTPS traffic immediately when the certificate expires
AWS extends the certificate 30 days automatically as a grace period
ACM automatically renews certificates it issued (not imported) — if domain validation is met, renewal is automatic
explain: ACM automatically renews certificates it provisions (domain-validated or email-validated) before expiration, as long as DNS/email validation requirements are met. Imported certificates require manual renewal. The ALB will serve an expired certificate if not renewed, causing browser warnings. There is no automatic grace period extension.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: access pattern by age → S3 Lifecycle policy. 💡 ELB type selection: Web application (HTTP/HTTPS) + routing rules + WAF → ALB. Extreme performance (millions RPS), static IP, TCP/UDP, source IP preservation → NLB. Third-party security appliances (firewall, IDS) inline → GWLB. Need PrivateLink endpoint → NLB. WebSocket → ALB. Key: ALB = smart routing at L7. NLB = dumb fast routing at L4. NLB has static IP per AZ (useful for whitelisting). ALB has dynamic IPs. ❌ Eliminate distractors: ACM automatically renews certificates it provisions (domain-validated or email-validated) before expiration, as long as DNS/email validation requirements are met. Imported certificates require manual renewal. The ALB will serve an expired certificate if not renewed, causing browser warnings. There is no automatic grace period extension.
