q: A company needs to allow their on-premises security operations center (SOC) to query CloudTrail logs in AWS without accessing the AWS console. What is the recommended approach?
options: Give the SOC team root account access for CloudTrail queries
Stream CloudTrail events to a CloudWatch Logs log group and create a subscription filter to ship logs to the SOC's SIEM via Kinesis Data Firehose
Run scheduled Athena queries and email results to the SOC
Export CloudTrail logs to S3 and provide the SOC with AWS credentials to access S3
explain: Streaming CloudTrail events via CloudWatch Logs subscription filter to Kinesis Data Firehose allows continuous delivery to a SIEM without AWS console access or providing static credentials. The SOC receives logs in their existing tooling. Providing S3 credentials gives persistent access requiring credential management. Root account access is a severe violation. Scheduled emails are delayed and incomplete.
hint: 🔐 Domain 1 (30%): Design Secure Architectures. Key task statements: 1.1 Secure access (IAM/SCP/roles), 1.2 Secure workloads (WAF/GuardDuty/VPC), 1.3 Data security (KMS/encryption/backup). 📋 Task 1.2 — Design secure workloads and applications: Secure workloads = protect at multiple layers. Attack type decision: SQL injection/XSS/OWASP → WAF (L7). Large-scale DDoS → Shield Advanced. Threat detection/anomaly → GuardDuty. Vulnerability scanning → Inspector. S3 sensitive data discovery → Macie. Centralized security findings → Security Hub. Secret storage with rotation → Secrets Manager. Config data (non-secret) → Parameter Store (free) or Secrets Manager (paid, auto-rotate). Network layers: NACL (stateless, subnet boundary) → SG (stateful, instance boundary) → WAF (application boundary). 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Streaming CloudTrail events via CloudWatch Logs subscription filter to Kinesis Data Firehose allows continuous delivery to a SIEM without AWS console access or providing static credentials. The SOC receives logs in their existing tooling. Providing S3 credentials gives persistent access requiring credential management. Root account access is a severe violation. Scheduled emails are delayed and incomplete.

q: A company runs a mission-critical application on a single EC2 instance with an RDS MySQL database. They need to achieve an RTO of 15 minutes and RPO of 5 minutes. Which architecture should they implement?
options: Create daily AMI snapshots of the EC2 instance and RDS snapshots
Use EC2 Reserved Instances and RDS Read Replicas in the same AZ
Enable RDS automated backups with 5-minute retention
Deploy the EC2 application in an Auto Scaling group with a minimum of 2 instances across two AZs, enable RDS Multi-AZ deployment
explain: Multi-AZ RDS provides synchronous replication with automatic failover in ~1-2 minutes, meeting the 5-minute RPO. Auto Scaling group across multiple AZs replaces failed EC2 instances within minutes, meeting the 15-minute RTO. Daily snapshots have up to 24-hour RPO. Read Replicas use asynchronous replication and don't auto-failover. Reserved Instances affect cost, not availability.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: RTO in minutes → Warm Standby or Multi-AZ. Hours → Pilot Light. | RPO in seconds/minutes → synchronous replication (RDS Multi-AZ, Aurora) | multi-AZ = eliminate single-AZ failure. Always for: RDS, ElastiCache, ALB, EFS | read scaling → Read Replicas (async, readable). Multi-AZ standby is NOT readable. 📋 Task 2.2 — Design highly available and fault-tolerant architectures: HA = eliminate single points of failure. Fault tolerance = withstand failure without impact. RTO/RPO drives DR tier: hours → Backup/Restore. Hours (core running) → Pilot Light. Minutes → Warm Standby. Near-zero → Active-Active (most expensive). RDS Multi-AZ ≠ Read Replicas: Multi-AZ = HA (failover), Read Replicas = read scale. Aurora > RDS Multi-AZ for failover speed (<30s vs 1-2 min). Global = Aurora Global, DynamoDB Global Tables, S3 CRR. Always multi-AZ: ALB, RDS, ElastiCache, EFS, NAT Gateway (one per AZ for HA). 💡 DR tier selection: cost vs speed trade-off. Cheapest → slowest: Backup/Restore → Pilot Light → Warm Standby → Active-Active. RTO > 1 hour → Backup/Restore is sufficient. RTO 1-4 hours + core DB must be ready → Pilot Light. RTO < 1 hour + some infra always running → Warm Standby. RTO near-zero + mission critical → Active-Active (multi-region). Aurora Global = best managed cross-region DB DR (<1s RPO). DynamoDB Global Tables = active-active NoSQL across regions. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Multi-AZ RDS provides synchronous replication with automatic failover in ~1-2 minutes, meeting the 5-minute RPO. Auto Scaling group across multiple AZs replaces failed EC2 instances within minutes, meeting the 15-minute RTO. Daily snapshots have up to 24-hour RPO. Read Replicas use asynchronous replication and don't auto-failover. Reserved Instances affect cost, not availability.

q: A company's DynamoDB table receives unpredictable spikes in read traffic. The application is experiencing throttling during peak loads. What is the most cost-effective solution?
options: Enable DynamoDB on-demand capacity mode or use DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) for caching
Switch to DynamoDB provisioned capacity with maximum expected throughput
Migrate to RDS Aurora with read replicas
Add read replicas to DynamoDB in each AZ
explain: DynamoDB on-demand mode automatically scales to handle any traffic level without throttling, charging per request. DAX provides in-memory caching to reduce read traffic to DynamoDB. Provisioned capacity with max throughput wastes cost during low traffic. DynamoDB doesn't have traditional read replicas. Aurora migration is operationally complex for a use case DynamoDB handles natively.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: predictable 24/7 → Reserved Instances or Savings Plans | cost optimization → match pricing model to workload type | read scaling → Read Replicas (async, readable). Multi-AZ standby is NOT readable. 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. ❌ Eliminate distractors: DynamoDB on-demand mode automatically scales to handle any traffic level without throttling, charging per request. DAX provides in-memory caching to reduce read traffic to DynamoDB. Provisioned capacity with max throughput wastes cost during low traffic. DynamoDB doesn't have traditional read replicas. Aurora migration is operationally complex for a use case DynamoDB handles natively.

q: An application uses SQS for decoupling. During a processing outage, messages accumulate. After recovery, the service processes messages but some are older than the visibility timeout and are being processed multiple times. What should be implemented?
options: Implement idempotent message processing in the consumer and configure an appropriate visibility timeout based on processing time
Enable SQS FIFO queues to prevent duplicate processing
Use SQS dead-letter queues to discard old messages
Increase the SQS message retention period
explain: Idempotent processing ensures that processing a message multiple times produces the same result, handling the inherent at-least-once delivery of SQS standard queues. DLQs handle messages that fail repeatedly, not duplicates during normal processing.

q: A company needs a disaster recovery strategy for an application in us-east-1 with an RTO of 4 hours and RPO of 1 hour. They want the lowest cost DR option. Which approach is appropriate?
options: Warm Standby with a scaled-down environment in us-west-2 and cross-region RDS read replica
Active-Active multi-region deployment
Pilot Light with a cross-region RDS replica and AMIs stored in us-west-2
Backup and Restore using S3 cross-region replication
explain: Pilot Light maintains the minimum critical components (like a cross-region RDS replica) and pre-staged AMIs. During a disaster, you scale up from the pilot light infrastructure. With a 4-hour RTO, pilot light provides enough time to provision. Warm Standby is more expensive as it runs a scaled-down environment continuously. Active-Active is the most expensive. Backup and Restore is cheapest but typically has longer RTO than 4 hours.

Cheapest → slowest: Backup/Restore → Pilot Light → Warm Standby → Active-Active. RTO > 1 hour → Backup/Restore is sufficient. RTO 1-4 hours + core DB must be ready → Pilot Light. RTO < 1 hour + some infra always running → Warm Standby. RTO near-zero + mission critical → Active-Active (multi-region). Aurora Global = best managed cross-region DB DR (<1s RPO). DynamoDB Global Tables = active-active NoSQL across regions. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). q: A company wants to ensure their static website on S3 is highly available globally with fast response times even if one AWS region becomes unavailable. What architecture should they deploy?

q: An application processes financial transactions and cannot afford data loss. It writes to an RDS Aurora MySQL cluster. The team wants to ensure writes are durable even if the writer instance fails. What should they configure?
options: Configure Aurora global database for synchronous cross-region replication
Enable Aurora backtrack to rewind to a point before the failure
Aurora automatically replicates data to 6 copies across 3 AZs — write durability is maintained even if the writer fails because storage is separate from compute

explain: Aurora stores data in a distributed shared storage volume with 6 copies across 3 AZs. A write is acknowledged when 4 of 6 copies confirm the write. q: A company's microservices use synchronous HTTP calls between services. During high load, slow downstream services cause a cascade of failures across the entire system. What pattern resolves this?

q: A company needs to process streaming data from IoT devices with no data loss, even during peak loads. The processing Lambda function sometimes fails. What architecture ensures reliability?
options: Kinesis Data Streams → Lambda event source mapping with bisect-on-error and DLQ for failed records
API Gateway → Lambda with no retry
SQS → Lambda with a 30-second visibility timeout
Direct IoT device to Lambda invocation
explain: Kinesis Data Streams with Lambda event source mapping retries failed batches. Enabling BisectOnFunctionError splits failing batches to isolate problematic records. A DLQ captures records that fail after all retries. This ensures no data loss. SQS can also work but Kinesis is designed for ordered streaming data from IoT. Direct Lambda invocation from IoT without a buffer is not resilient to Lambda throttling.

q: A company's Route 53 DNS points to an Application Load Balancer in us-east-1. They want automatic failover to an ALB in us-west-2 if the primary region becomes unavailable. What Route 53 configuration achieves this?
options: Configure Route 53 Failover routing with health checks on both ALBs — primary in us-east-1, secondary in us-west-2
Create A records with equal weights for both ALBs
Use Route 53 latency routing to route to the fastest region

explain: Route 53 Failover routing with associated health checks automatically routes traffic to the secondary record when the primary health check fails. Health checks monitor the ALB endpoint. Weighted routing distributes traffic to both but doesn't stop sending to a failed region. Latency routing optimizes for speed, not failover. Resolver rules are for DNS resolution within VPCs.

q: A company runs an ECS Fargate application with a single task definition. During deployments, there is a brief downtime as old tasks stop before new ones start. How can they achieve zero-downtime deployments?

Use blue/green deployments via AWS CodeDeploy with an ALB target group swap
Deploy new tasks manually before stopping old ones using a custom script
explain: Both approaches achieve zero downtime. ECS rolling update with min 100%/max 200% keeps all current tasks running while adding new tasks before removing old ones. Key: ALB = smart routing at L7. NLB = dumb fast routing at L4. NLB has static IP per AZ (useful for whitelisting). ALB has dynamic IPs. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Both approaches achieve zero downtime. ECS rolling update with min 100%/max 200% keeps all current tasks running while adding new tasks before removing old ones. Blue/green with CodeDeploy shifts traffic between target groups after validation, with instant rollback capability. Choice depends on whether instant rollback or gradual traffic shifting is preferred.

q: An application stores session state on individual EC2 instances behind an ALB. When an instance is replaced by Auto Scaling, users lose their sessions. What is the recommended fix?
options: Enable ALB sticky sessions (session affinity)
Increase the Auto Scaling minimum instance count to prevent replacements
Move session state to Amazon ElastiCache for Redis, which is accessible by all instances
Use EC2 instance store for session data with more frequent backups
explain: Externalizing session state to ElastiCache for Redis makes sessions available to all instances. If an instance fails or is replaced, users can continue their session on any other instance. Sticky sessions route users to the same instance but fail if that instance is terminated. Instance store data is lost on termination. Preventing scale-in reduces cost efficiency.

q: A company has a Lambda function that calls an external API. The external API occasionally times out. The team wants the function to retry with exponential backoff without blocking other invocations. What is the best approach?
options: Use synchronous Lambda invocations with retry logic in the calling application
Use Step Functions to orchestrate the Lambda with Wait states for exponential backoff between retries
Wrap the API call in a try/catch and sleep between retries inside the Lambda function
Configure Lambda destination for failures and use SQS for retry with exponential backoff via Lambda event source mapping redrive policy
explain: AWS Step Functions can implement retry logic with exponential backoff, max attempts, and jitter using the Retry field in state machine tasks — without blocking Lambda invocations. Sleeping inside Lambda wastes compute time and cost. SQS with redrive works for async patterns but adds queue latency. Synchronous retries in the calling app couple the caller to the retry logic.

q: A company runs a three-tier web application. The database tier uses RDS MySQL. The middle tier uses EC2 in an Auto Scaling group. They want the middle tier to be resilient to AZ failures. What changes are needed?

q: A financial application requires that messages be processed exactly once and in order. What AWS messaging solution should be used?
options: Amazon SNS with SQS fan-out
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with enhanced fan-out
Amazon SQS Standard queue
Amazon SQS FIFO queue with message deduplication enabled
explain: SQS FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once processing (using deduplication IDs) and first-in-first-out ordering within a message group. Standard SQS offers at-least-once delivery and best-effort ordering. SNS fan-out doesn't guarantee ordering. Kinesis provides ordered records per shard but delivers at-least-once, requiring application-level deduplication.

q: A company's application in us-east-1 uses DynamoDB. They need a globally available database with reads and writes accepted in both us-east-1 and eu-west-1 with conflict resolution. What should they configure?
options: Enable DynamoDB Cross-Region Replication with active-passive configuration
Create DynamoDB tables in both regions and handle replication with Lambda
Use Aurora Global Database with write forwarding enabled
Enable DynamoDB Global Tables with tables in both regions — it provides active-active replication with last-writer-wins conflict resolution
explain: DynamoDB Global Tables provide fully managed active-active multi-region replication. Both regions accept reads and writes, with last-writer-wins conflict resolution based on timestamps. Cross-region DynamoDB replication is not a native feature without Global Tables. Lambda-based replication is operational overhead. Aurora is a relational database, not DynamoDB.

Both regions accept reads and writes, with last-writer-wins conflict resolution based on timestamps. Cross-region DynamoDB replication is not a native feature without Global Tables. Lambda-based replication is operational overhead. Aurora is a relational database, not DynamoDB.

q: A company's architecture has a downstream service that occasionally returns 500 errors and slow responses. The upstream service needs to handle this gracefully without failing. What architecture pattern should be applied?
options: Retry the request indefinitely until it succeeds
Increase Lambda concurrency limits on the downstream service
Implement timeouts, retries with exponential backoff and jitter, and a circuit breaker that opens after consecutive failures
Queue all requests in SQS and let the downstream service process when available
explain: Resilient service-to-service calls should use timeouts (don't wait indefinitely), exponential backoff with jitter (avoid thundering herd on retry), and circuit breakers (stop calling a failing service, allow time to recover). Indefinite retries exacerbate the problem. SQS decoupling is a valid pattern but changes the architecture to async. Increasing concurrency addresses capacity, not failure handling.

Indefinite retries exacerbate the problem. SQS decoupling is a valid pattern but changes the architecture to async. Increasing concurrency addresses capacity, not failure handling.

q: A company's ECS cluster runs stateless microservices. They want to maximize availability during an underlying EC2 instance failure. Which placement strategy achieves the best distribution of tasks?
options: Use a single large instance with multiple tasks
Use the 'binpack' placement strategy to consolidate tasks on fewer instances
Use the 'random' placement strategy
Use the 'spread' placement strategy across both AZ and instanceId to distribute tasks across instances and AZs
explain: ECS spread placement strategy distributes tasks evenly across specified attributes. Spreading first by AZ then by instanceId ensures maximum distribution — tasks are spread across AZs first, then across individual instances within AZs. Binpack consolidates tasks, maximizing cost efficiency but reducing fault tolerance. Random is unpredictable. Single instance is a single point of failure.

Always for: RDS, ElastiCache, ALB, EFS. ❌ Eliminate distractors: ECS spread placement strategy distributes tasks evenly across specified attributes. Spreading first by AZ then by instanceId ensures maximum distribution — tasks are spread across AZs first, then across individual instances within AZs. q: A company runs a batch processing workload that takes 6 hours to complete. The workload uses EC2 Spot Instances to save costs. How should they design the job to handle Spot interruptions?
options: Implement checkpointing to save progress to S3 or DynamoDB, and use Spot interruption notices (2-minute warning) to save state before termination
Use Spot Instances with On-Demand as backup — no code changes needed
Run the job on a single large On-Demand instance to avoid interruptions
Use SQS to queue each 6-hour batch job and retry if interrupted
explain: Spot Instances provide a 2-minute interruption notice via EC2 instance metadata. The application should save its progress (checkpoint) periodically and on receiving the interruption notice. After a replacement Spot Instance starts, it resumes from the last checkpoint. This avoids restarting from scratch. Spot with On-Demand backup (Spot Fleet) helps with availability but without checkpointing, interrupted jobs restart from scratch.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: fault-tolerant workload → Spot Instances (up to 90% off). 📋 Task 2.2 — Design highly available and fault-tolerant architectures: HA = eliminate single points of failure. Fault tolerance = withstand failure without impact. RTO/RPO drives DR tier: hours → Backup/Restore. Hours (core running) → Pilot Light. Minutes → Warm Standby. Near-zero → Active-Active (most expensive). RDS Multi-AZ ≠ Read Replicas: Multi-AZ = HA (failover), Read Replicas = read scale. Aurora > RDS Multi-AZ for failover speed (<30s vs 1-2 min). Global = Aurora Global, DynamoDB Global Tables, S3 CRR. Always multi-AZ: ALB, RDS, ElastiCache, EFS, NAT Gateway (one per AZ for HA). 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Spot Instances provide a 2-minute interruption notice via EC2 instance metadata. The application should save its progress (checkpoint) periodically and on receiving the interruption notice. After a replacement Spot Instance starts, it resumes from the last checkpoint. This avoids restarting from scratch. Spot with On-Demand backup (Spot Fleet) helps with availability but without checkpointing, interrupted jobs restart from scratch.

Amazon EC2
Amazon S3
Amazon SQS

S3 Access Points (named access control per app/department/team)
S3 Event Notifications → SQS, SNS, Lambda, EventBridge
S3 MFA Delete (requires MFA to delete versions or suspend versioning)
S3 Object Lock (WORM): Compliance mode (no one can delete) vs Governance mode (admin can override)
S3 Presigned URL (time-limited access to private object, generated by owner)
S3 Replication: SRR (same-region) vs CRR (cross-region), requires versioning ON

S3 versioning (protect from accidental delete, enable before MFA Delete)

q: A company's serverless application uses Lambda and DynamoDB. Lambda functions sometimes fail due to downstream API timeouts. The team wants to retry failed Lambda invocations automatically. What should they configure?
options: Increase the Lambda function timeout to 15 minutes to wait for slow APIs
Use Lambda provisioned concurrency to prevent timeouts
For asynchronous Lambda invocations, configure the retry attempts (up to 2) and configure a Dead Letter Queue (SQS or SNS) for failed events after all retries
Set Lambda retry attempts to 0 and handle retries in the calling code
explain: Async Lambda invocations support up to 2 retry attempts with delays between retries. A DLQ captures events that fail after all retry attempts for analysis or manual reprocessing. Provisioned concurrency prevents cold starts but doesn't help with downstream timeouts. Increasing timeout wastes compute cost waiting for a potentially unavailable API. Suppressing retries loses data.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). q: A company uses Amazon SQS to decouple their order processing system. During a major sale, the queue depth grows to 1 million messages and processing falls behind. What is the most cost-effective way to scale processing?
options: Manually add more EC2 instances to the processing tier
Use Auto Scaling based on the ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible SQS metric to scale EC2 or Lambda consumers automatically
Increase the SQS message retention period to 14 days
Use SQS FIFO to process messages faster
explain: Auto Scaling using the ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric allows the consumer fleet to scale automatically with queue depth. Lambda with SQS event source mapping scales automatically up to concurrency limits. Manual scaling reacts too slowly. Message retention affects how long messages stay in queue, not processing speed. FIFO queues have lower throughput than Standard queues.

q: A company runs a critical API on EC2 instances. An ALB health check fails when the application is under heavy load, causing healthy instances to be terminated by Auto Scaling. How should they address this?
options: Increase the EC2 instance size to handle the heavy load
Tune the ALB health check thresholds (increase healthy/unhealthy threshold counts and interval), and configure a separate lightweight health check endpoint that doesn't perform heavy operations
Disable ALB health checks during peak periods
Switch from ALB to NLB — NLB health checks are less sensitive
explain: Creating a lightweight health check endpoint (e.g., returns HTTP 200 with no database calls) ensures health checks succeed even under load. Tuning thresholds reduces false positives from momentary slowness. Disabling health checks removes protection against actual failures. Larger instances may still fail under extreme load. NLB health checks are at Layer 4 and don't solve application-level health check accuracy.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 📋 Task 2.2 — Design highly available and fault-tolerant architectures: HA = eliminate single points of failure. Fault tolerance = withstand failure without impact. RTO/RPO drives DR tier: hours → Backup/Restore. Hours (core running) → Pilot Light. Minutes → Warm Standby. Near-zero → Active-Active (most expensive). RDS Multi-AZ ≠ Read Replicas: Multi-AZ = HA (failover), Read Replicas = read scale. Aurora > RDS Multi-AZ for failover speed (<30s vs 1-2 min). Global = Aurora Global, DynamoDB Global Tables, S3 CRR. Always multi-AZ: ALB, RDS, ElastiCache, EFS, NAT Gateway (one per AZ for HA). 💡 ELB type selection: Web application (HTTP/HTTPS) + routing rules + WAF → ALB. Extreme performance (millions RPS), static IP, TCP/UDP, source IP preservation → NLB. Third-party security appliances (firewall, IDS) inline → GWLB. Need PrivateLink endpoint → NLB. WebSocket → ALB. Key: ALB = smart routing at L7. NLB = dumb fast routing at L4. NLB has static IP per AZ (useful for whitelisting). ALB has dynamic IPs. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Creating a lightweight health check endpoint (e.g., returns HTTP 200 with no database calls) ensures health checks succeed even under load. Tuning thresholds reduces false positives from momentary slowness. Disabling health checks removes protection against actual failures. Larger instances may still fail under extreme load. NLB health checks are at Layer 4 and don't solve application-level health check accuracy.

q: A company's application writes to S3 and requires that writes be confirmed before proceeding. A downstream Lambda function processes S3 objects as they arrive. Objects are sometimes processed before the write is fully committed, resulting in empty reads. What is the root cause and fix?
options: S3 has eventual consistency for all write operations — add a delay before Lambda reads
Switch to EFS for strongly consistent file writes
This was previously a limitation but S3 now provides strong consistency for all operations — ensure the Lambda is using the correct S3 GetObject API and is not reading stale cached data
Use S3 Versioning to ensure Lambda always reads the latest version
explain: As of December 2020, Amazon S3 provides strong read-after-write consistency for all operations including PUTs of new objects and overwrites. If Lambda reads an empty object after an S3 event trigger, the issue is likely a race condition in the application logic or a caching/configuration issue, not S3 consistency. Delays are unnecessary. Versioning helps with overwrites but not the fundamental issue described.

q: A company needs to implement a blue/green deployment for their RDS database schema changes. What is the recommended AWS approach?
options: Use RDS Read Replicas as the green environment and promote after schema changes
Use RDS Blue/Green Deployments feature to create a green environment, apply changes, and switch over with minimal downtime
Create a new RDS instance (green), apply schema changes, switch the application's connection string, and decommission the old instance (blue)
Apply schema changes directly to production after creating a manual snapshot
explain: RDS Blue/Green Deployments is a native AWS feature that creates a staging environment (green) synchronized with production (blue) using logical replication. You can apply and test schema changes on green, then switch over with minimal downtime. Switchover is managed by RDS. Manual green deployments are complex. Read Replica promotion doesn't support rollback to blue. Direct production changes are risky.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Multi-AZ standby is NOT readable. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: RDS Blue/Green Deployments is a native AWS feature that creates a staging environment (green) synchronized with production (blue) using logical replication. q: A company's application uses AWS Lambda and experienced a sharp increase in errors during a deployment of new code. How can they minimize impact of future bad deployments?
options: Always deploy Lambda functions manually one at a time
Use Lambda aliases with weighted routing (canary deployments) — route a small percentage of traffic to the new version and monitor for errors before full deployment

Use Lambda layers to deploy code separately from configuration

q: A company's multi-tier application uses EC2, RDS, and ElastiCache. All resources are in a single AZ. A storm causes the AZ to lose power. They want to recover within 30 minutes. What is the minimum architecture change?
options: Enable RDS automated backups and EC2 AMI snapshots
Enable CloudFront caching to serve content during the outage
Enable RDS Multi-AZ, deploy EC2 in an Auto Scaling group across 2+ AZs, and ensure ElastiCache uses Multi-AZ replication groups
Replicate everything to a second region with Route 53 failover
explain: Deploying across multiple AZs within the same region provides resilience against single-AZ failures. RDS Multi-AZ automatic failover, EC2 Auto Scaling across AZs, and ElastiCache Multi-AZ replication groups all recover within minutes of an AZ failure. Cross-region replication meets the 30-minute RTO but adds significant cost and complexity. Snapshots enable recovery but typically exceed 30-minute RTO. CloudFront caches content but not application state.

q: A company's application sends emails via Amazon SES. Occasionally SES returns throttling errors and emails are dropped. How should they make email sending resilient?

Increase the SES sending quota and no further changes are needed
Use SNS instead of SES for email delivery

explain: An SQS-based buffer decouples email generation from SES delivery. The Lambda consumer retries with exponential backoff when SES returns throttling errors, ensuring no emails are dropped. q: A company uses an Application Load Balancer with EC2 instances. Occasionally, a single instance becomes unhealthy due to memory leak but takes 5 minutes to be detected and removed by the ALB. Users receive errors during this window. How can the detection time be reduced?
options: Increase the number of EC2 instances to dilute traffic to the unhealthy instance
Use NLB instead of ALB for faster health checks
Tune ALB health check settings: reduce the check interval to 10 seconds and unhealthy threshold to 2, ensuring the instance is removed within ~20 seconds of becoming unhealthy
Enable Auto Scaling with a 5-minute cooldown period
explain: The default ALB health check interval is 30 seconds with an unhealthy threshold of 2-3. Reducing the interval to 10 seconds and threshold to 2 means an instance is removed within ~20 seconds (2 x 10s) of failing health checks. NLB health checks also support tuning but ALB provides better application-layer health checking. More instances dilute errors but don't accelerate detection.

Extreme performance (millions RPS), static IP, TCP/UDP, source IP preservation → NLB. Third-party security appliances (firewall, IDS) inline → GWLB. Need PrivateLink endpoint → NLB. WebSocket → ALB. Key: ALB = smart routing at L7. NLB = dumb fast routing at L4. NLB has static IP per AZ (useful for whitelisting). ALB has dynamic IPs. ❌ Eliminate distractors: The default ALB health check interval is 30 seconds with an unhealthy threshold of 2-3. Reducing the interval to 10 seconds and threshold to 2 means an instance is removed within ~20 seconds (2 x 10s) of failing health checks. NLB health checks also support tuning but ALB provides better application-layer health checking. More instances dilute errors but don't accelerate detection.

q: A company wants to ensure their S3-hosted data is protected against accidental deletion by any user, including administrators. Which feature prevents this?
options: Enable S3 Object Lock with COMPLIANCE mode and a retention period — prevents deletion even by the root account
Enable S3 versioning — it moves deleted objects to a delete marker
Enable MFA Delete on the S3 bucket
Apply a bucket policy denying DeleteObject for IAM users
explain: S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode prevents object deletion or modification until the retention period expires — no user, including root, can override this. MFA Delete requires MFA for deletions but can be disabled by the root account. Bucket policies denying DeleteObject can be overridden by the account administrator. Versioning creates delete markers but objects can still be permanently deleted by deleting all versions.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode prevents object deletion or modification until the retention period expires — no user, including root, can override this. MFA Delete requires MFA for deletions but can be disabled by the root account. Bucket policies denying DeleteObject can be overridden by the account administrator. Versioning creates delete markers but objects can still be permanently deleted by deleting all versions.
keywords: Amazon S3
AWS IAM
S3 Access Points (named access control per app/department/team)
S3 Event Notifications → SQS, SNS, Lambda, EventBridge
S3 MFA Delete (requires MFA to delete versions or suspend versioning)
S3 Object Lock (WORM): Compliance mode (no one can delete) vs Governance mode (admin can override)
S3 Presigned URL (time-limited access to private object, generated by owner)
S3 Replication: SRR (same-region) vs CRR (cross-region), requires versioning ON

q: A company's Lambda functions are experiencing cold starts affecting user experience. The functions are invoked unpredictably with occasional bursts. What is the most effective mitigation?

Increase Lambda function memory allocation
Enable Lambda Provisioned Concurrency for the most latency-sensitive functions
Package the Lambda function as a container image

q: A company uses Amazon Aurora. They need to ensure their reporting queries don't impact the transactional workload performance. What is the recommended approach?
options: Increase the Aurora writer instance size
Use Aurora Serverless for the reporting workload
Create Aurora Read Replicas and direct reporting queries to the reader endpoint
Schedule reports to run during off-peak hours on the primary instance
explain: Aurora Read Replicas share the same distributed storage as the writer but handle read traffic independently. The Aurora reader endpoint automatically load balances reads across all replicas. This offloads reporting query load from the writer. Scheduling doesn't prevent contention if the workload overlaps. Increasing writer size doesn't prevent resource contention. Aurora Serverless v2 is appropriate for unpredictable workloads, not isolated reporting.

Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: repeated reads → add caching layer (ElastiCache or DAX) | predictable 24/7 → Reserved Instances or Savings Plans | read scaling → Read Replicas (async, readable). Multi-AZ standby is NOT readable | decoupling → SQS (queue), SNS (pub/sub), EventBridge (events). 📋 Task 2.1 — Design scalable and loosely coupled architectures: Loose coupling = remove direct dependencies. Replace synchronous call with queue/event. Message queue (SQS) vs pub/sub (SNS): SQS = one consumer pulls. SNS = pushes to many. Fan-out = SNS → multiple SQS for parallel processing of same event. Real-time streaming with replay → Kinesis Streams. Near-real-time delivery to S3/Redshift → Kinesis Firehose (no replay). Legacy message broker (AMQP, MQTT, STOMP) → Amazon MQ (not SQS/SNS). Serverless API → API Gateway + Lambda. 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 Serverless = no server management, pay per use, auto-scale. Lambda limits: 15 min max, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, 1000 concurrent (soft). Cold start: Lambda function first invocation takes longer. Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm. q: A company has a microservices architecture where Service A calls Service B. Service B has a rolling deployment happening and some requests fail during the rollout. How can the client (Service A) handle these failures without user impact?
options: Delay Service A deployments until Service B deployment is complete
Deploy Service B only during maintenance windows with 0 traffic

q: A company stores critical application data in an S3 bucket. They want to recover from accidental overwrites of specific objects. Which feature enables recovery of overwritten S3 objects?
options: S3 Lifecycle policies
S3 Object Lock
S3 Cross-Region Replication
S3 Versioning — each overwrite creates a new version, and previous versions can be restored
explain: S3 Versioning maintains all versions of every object. An accidental overwrite creates a new version while preserving the previous version, which can be restored by retrieving the previous version ID. Cross-Region Replication replicates to another region but also replicates overwrites. Lifecycle policies manage object expiration. Object Lock prevents deletion/modification but doesn't help with restoring from overwrites.

SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: S3 Versioning maintains all versions of every object. An accidental overwrite creates a new version while preserving the previous version, which can be restored by retrieving the previous version ID. Cross-Region Replication replicates to another region but also replicates overwrites. Lifecycle policies manage object expiration. Object Lock prevents deletion/modification but doesn't help with restoring from overwrites.

Use an Elastic IP for the RDS instance to maintain a consistent endpoint

q: A company uses AWS Elastic Beanstalk for a web application. They want to update the application with zero downtime and the ability to instantly roll back. Which deployment policy should they use?
options: Immutable deployment or Blue/Green via Elastic Beanstalk environment swap
All at once deployment
Rolling deployment with batch size 25%
Rolling with additional batch
explain: Immutable deployment creates new instances with the new version. If the deployment fails, the new instances are discarded (instant rollback, no downtime). Blue/Green swaps traffic between two full environments with instant URL swap and rollback. All at once causes brief downtime. Rolling and Rolling with additional batch cause partial downtime during replacement.

If the deployment fails, the new instances are discarded (instant rollback, no downtime). Blue/Green swaps traffic between two full environments with instant URL swap and rollback. All at once causes brief downtime. Rolling and Rolling with additional batch cause partial downtime during replacement.

q: A company runs a 24/7 e-commerce platform. They must maintain availability during a region-wide AWS outage. Which architecture pattern achieves this?
options: Active-Active multi-region deployment with Route 53 health checks and data replicated between regions
Daily cross-region AMI copy with a recovery runbook
Multi-AZ deployment in the primary region
AWS Outposts for on-premises redundancy
explain: Active-Active multi-region deployment means both regions actively serve traffic. Route 53 health checks detect a region failure and stop routing to it. Data is replicated between regions (e.g., Aurora Global Database, DynamoDB Global Tables). This provides the highest availability against region-wide outages. Multi-AZ only protects against AZ failures. AMI copies enable recovery but require manual steps with longer RTO. Outposts bring AWS to on-premises but don't protect against a region-wide failure.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: predictable 24/7 → Reserved Instances or Savings Plans | multi-AZ = eliminate single-AZ failure. Always for: RDS, ElastiCache, ALB, EFS. 📋 Task 2.1 — Design scalable and loosely coupled architectures: Loose coupling = remove direct dependencies. Replace synchronous call with queue/event. Message queue (SQS) vs pub/sub (SNS): SQS = one consumer pulls. SNS = pushes to many. Fan-out = SNS → multiple SQS for parallel processing of same event. Real-time streaming with replay → Kinesis Streams. Near-real-time delivery to S3/Redshift → Kinesis Firehose (no replay). Legacy message broker (AMQP, MQTT, STOMP) → Amazon MQ (not SQS/SNS). Complex multi-step workflows with retry/parallel/wait → Step Functions. Event-driven automation on AWS service events → EventBridge rules. Serverless API → API Gateway + Lambda. 💡 DR tier selection: cost vs speed trade-off. Cheapest → slowest: Backup/Restore → Pilot Light → Warm Standby → Active-Active. RTO > 1 hour → Backup/Restore is sufficient. RTO 1-4 hours + core DB must be ready → Pilot Light. RTO < 1 hour + some infra always running → Warm Standby. RTO near-zero + mission critical → Active-Active (multi-region). Aurora Global = best managed cross-region DB DR (<1s RPO). DynamoDB Global Tables = active-active NoSQL across regions. 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Active-Active multi-region deployment means both regions actively serve traffic. Route 53 health checks detect a region failure and stop routing to it. Data is replicated between regions (e.g., Aurora Global Database, DynamoDB Global Tables). This provides the highest availability against region-wide outages. Multi-AZ only protects against AZ failures. AMI copies enable recovery but require manual steps with longer RTO. Outposts bring AWS to on-premises but don't protect against a region-wide failure.

q: A company's application uses Amazon SNS to fan out to 5 SQS queues. One downstream processing service is slow and its SQS queue is growing. How does this affect the other queues?
options: SNS applies backpressure and all subscribed queues receive messages at the slowest consumer's rate
SNS will drop messages to the slow queue and redistribute to other queues
Each SQS queue is independent — a slow consumer on one queue does not affect delivery or processing of other queues
SNS slows down delivery to all queues when one queue is slow
explain: SNS fan-out delivers messages independently to each subscribed SQS queue. Each SQS queue has its own message retention, visibility timeout, and consumer processing rate. A slow consumer causes message accumulation in its queue but has no effect on other queues' delivery or processing. SNS doesn't throttle based on consumer speed.

q: A company uses AWS Step Functions to orchestrate a long-running workflow. A task in the middle of the workflow sometimes fails due to a transient error in a downstream service. How should they handle this?
options: Configure Retry policies on the failing task state with exponential backoff and MaxAttempts, and a Catch block to handle persistent failures gracefully
Set the entire workflow's timeout to 0 to fail fast
Restart the entire workflow from the beginning on any failure
Use a Lambda function inside the task to implement retry logic
explain: Step Functions state machines support per-state Retry configuration with ErrorEquals, IntervalSeconds, MaxAttempts, and BackoffRate fields for exponential backoff. A Catch block routes the workflow to a failure handling state if all retries fail. Restarting the entire workflow wastes completed work. Task-level retries in Step Functions are cleaner than in-Lambda retries for orchestration concerns.

q: A company's EC2-based application in a VPC needs to call an external weather API over the internet. The instances are in private subnets. What provides internet access for the private instances with high availability?
options: Deploy a single NAT Gateway in one public subnet and route all private subnets to it
Deploy NAT Gateways in multiple public subnets (one per AZ) and configure private subnet route tables to use the NAT Gateway in the same AZ
Assign public IPs to all EC2 instances in the private subnets
Attach an internet gateway directly to the private subnet
explain: NAT Gateways in each AZ provide HA for outbound internet access. If one AZ's NAT Gateway fails, instances in other AZs use their own AZ's NAT Gateway. A single NAT Gateway is a single point of failure for all AZs. Internet gateway attached to private subnets makes them public subnets. Assigning public IPs exposes instances directly to the internet.

q: A company uses Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. A shard is receiving more data than it can handle (exceeding 1 MB/s or 1000 records/s). What should the architect do?
options: Increase the Kinesis record retention period
Enable Kinesis Enhanced Fan-Out for the affected consumers
Reshard the stream by splitting the hot shard into two shards to double the capacity for that partition
Compress records before writing to reduce the data size below the shard limit
explain: When a shard is hot (exceeding its limits), splitting it doubles the capacity for that partition. The hot shard is split based on the hash key range. Enhanced Fan-Out increases consumer read throughput (2 MB/s per consumer per shard) but doesn't increase write capacity. Compression helps if the bottleneck is data size, but record count limits (1000/s) would still apply. Retention period affects how long data is available, not throughput.

RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. ❌ Eliminate distractors: When a shard is hot (exceeding its limits), splitting it doubles the capacity for that partition. The hot shard is split based on the hash key range. Enhanced Fan-Out increases consumer read throughput (2 MB/s per consumer per shard) but doesn't increase write capacity. q: A company wants to implement automated failover for their on-premises primary data center to AWS. The connection to AWS is through Direct Connect. If Direct Connect fails, how can they maintain connectivity?
options: Configure a Site-to-Site VPN as a backup path over the internet, with BGP route preferences favoring Direct Connect when available
Configure a second Direct Connect connection as the only backup
Use AWS CloudFront as a fallback for Direct Connect failures
Use VPC peering to route around the Direct Connect failure
explain: AWS recommends a Site-to-Site VPN as backup for Direct Connect failures. BGP can be used to set lower preference (higher metric) on the VPN path, ensuring Direct Connect is preferred when available and automatic failover to VPN when Direct Connect fails. A second Direct Connect increases availability but shares risk factors (same ISP or carrier path). CloudFront and VPC peering don't address on-premises connectivity.

q: A company's application uses AWS SQS. Messages must not be lost if processing fails. After 3 failed processing attempts, messages should be preserved for manual review. What configuration achieves this?

Use SNS with SQS subscription and set SNS retry policy to 3 attempts

q: A company needs to migrate an on-premises Oracle database to AWS with zero data loss and minimal downtime. Which AWS service facilitates this migration?
options: AWS DataSync for database file transfer
AWS Snowball for offline database export
RDS Oracle Multi-AZ as target with manual export/import
AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with Oracle as source and Aurora PostgreSQL as target, using continuous replication for cut-over
explain: AWS DMS supports continuous replication (CDC) from Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL. The initial full load copies existing data, then ongoing CDC replicates changes. Cut-over happens when both databases are in sync, minimizing downtime and ensuring zero data loss during migration. Snowball is for offline bulk migration with data loss during transit. Manual export/import requires extended downtime. DataSync is for file/object storage, not databases.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: multi-AZ = eliminate single-AZ failure. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS DMS supports continuous replication (CDC) from Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL. The initial full load copies existing data, then ongoing CDC replicates changes. Cut-over happens when both databases are in sync, minimizing downtime and ensuring zero data loss during migration. Snowball is for offline bulk migration with data loss during transit. Manual export/import requires extended downtime. DataSync is for file/object storage, not databases.

q: A company has a distributed application across multiple AWS regions. They use Route 53 for DNS. They want to ensure users are always routed to the closest healthy region. What Route 53 routing policy should be configured?
options: Simple routing with TTL set to 1 second
Geolocation routing based on user country
Weighted routing with equal weights across regions
Latency-based routing with health checks on each regional endpoint
explain: Latency-based routing with health checks routes users to the lowest-latency healthy region. If the closest region fails health checks, Route 53 automatically routes to the next lowest-latency healthy region. Weighted routing distributes by percentage, not proximity. Geolocation routing is based on geography (country/continent), not latency. Simple routing doesn't support health checks or multi-region failover.

Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Scheduled scaling proactively adds capacity before the known spike at 9 AM (e.g., scale out at 8:45 AM). Dynamic scaling (target tracking or step scaling) handles unexpected spikes throughout the day. Running excess instances 24/7 wastes cost. Cooldown periods slow down scaling, making the problem worse. Spot Instances save cost but don't address the 10-minute provisioning lag.

q: A company's web application experiences a large number of unique session reads from ElastiCache. Cache hit rate is below 50%. What architectural change would improve this?
options: Review the cache key design — ensure objects are being cached effectively, consider increasing TTL for frequently accessed, infrequently changed data, and evaluate whether lazy loading or write-through caching is appropriate
Switch from ElastiCache Redis to ElastiCache Memcached for better hit rates
Replicate ElastiCache to multiple AZs
Increase ElastiCache node size
explain: Cache hit rate below 50% usually indicates poor cache key design, TTL too short, or data being evicted too quickly. Reviewing caching strategy (lazy loading vs. write-through) and TTL values for read patterns typically resolves this. Larger nodes help with eviction due to memory pressure but don't address key design issues. Multi-AZ replication is for HA, not hit rate. Redis vs. Memcached doesn't inherently improve hit rates.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: multi-AZ = eliminate single-AZ failure. Always for: RDS, ElastiCache, ALB, EFS | read scaling → Read Replicas (async, readable). Multi-AZ standby is NOT readable | static IP for load balancer → NLB (has EIP). ALB has dynamic IPs. 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Cache hit rate below 50% usually indicates poor cache key design, TTL too short, or data being evicted too quickly. Reviewing caching strategy (lazy loading vs. write-through) and TTL values for read patterns typically resolves this. Larger nodes help with eviction due to memory pressure but don't address key design issues. Multi-AZ replication is for HA, not hit rate. Redis vs. Memcached doesn't inherently improve hit rates.

q: A company needs their production RDS database to be protected from accidental deletion by CloudFormation stack updates or deletions. What is the recommended protection?
options: Enable RDS automated backups with a 35-day retention period
Use a CloudFormation StackSet to prevent stack deletion
Set the CloudFormation termination protection on the stack
Enable the DeletionPolicy: Retain and UpdateReplacePolicy: Retain in the CloudFormation resource, and enable RDS deletion protection
explain: CloudFormation DeletionPolicy: Retain ensures the RDS instance is not deleted when the stack or resource is deleted. UpdateReplacePolicy: Retain prevents replacement during updates. RDS deletion protection also prevents deletion via the API or console. Stack termination protection prevents the entire stack from being deleted but is overridable by administrators. Automated backups enable recovery after deletion but don't prevent it.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Fault tolerance = withstand failure without impact. RTO/RPO drives DR tier: hours → Backup/Restore. Hours (core running) → Pilot Light. Minutes → Warm Standby. Near-zero → Active-Active (most expensive). RDS Multi-AZ ≠ Read Replicas: Multi-AZ = HA (failover), Read Replicas = read scale. Aurora > RDS Multi-AZ for failover speed (<30s vs 1-2 min). Global = Aurora Global, DynamoDB Global Tables, S3 CRR. Always multi-AZ: ALB, RDS, ElastiCache, EFS, NAT Gateway (one per AZ for HA). ❌ Eliminate distractors: CloudFormation DeletionPolicy: Retain ensures the RDS instance is not deleted when the stack or resource is deleted. UpdateReplacePolicy: Retain prevents replacement during updates. RDS deletion protection also prevents deletion via the API or console. Stack termination protection prevents the entire stack from being deleted but is overridable by administrators. Automated backups enable recovery after deletion but don't prevent it.

options: Reserved EC2 capacity ensures consistent performance during spikes
CloudFront caching handles all Lambda invocations during spikes

Lambda's automatic scaling from 0 to thousands of concurrent executions, API Gateway's ability to handle millions of requests per second, and DynamoDB on-demand capacity absorb traffic spikes without pre-provisioning
SQS buffers API Gateway requests and Lambda processes at a fixed rate

q: A company's application uses an ALB with multiple target groups. They want canary testing where 5% of traffic goes to a new version. Which ALB feature enables this?
options: ALB sticky sessions
ALB path-based routing rules
ALB weighted target groups — assign 95% weight to the stable target group and 5% to the canary target group
ALB host-based routing
explain: ALB weighted target groups allow splitting traffic by percentage between target groups on the same listener rule. Set weight 95 for stable and 5 for canary. Sticky sessions pin users to specific targets, not traffic splitting. Path-based and host-based routing are for routing different URL paths or hostnames, not traffic splitting.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 💡 ELB type selection: Web application (HTTP/HTTPS) + routing rules + WAF → ALB. Extreme performance (millions RPS), static IP, TCP/UDP, source IP preservation → NLB. Third-party security appliances (firewall, IDS) inline → GWLB. Need PrivateLink endpoint → NLB. WebSocket → ALB. Key: ALB = smart routing at L7. NLB = dumb fast routing at L4. NLB has static IP per AZ (useful for whitelisting). ALB has dynamic IPs. ❌ Eliminate distractors: ALB weighted target groups allow splitting traffic by percentage between target groups on the same listener rule. Set weight 95 for stable and 5 for canary. Sticky sessions pin users to specific targets, not traffic splitting. Path-based and host-based routing are for routing different URL paths or hostnames, not traffic splitting.

Auto Scaling Group
Classic LB (deprecated): avoid in new designs
Connection draining / deregistration delay: allows in-flight requests to complete before target deregistration
Cross-zone load balancing: distributes evenly across all AZs (default ON for ALB, OFF for NLB)
Elastic Load Balancing
Gateway LB (GWLB): Layer 3, inline security appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS), GENEVE protocol
RDS Multi-AZ (sync, auto-failover)
Sticky sessions (session affinity): route same client to same target (ALB + NLB)

q: A company's application writes large files to Amazon EFS. When the EFS mount target in one AZ becomes unavailable, applications in that AZ lose access to files. How can they improve EFS resilience?
options: Use EBS instead of EFS for shared file storage
Set up S3 as a cache in front of EFS
Ensure the application mounts EFS using the DNS name which automatically routes to an available mount target — deploy mount targets in all AZs used by the application
Enable EFS replication to a second EFS file system in another region
explain: EFS mount targets should be created in each AZ where application instances run. When mounting using the DNS name (fs-xxxx.efs.region.amazonaws.com), EFS automatically resolves to a mount target in the same AZ. If one mount target fails, only instances in that AZ are affected — other AZ instances continue normally. EFS replication creates a read-only replica. EBS is not shared across instances.

q: A company processes medical imaging data with large batch jobs on EC2 Spot Instances. A Spot interruption causes the batch job to fail after 5 hours of processing. What design change minimizes reprocessing?
options: Use Amazon EMR Spot with checkpointing disabled
Implement job checkpointing every 30 minutes, saving progress to S3, so interrupted jobs resume from the last checkpoint rather than restarting from scratch
Use Amazon Batch which automatically handles Spot interruptions
Switch to On-Demand Instances for all batch processing
explain: Checkpointing to S3 every 30 minutes limits potential reprocessing to 30 minutes of work after a Spot interruption. Without checkpointing, the full 5 hours of work is lost. On-Demand is more expensive and doesn't address workflow design. AWS Batch manages Spot interruptions by re-queuing the job but still starts it from the beginning without checkpointing. EMR Spot is for distributed data processing frameworks.

SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Checkpointing to S3 every 30 minutes limits potential reprocessing to 30 minutes of work after a Spot interruption. Without checkpointing, the full 5 hours of work is lost. On-Demand is more expensive and doesn't address workflow design. q: A company wants to implement a multi-region active-passive architecture. The secondary region should only receive traffic if the primary fails. How should they configure Route 53?
options: Use weighted routing with 100% weight on primary and 0% on secondary
Use geolocation routing to force all traffic to the primary region
Use failover routing policy with health checks on the primary endpoint — Route 53 automatically switches to the secondary when the primary fails
Use latency routing to prefer the primary region
explain: Route 53 failover routing designates a primary and secondary record. Health checks monitor the primary. When the primary health check fails, Route 53 automatically returns the secondary record's IP. Weighted routing with 0% never sends traffic to the secondary and doesn't auto-failover. Latency routing might route to a slower secondary region. Geolocation doesn't support failover behavior.

q: A company needs to ensure their Lambda functions continue processing during an AZ failure. Lambda functions are deployed in a VPC. What should the architect verify?
options: Lambda functions automatically span multiple AZs — no VPC configuration needed
Ensure the Lambda function's VPC configuration includes subnets in at least 2-3 AZs — Lambda distributes invocations across healthy AZs when one fails
Deploy identical Lambda functions in each AZ separately
Enable Lambda Provisioned Concurrency across all AZs
explain: When Lambda runs in a VPC, it attaches elastic network interfaces (ENIs) in the specified subnets. Configuring subnets in multiple AZs allows Lambda to use ENIs in healthy AZs if one AZ fails. Without multi-AZ subnets, an AZ failure can cause Lambda invocation failures for VPC-connected functions. Provisioned concurrency is for latency, not AZ resilience. Lambda is a regional service — you don't deploy per-AZ.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: multi-AZ = eliminate single-AZ failure. Always for: RDS, ElastiCache, ALB, EFS | many VPCs → Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke). Few VPCs → VPC Peering. 💡 Serverless = no server management, pay per use, auto-scale. Lambda limits: 15 min max, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, 1000 concurrent (soft). Cold start: Lambda function first invocation takes longer. Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm. Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. ❌ Eliminate distractors: When Lambda runs in a VPC, it attaches elastic network interfaces (ENIs) in the specified subnets. Configuring subnets in multiple AZs allows Lambda to use ENIs in healthy AZs if one AZ fails. Without multi-AZ subnets, an AZ failure can cause Lambda invocation failures for VPC-connected functions. Provisioned concurrency is for latency, not AZ resilience. Lambda is a regional service — you don't deploy per-AZ.

q: A company runs an application using API Gateway and Lambda behind CloudFront. During a region-wide event, API Gateway in the primary region becomes unavailable. How can they ensure continuity?
options: Use Route 53 to redirect CloudFront to a backup URL
Enable API Gateway multi-region deployment — it replicates automatically
CloudFront has a built-in API Gateway failover feature
Configure CloudFront with origin failover using an origin group — set the primary origin as the primary region's API Gateway and secondary as a standby region's API Gateway URL
explain: CloudFront origin failover with origin groups allows configuring a primary and secondary origin. If the primary origin returns 5xx errors or is unreachable, CloudFront automatically routes to the secondary origin. This provides transparent failover to a DR region without DNS changes. API Gateway doesn't auto-replicate across regions. Route 53 failover at the DNS level takes minutes due to TTL propagation.

q: A company's EventBridge rule triggers a Lambda function that processes order events. Lambda occasionally fails to process orders. They need to ensure no orders are lost and failed orders can be retried. What configuration achieves this?
options: Configure the EventBridge rule with a retry policy (up to 24 hours / 185 attempts) and a DLQ on the rule target — failed events after all retries go to the DLQ
Enable EventBridge event replay — it automatically retries all events
Use CloudWatch alarms to detect Lambda failures and resubmit events manually
Set the EventBridge rule target retry policy to 0 attempts and handle retries in the Lambda code
explain: EventBridge rule targets support retry policies with configurable maximum event age (up to 24 hours) and maximum retry attempts (up to 185). A DLQ on the rule target captures events that exhaust all retries for manual review. Event replay is for replaying events from an archive, not automatic retry of failed targets. CloudWatch alarms are monitoring tools, not retry mechanisms.

q: A company stores configuration data for 1000 EC2 instances in an S3 bucket. Instances read config on startup. The S3 bucket is in us-east-1. They want configuration reads to be available even during an S3 regional issue. What is the best approach?
options: Store configuration in EC2 user data — it's highly available
Use CloudFront to cache S3 configuration files — CloudFront serves from cache if S3 is unavailable
Enable S3 Cross-Region Replication to a bucket in a second region and configure instances to read from the nearest healthy bucket via Route 53 latency routing
Cache the configuration on instance startup in a local file and reload periodically
explain: CloudFront caches S3 content at edge locations. During an S3 regional event, CloudFront can serve cached configuration from the edge, maintaining high availability. TTL should be set appropriately. S3 CRR with Route 53 also works but adds operational complexity. EC2 user data isn't easily updated for 1000 instances. Local caching on instances requires restart or daemon reload.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: read scaling → Read Replicas (async, readable). Multi-AZ standby is NOT readable. 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. ❌ Eliminate distractors: CloudFront caches S3 content at edge locations. During an S3 regional event, CloudFront can serve cached configuration from the edge, maintaining high availability. TTL should be set appropriately. S3 CRR with Route 53 also works but adds operational complexity. EC2 user data isn't easily updated for 1000 instances. Local caching on instances requires restart or daemon reload.
