q: A company's on-premises application is being migrated to AWS. During the migration period, both on-premises and AWS versions must run simultaneously with data kept in sync. What database architecture supports this?
options: Use RDS Multi-AZ with the on-premises instance as the primary
Use AWS DataSync to sync database files between on-premises and AWS
Export on-premises data to S3 daily and import to RDS
Use AWS DMS with continuous replication between on-premises Oracle and AWS RDS, with bi-directional replication if writes occur on both sides
explain: AWS DMS with CDC (change data capture) supports ongoing replication during the migration cutover period. For bidirectional sync where both sides receive writes, DMS supports this with careful conflict management. Daily exports have high RPO and don't support real-time synchronization. DataSync handles file and object storage, not database transactions.
hint: 🛡️ Domain 2 (26%): Design Resilient Architectures. Key task statements: 2.1 Loosely coupled (SQS/SNS/EventBridge/microservices), 2.2 HA + fault-tolerant (Multi-AZ/Auto Scaling/DR tiers). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: multi-AZ = eliminate single-AZ failure. Always for: RDS, ElastiCache, ALB, EFS | on-prem file server → Storage Gateway File Gateway (NFS/SMB → S3). 💡 DR tier selection: cost vs speed trade-off. Cheapest → slowest: Backup/Restore → Pilot Light → Warm Standby → Active-Active. RTO > 1 hour → Backup/Restore is sufficient. RTO 1-4 hours + core DB must be ready → Pilot Light. RTO < 1 hour + some infra always running → Warm Standby. RTO near-zero + mission critical → Active-Active (multi-region). Aurora Global = best managed cross-region DB DR (<1s RPO). DynamoDB Global Tables = active-active NoSQL across regions. 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS DMS with CDC (change data capture) supports ongoing replication during the migration cutover period. For bidirectional sync where both sides receive writes, DMS supports this with careful conflict management. Daily exports have high RPO and don't support real-time synchronization. DataSync handles file and object storage, not database transactions.

q: A company wants to ensure that no single EC2 instance failure affects more than 25% of their application capacity. What is the minimum number of instances needed?
options: 2 instances — 50% impact if one fails
5 instances — 20% impact if one fails
3 instances — approximately 33% impact if one fails
4 instances — if one fails, 75% capacity remains (25% impact)
explain: With 4 instances, losing 1 means 1/4 = 25% capacity loss, which meets the 'no more than 25% impact' requirement exactly. With 3 instances, losing 1 is ~33% impact. With 5, it's 20% (better than required). The minimum to meet the constraint is 4 instances.

With 3 instances, losing 1 is ~33% impact. With 5, it's 20% (better than required). The minimum to meet the constraint is 4 instances.
keywords: Amazon EC2

q: A company uses CloudFront to serve a web application. During a new feature rollout, a bug causes incorrect responses. How can they quickly roll back to the previous working version?
options: Update the CloudFront distribution to point back to the previous origin
Invalidate the CloudFront cache and the origin will serve the old content
Use CloudFront Functions to rewrite responses back to the previous format
Use CloudFront continuous deployment policies with traffic weights — set 100% back to the primary distribution immediately
explain: CloudFront continuous deployment policies allow canary testing with traffic weights between a staging and production distribution. To roll back, set the staging distribution traffic weight to 0 and promote or revert with a single API call. Cache invalidation only removes cached objects — it doesn't change origin behavior. CloudFront Functions add processing but don't roll back origin code.

Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. ❌ Eliminate distractors: CloudFront continuous deployment policies allow canary testing with traffic weights between a staging and production distribution. To roll back, set the staging distribution traffic weight to 0 and promote or revert with a single API call. Cache invalidation only removes cached objects — it doesn't change origin behavior. q: A company's web application is experiencing slow database queries. Analysis shows that the same set of product catalog queries are run thousands of times per minute with no data changes. What is the most effective performance improvement?
options: Increase the RDS instance size to a larger instance class
Partition the RDS database table by product category
Add an RDS Read Replica and direct all queries to it
Implement Amazon ElastiCache for Redis in front of RDS to cache frequent query results
explain: ElastiCache for Redis caches query results in memory (sub-millisecond latency) and reduces database load from repeated identical queries. Since product catalog data changes infrequently, a cache TTL of several minutes is appropriate. Read Replicas reduce primary load but still hit the database for every query. Larger instance size helps but doesn't eliminate redundant query processing. Partitioning helps with large datasets but not repetitive read patterns.

q: A company serves static and dynamic content from a web application deployed globally. Static content (images, CSS, JS) loads slowly for users in Asia as the servers are in us-east-1. What is the best solution?
options: Deploy EC2 instances in ap-southeast-1 and replicate all content
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration for the static assets
Use Route 53 latency routing to direct Asian users to a separate S3 bucket
Use Amazon CloudFront to cache and serve static assets from edge locations closest to users globally
explain: CloudFront's global edge network (500+ points of presence) caches static content and delivers it from the nearest location to users. This dramatically reduces latency for global users. EC2 deployment in multiple regions requires content synchronization and is operationally complex. S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds up uploads to S3, not downloads by end users. Latency routing helps with origin selection but doesn't cache content at the edge.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. Key task statements: 3.1 Storage (EBS/EFS/S3 tiers), 3.2 Compute (EC2 types/placement groups/serverless), 3.3 Database (caching/read scaling/OLAP), 3.4 Network (CloudFront/Global Accelerator/Route 53). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: global low latency → CloudFront (L7/HTTP) or Global Accelerator (any TCP/UDP) | decoupling → SQS (queue), SNS (pub/sub), EventBridge (events) | static IP for load balancer → NLB (has EIP). ALB has dynamic IPs. 📋 Task 3.2 — High-performing and elastic compute solutions: Compute selection: No server management, event-driven, ≤15 min → Lambda. Containers, no cluster mgmt → Fargate (ECS or EKS). Containers with control of nodes → ECS/EKS on EC2. Batch jobs, retry, array → AWS Batch. PaaS (deploy code, AWS handles infra) → Elastic Beanstalk. Network performance: Cluster PG = lowest latency (HPC). EFA = MPI/HPC workloads needing RDMA. Instance type: memory-heavy → R family. Compute → C family. GPU/ML → P/G/Inf. 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: CloudFront's global edge network (500+ points of presence) caches static content and delivers it from the nearest location to users. This dramatically reduces latency for global users. EC2 deployment in multiple regions requires content synchronization and is operationally complex. S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds up uploads to S3, not downloads by end users. Latency routing helps with origin selection but doesn't cache content at the edge.

q: A company has an application that queries DynamoDB and frequently reads the same items. They want to reduce DynamoDB latency from single-digit milliseconds to microseconds for hot items. What should they implement?
options: Deploy DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) as an in-memory cache — it provides microsecond response times for cached reads
Use DynamoDB on-demand mode for unlimited read throughput
Enable DynamoDB auto-scaling to provision more read capacity
Add DynamoDB Global Tables to cache reads in multiple regions
explain: DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) is a purpose-built in-memory cache for DynamoDB that reduces read latency from milliseconds to microseconds. It's API-compatible with DynamoDB — minimal code changes needed. Auto-scaling adds capacity but DynamoDB is already single-digit milliseconds at scale. On-demand mode scales throughput but doesn't reduce latency. Global Tables provide multi-region replication, not per-region caching.

q: A company's application requires a shared file system accessible by 100 EC2 instances simultaneously with POSIX-compliant file access. What storage solution should they use?
options: Amazon EFS (Elastic File System) — provides shared POSIX-compliant NFS access for thousands of concurrent instances
EBS Multi-Attach with io2 volumes
EC2 instance store with rsync replication between instances
S3 with the S3 FUSE driver mounted on each instance

S3 with FUSE has limitations and performance issues for POSIX workloads. EBS Multi-Attach supports up to 16 instances (Nitro) and requires careful cluster file system management. Instance store with rsync creates consistency issues.

q: A machine learning training workload requires access to a large dataset stored in S3. Training jobs on multiple EC2 P3 instances are I/O bound, waiting on S3 reads. What improves training throughput?
options: Use EFS Provisioned Throughput mode instead of S3

q: A company's API responds slowly because every request makes multiple sequential database calls. Each call waits for the previous to complete, adding ~100ms per call. What architectural improvement reduces latency?
options: Move the API to Lambda for faster execution
Increase RDS instance size to handle queries faster
Execute independent database queries in parallel using async patterns or concurrent threads, reducing total latency to the slowest single query rather than the sum
Add an API Gateway caching layer
explain: Sequential independent queries add latency linearly (N × 100ms). Running independent queries in parallel reduces total latency to max(all queries) instead of sum(all queries). If 5 queries each take 100ms sequentially (500ms total), running in parallel reduces to ~100ms. This is the most impactful change for this specific pattern. Larger RDS improves query speed but doesn't change sequential execution. API Gateway caching helps for repeated identical requests.

q: A company's Lambda function loads a 50 MB machine learning model from S3 on every invocation, causing high latency. The model changes once per week. What optimization reduces this?
options: Switch to EC2 with the model pre-loaded on an AMI
Load the model lazily only when needed
Store the model in /tmp during initialization (outside the handler) so it is reused across warm invocations, or use Lambda Layers for the model file
Increase Lambda memory to load the model faster
explain: Lambda execution environments persist between invocations ('warm' environments). Initializing the model in the global scope (outside the handler function) means it is loaded once and reused across warm invocations. Lambda Layers allow packaging the model as a layer. /tmp storage persists across warm invocations (up to 512MB-10GB depending on configuration). Loading lazily per invocation doesn't improve latency. EC2 is appropriate for always-on inference but loses serverless benefits.

q: A real-time data pipeline needs to ingest 500,000 events per second from IoT devices, process each event, and store results. Which ingestion service handles this scale?
options: Amazon API Gateway with direct Lambda integration
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with sufficient shards (each shard handles 1,000 records/sec or 1 MB/sec write)
Amazon SQS FIFO queue
Amazon SQS Standard queue
explain: Kinesis Data Streams scales horizontally by adding shards. At 500K events/second, the required shard count depends on record size. Kinesis is designed for high-throughput real-time streaming. SQS Standard can handle high throughput but isn't designed for ordered real-time streaming analytics. SQS FIFO is limited to 3,000 msg/sec (with batching) per queue. API Gateway → Lambda would require extreme concurrency and isn't designed for IoT-scale ingestion.

q: A company serves a global web application with content in multiple languages. Pages are generated dynamically based on user location and language. The team wants to reduce origin server load while still personalizing content. What approach achieves this?
options: Deploy separate EC2 fleets in each AWS region for each language
Use CloudFront with cache behaviors based on language/region query strings or cookies, and Lambda@Edge to customize content at the edge before serving from cache
Use Route 53 geolocation routing to direct each user to a region-specific server
Cache all pages at CloudFront with a default TTL of 1 day
explain: CloudFront cache behaviors with query string/cookie forwarding allow caching different versions of pages per language/region combination. Lambda@Edge can perform lightweight personalization at the edge (e.g., redirects, header modification) without hitting the origin. Full per-region EC2 deployments are operationally complex. Route 53 geolocation routing helps but doesn't cache content — still hits the origin per request.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. Key task statements: 3.1 Storage (EBS/EFS/S3 tiers), 3.2 Compute (EC2 types/placement groups/serverless), 3.3 Database (caching/read scaling/OLAP), 3.4 Network (CloudFront/Global Accelerator/Route 53). 📋 Task 3.4 — High-performing network architectures: Network performance decision tree: Global static content delivery → CloudFront (CDN, edge caching). Global TCP/UDP with health routing + static IPs → Global Accelerator (not CDN). CloudFront vs Global Accelerator: CloudFront caches HTTP content. GA routes all protocols, no caching. Connect multiple VPCs at scale → Transit Gateway (replaces VPC peering mesh). Private on-premises to AWS (consistent, high bandwidth) → Direct Connect. Private on-premises to AWS (encrypted, cheaper, variable) → VPN. Private access to AWS services without internet → VPC Endpoint (Gateway = free for S3/DDB; Interface = paid for others). ALB vs NLB: ALB for HTTP apps (L7 features). NLB for extreme performance, static IP, TCP (L4). Route 53 failover vs Global Accelerator: both do failover but GA is faster (anycast). 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 Serverless = no server management, pay per use, auto-scale. Lambda limits: 15 min max, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, 1000 concurrent (soft). Cold start: Lambda function first invocation takes longer. Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm. Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. ❌ Eliminate distractors: CloudFront cache behaviors with query string/cookie forwarding allow caching different versions of pages per language/region combination. Lambda@Edge can perform lightweight personalization at the edge (e.g., redirects, header modification) without hitting the origin. Full per-region EC2 deployments are operationally complex. Route 53 geolocation routing helps but doesn't cache content — still hits the origin per request.

q: An application needs to store and query time-series data from 10,000 IoT sensors, generating 100 data points per second per sensor. Queries are time-range based. What is the most appropriate database service?
options: Amazon Timestream — purpose-built for time-series data with automatic tiering, fast time-range queries, and built-in time-series analytics functions
Amazon DynamoDB with timestamp sort key
Amazon RDS MySQL with a timestamp index
Amazon Redshift for all time-series analytics
explain: Amazon Timestream is purpose-built for IoT and operational time-series data. It automatically tiers data between in-memory and magnetic storage, provides built-in time-series functions, and scales to ingest trillions of events per day. RDS MySQL with timestamps works but doesn't scale to 1M data points/second. DynamoDB can store time-series but lacks native time-series analytics. Redshift is for analytical data warehousing, not real-time ingestion.

Relational + high performance + managed → Aurora. NoSQL + flexible schema + high throughput → DynamoDB. Analytics/reporting + SQL + petabyte → Redshift (OLAP, not OLTP). In-memory cache → ElastiCache Redis (features) or Memcached (simple). Graph relationships → Neptune. MongoDB workloads → DocumentDB. Cassandra workloads → Keyspaces. Read scaling vs HA: Read Replicas = scale reads. Multi-AZ = HA failover (standby not readable). DAX = DynamoDB only, microsecond. ElastiCache = any DB, sub-millisecond. OLAP hint words: 'analytics', 'reporting', 'BI', 'aggregate millions of rows' → Redshift. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Amazon Timestream is purpose-built for IoT and operational time-series data. RDS MySQL with timestamps works but doesn't scale to 1M data points/second. DynamoDB can store time-series but lacks native time-series analytics. Redshift is for analytical data warehousing, not real-time ingestion.

q: A company's Amazon RDS PostgreSQL instance is reaching CPU and IOPS limits during peak hours due to complex analytical queries from a reporting application. What is the most effective solution?
options: Upgrade to a larger RDS instance size
Increase RDS storage to SSD (gp3) for better IOPS
Enable RDS Multi-AZ to distribute read load between primary and standby
Create an RDS Read Replica and direct all reporting queries to the replica, offloading the primary for transactional workloads
explain: RDS Read Replicas use asynchronous replication to maintain a copy of the database. Directing analytical queries to the replica offloads the primary completely. The RDS Multi-AZ standby is not accessible for read traffic in standard RDS (only in Aurora). Larger instance helps but doesn't address the fundamental issue of mixing OLTP and analytical workloads. SSD storage reduces I/O wait but doesn't reduce CPU usage from complex queries.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. Key task statements: 3.1 Storage (EBS/EFS/S3 tiers), 3.2 Compute (EC2 types/placement groups/serverless), 3.3 Database (caching/read scaling/OLAP), 3.4 Network (CloudFront/Global Accelerator/Route 53). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: multi-AZ = eliminate single-AZ failure. Always for: RDS, ElastiCache, ALB, EFS | read scaling → Read Replicas (async, readable). Multi-AZ standby is NOT readable. 📋 Task 3.1 — High-performing and scalable storage solutions: Storage choice decision tree: Shared file access (multiple EC2, Linux) → EFS. Shared file access (Windows/SMB) → FSx for Windows. HPC/ML training high throughput → FSx for Lustre. Block storage (single EC2) → EBS. Object storage → S3. EBS IOPS tier: gp3 (baseline 3000 IOPS) → io2 (64000 IOPS, <0.1ms). S3 cost tier: Standard → IA (30d min) → Glacier Instant (quarterly) → Glacier Flexible (90d min) → Deep Archive (180d min, cheapest). Access pattern known → Lifecycle. Access pattern unknown → Intelligent-Tiering. Transfer Acceleration = faster UPLOAD to S3 (not for serving downloads → use CloudFront). 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: RDS Read Replicas use asynchronous replication to maintain a copy of the database. Directing analytical queries to the replica offloads the primary completely. The RDS Multi-AZ standby is not accessible for read traffic in standard RDS (only in Aurora). Larger instance helps but doesn't address the fundamental issue of mixing OLTP and analytical workloads. SSD storage reduces I/O wait but doesn't reduce CPU usage from complex queries.

q: A company needs to migrate a data warehouse from on-premises to AWS. The warehouse processes petabytes of data with complex SQL queries across many tables. Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?
options: Amazon Athena for all warehouse queries
Amazon Redshift with RA3 nodes — columnar storage, massively parallel processing, and Redshift Spectrum for querying S3
Amazon RDS Aurora PostgreSQL
Amazon EMR with Hive
explain: Amazon Redshift is AWS's managed data warehouse service, purpose-built for complex analytical SQL queries on petabyte-scale data. RA3 nodes separate compute from storage (using Redshift Managed Storage backed by S3). Redshift Spectrum queries data directly in S3. Aurora is an OLTP database. EMR/Hive is for Hadoop-based processing. Athena queries S3 data directly but doesn't provide a persistent warehouse with materialized tables and query optimization.

q: A company's application uses Amazon S3 to store millions of small objects. GET request latency is inconsistent and sometimes reaches seconds. What architectural improvement should be made?
options: Use Amazon CloudFront in front of S3 to cache objects at edge locations, ensuring consistent low-latency delivery
Increase S3 request rate by adding a prefix randomization strategy
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration
Move objects to EBS for lower latency
explain: CloudFront caches S3 objects at edge locations globally, providing consistent low-latency delivery regardless of where users are located. For frequently accessed objects, CloudFront dramatically reduces latency and S3 GET costs. S3 Transfer Acceleration is for faster uploads to S3. EBS is block storage and not suitable for millions of objects. Prefix randomization helps with S3 request rate limits (3,500 PUT/5,500 GET per prefix per second), which was relevant before AWS improved S3 partitioning.

Containers, no cluster mgmt → Fargate (ECS or EKS). Containers with control of nodes → ECS/EKS on EC2. Batch jobs, retry, array → AWS Batch. PaaS (deploy code, AWS handles infra) → Elastic Beanstalk. Network performance: Cluster PG = lowest latency (HPC). EFA = MPI/HPC workloads needing RDMA. Instance type: memory-heavy → R family. Compute → C family. GPU/ML → P/G/Inf. 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. q: A company runs a video streaming application and needs to deliver high-quality video to millions of concurrent viewers globally with adaptive bitrate streaming. What AWS architecture achieves this?
options: Serve video directly from EC2 instances with S3 as storage backend
Store video in EFS and mount on multiple EC2 instances behind an ALB
Use Amazon S3 for video storage, AWS Elemental MediaConvert for transcoding to adaptive bitrate (HLS/DASH), and CloudFront for global delivery
Use EC2 with GPU instances for video transcoding and delivery
explain: This is the standard AWS video-on-demand (VOD) architecture: S3 stores original and transcoded files, MediaConvert converts video to adaptive bitrate formats (HLS/DASH), and CloudFront delivers content from edge locations to millions of concurrent viewers. EC2 for transcoding and delivery requires massive infrastructure management. EFS with EC2 doesn't scale to millions of concurrent viewers and lacks global edge delivery.

q: A company processes large genomics datasets using AWS Batch. Jobs read input data from S3, process it, and write results back to S3. I/O throughput to S3 is the bottleneck. What improves throughput?
options: Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket
Move data to EBS io2 volumes for faster access
Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering to improve read performance
Use S3 multipart upload for large output files, S3 byte-range fetches for parallel reads, and ensure job instances are in the same region as the S3 bucket
explain: S3 multipart upload parallelizes large file writes. Byte-range GETs parallelize large file reads. Same-region access avoids cross-region data transfer latency and costs. These are standard S3 performance optimization techniques for large-scale data processing. Transfer Acceleration is for cross-internet transfers from outside AWS. EBS io2 isn't appropriate for batch job shared datasets. Intelligent-Tiering manages storage class/cost, not throughput.

q: A company's web application has a bottleneck at the database connection pool. The application uses Java with connection pooling and RDS PostgreSQL. During load spikes, the pool exhausts and requests queue or fail. What is the recommended solution?
options: Deploy Amazon RDS Proxy — it maintains a persistent pool of database connections and multiplexes application connections, handling spikes without exhausting database connections
Add more RDS Read Replicas to distribute connections
Increase max_connections in RDS PostgreSQL parameter group
Use PgBouncer on each EC2 application server
explain: RDS Proxy is a managed proxy that pools and shares database connections. During spikes, hundreds of application requests share a smaller pool of actual database connections. It reduces connection overhead and handles load spikes gracefully. Increasing max_connections has limits based on instance memory. PgBouncer works but requires self-managed deployment on each server. Read Replicas handle read queries, not connection pooling for writes.

q: A company uses Amazon Redshift for analytics. Complex queries that join large tables are taking 30+ minutes. What optimization technique should the Solutions Architect recommend first?
options: Analyze query execution plans, ensure distribution keys minimize data movement between nodes, and apply sort keys on frequently filtered columns
Add more Redshift compute nodes to increase parallel processing
Enable Redshift concurrency scaling
Move the queries to Amazon Athena
explain: Optimizing Redshift distribution keys (to co-locate joined data on the same nodes, minimizing data redistribution) and sort keys (to allow zone map pruning on filtered columns) often provides 10-100x query improvement. Adding nodes increases capacity but doesn't fix inefficient distribution. Concurrency scaling adds temporary capacity for concurrent queries but doesn't speed up individual slow queries. Athena doesn't automatically run Redshift queries faster.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. Key task statements: 3.1 Storage (EBS/EFS/S3 tiers), 3.2 Compute (EC2 types/placement groups/serverless), 3.3 Database (caching/read scaling/OLAP), 3.4 Network (CloudFront/Global Accelerator/Route 53). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: repeated reads → add caching layer (ElastiCache or DAX). 📋 Task 3.3 — High-performing database solutions: DB selection decision tree: Relational + ACID + SQL → RDS. Relational + high performance + managed → Aurora. NoSQL + flexible schema + high throughput → DynamoDB. Analytics/reporting + SQL + petabyte → Redshift (OLAP, not OLTP). In-memory cache → ElastiCache Redis (features) or Memcached (simple). Graph relationships → Neptune. MongoDB workloads → DocumentDB. Cassandra workloads → Keyspaces. Read scaling vs HA: Read Replicas = scale reads. Multi-AZ = HA failover (standby not readable). DAX = DynamoDB only, microsecond. ElastiCache = any DB, sub-millisecond. OLAP hint words: 'analytics', 'reporting', 'BI', 'aggregate millions of rows' → Redshift. 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Optimizing Redshift distribution keys (to co-locate joined data on the same nodes, minimizing data redistribution) and sort keys (to allow zone map pruning on filtered columns) often provides 10-100x query improvement. Adding nodes increases capacity but doesn't fix inefficient distribution. Concurrency scaling adds temporary capacity for concurrent queries but doesn't speed up individual slow queries. Athena doesn't automatically run Redshift queries faster.

q: A company runs an e-commerce platform where the product search feature is the most performance-critical component. The current MySQL LIKE queries on product names are slow. What is the most performant solution?
options: Use Amazon OpenSearch Service (formerly Elasticsearch) for product search — it provides inverted indexes, relevance scoring, and sub-second search on millions of products
Use DynamoDB with a GSI on the product name field
Cache the entire product catalog in ElastiCache
Add a MySQL full-text search index
explain: Amazon OpenSearch Service is purpose-built for full-text search with inverted indexes, providing relevance-scored search results in milliseconds across millions of products. MySQL full-text search works but doesn't scale as well or provide the same relevance features. Caching the full catalog in ElastiCache isn't practical for complex search queries. DynamoDB GSIs support exact lookups and prefix queries, not full-text search with relevance scoring.

q: A financial services company processes stock market data that must be analyzed in real-time as it arrives. Data arrives as a continuous stream and needs to be analyzed within 1 second of arrival. What service is appropriate?
options: Load data into S3 and run Athena queries every minute
Use Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics for Apache Flink to process the streaming data with SQL or Java/Scala applications, with sub-second processing latency
Load data into Redshift with real-time batch inserts every second
Use Lambda functions triggered by SQS to process each record
explain: Kinesis Data Analytics for Apache Flink is designed for real-time stream processing with millisecond-to-second latency. It processes data in-flight without storing it first. Flink supports windowed aggregations, joins, and complex event processing. Athena queries S3 data with seconds-to-minutes latency, not real-time. Redshift batch inserts aren't designed for sub-second real-time analytics. SQS → Lambda works but doesn't provide streaming windowed analytics natively.

q: A company's application uses EC2 instances backed by EBS gp2 volumes. Storage performance degrades during sustained high I/O workloads as the burst credits are depleted. What is the recommended fix?
options: Use EBS io1 volumes for all workloads regardless of cost
Increase EBS volume size — gp2 provides 3 IOPS/GB, so larger volume gives more baseline IOPS
Enable EBS burst credit monitoring with CloudWatch and add more gp2 volumes
Migrate to EBS gp3 volumes — gp3 provides a consistent 3,000 IOPS baseline regardless of size, with up to 16,000 IOPS configurable independently of capacity
explain: EBS gp3 provides a consistent 3,000 IOPS baseline (not burst-dependent) and allows independently configuring up to 16,000 IOPS and 1,000 MB/s throughput at lower cost than gp2 or io1. gp2 burst credits deplete under sustained I/O, causing exactly the described issue. io1 provides consistent IOPS but at higher cost than needed for most workloads. Multiple gp2 volumes add complexity.

q: A company needs to query log data stored in S3 without loading it into a database. The logs are in JSON format, partitioned by date. They run ad-hoc analytical queries. What is the recommended approach?
options: Load logs into RDS daily for querying
Use EMR Hive for all log analytics
Use Amazon Athena with AWS Glue Data Catalog to query S3 data using standard SQL — partitioning by date enables partition pruning for faster, cheaper queries
Use S3 Select to run SQL on individual log files
explain: Athena queries S3 data in-place using standard SQL without ETL. The Glue Data Catalog defines the schema. Partition pruning on date partitions ensures only relevant data is scanned, reducing cost (charged per TB scanned) and latency. S3 Select works on individual objects, not multi-file analytical queries. RDS loading is costly and slow. EMR Hive works but requires cluster management and is slower for ad-hoc queries than Athena.

q: A company's microservices make many small requests to a shared DynamoDB table. The hot partition containing the most popular items is being throttled. What is the most effective resolution?
options: Enable DynamoDB Streams and process hot items separately
Switch from DynamoDB provisioned to on-demand capacity
Review and change the partition key design to distribute writes more evenly — consider adding a random suffix or using a composite key that distributes hot items across partitions
Add a DynamoDB Global Secondary Index on the hot items
explain: Hot partition issues occur when too many requests hit the same partition key. Redesigning the partition key to distribute requests evenly (e.g., adding a hash suffix, using a composite key with higher cardinality) is the most effective long-term fix. On-demand capacity helps with burst but doesn't fix hot partitions — individual partitions still have limits. GSIs replicate data to different partitions but reads on the GSI would still hit the same hot items.

q: A company runs a mobile game with a leaderboard that must display the top 100 players in real-time. The leaderboard updates thousands of times per second. What data store is optimized for this use case?
options: Amazon DynamoDB with a GSI on score
Amazon ElastiCache for Redis using a Sorted Set (ZSET) — provides O(log N) score updates and O(log N + k) range queries for real-time leaderboards
Amazon RDS MySQL with ORDER BY score DESC LIMIT 100
Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL with partitioned tables
explain: Redis Sorted Sets are purpose-built for leaderboard use cases. ZADD updates scores in O(log N), ZREVRANGE retrieves top K players in O(log N + K). This provides sub-millisecond leaderboard operations at any scale. RDS ORDER BY queries scan all records. DynamoDB GSI doesn't support range queries on non-partition-key attributes efficiently for real-time leaderboards. Aurora handles the query but with higher latency than in-memory Redis.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. q: A company runs a RESTful API with 50,000 requests per minute. About 80% of requests are for the same top 100 product details, which change hourly. What configuration reduces backend load and improves response time?
options: Enable API Gateway caching with a 1-hour TTL — cache responses for identical requests, serving 80% of traffic from cache without hitting the backend Lambda or EC2
Use Lambda reserved concurrency for the product API
Deploy more API Gateway stages
Add ElastiCache in front of the database only
explain: API Gateway caching with an appropriate TTL caches identical request+response pairs at the API Gateway layer. 80% of requests served from cache means 80% reduction in Lambda invocations and database queries, reducing latency to <10ms for cached responses. Lambda reserved concurrency manages scaling but doesn't reduce invocation count. ElastiCache reduces database load but requests still go through Lambda.

q: A data processing application on EC2 requires low-latency, high-throughput access to a temporary scratch dataset of 10 TB during processing. Data does not need to persist after the instance terminates. What storage option provides the best performance?
options: EC2 instance store — NVMe SSD directly attached to the physical host, providing the lowest latency and highest I/O throughput with no network overhead
Amazon EFS with Max I/O performance mode
S3 with multipart download for the scratch dataset
EBS gp3 volume attached to the instance
explain: EC2 instance store (NVMe SSD) is physically attached to the server hosting the EC2 instance, providing the lowest possible latency (no network hop) and highest I/O throughput. For temporary scratch data that doesn't need to persist, instance store is ideal. EBS adds network latency. EFS adds network latency and NFS overhead. S3 is object storage not suitable for low-latency scratch I/O.

q: A company serves user-generated images uploaded to S3. Images are served in multiple sizes (thumbnail, medium, full). Currently, all resizing happens at upload time for all possible sizes. This is slow and wastes storage for sizes that may never be requested. What is a better approach?
options: Pre-generate all sizes using Lambda at upload time — faster than the current approach
Store only the original and resize on every request
Use Amazon Rekognition to resize images
Generate image sizes on-demand using Lambda@Edge or CloudFront Functions when a specific size is first requested, cache the result in CloudFront — generate only sizes actually needed
explain: On-demand image resizing with caching generates only requested sizes, eliminating wasted processing and storage. Lambda@Edge intercepts CloudFront cache misses, resizes the image from the S3 original, and the result is cached at the edge for subsequent requests. Pre-generating all sizes wastes resources. Resizing on every request without caching is the worst performance pattern. Rekognition is for image analysis, not resizing.

q: A company uses AWS Lambda for a high-throughput event processing pipeline. They notice increased latency when Lambda scales from 0 to handling 1,000 concurrent invocations within a few seconds. What is causing this and how do they fix it?
options: Use smaller Lambda function packages to reduce cold start time
Increase Lambda memory allocation to allow faster scaling
Lambda's burst scaling limit causes cold starts when scaling rapidly — use Provisioned Concurrency for predictable traffic patterns and request higher burst limits for sudden spikes
Lambda cannot scale to 1,000 concurrent invocations — use EC2 instead
explain: Lambda has a burst scaling limit (default 3,000 concurrent invocations added per minute, varies by region). Rapid scaling from 0 to 1,000 causes many cold starts simultaneously. Provisioned Concurrency keeps environments warm for predictable traffic. For unpredictable sudden spikes, request a burst limit increase. Memory increases speed up cold start initialization but don't address burst scaling. Smaller packages reduce cold start time marginally.

q: A company's microservices architecture has services communicating via REST APIs. They need a way to discover service endpoints automatically as services scale up and down. What AWS service provides service discovery?
options: Amazon API Gateway with Lambda integration
Application Load Balancer with path-based routing
Route 53 public hosted zones with manual updates
AWS Cloud Map — provides service discovery for cloud resources, integrating with Route 53 and ECS for automatic DNS-based or API-based service registration and discovery
explain: AWS Cloud Map allows services to register themselves with health checks and be discovered by other services via DNS or API calls. It integrates natively with Amazon ECS and EKS for automatic registration. Route 53 public zones expose internal services externally and require manual updates. ALB and API Gateway are traffic management tools, not service discovery mechanisms.

q: A company needs to run database queries that join multiple tables with billions of rows. Query times exceed 10 hours on their current RDS instance. They cannot change the application queries. What architectural change should the Solutions Architect recommend?
options: Enable RDS Performance Insights and optimize indexes
Migrate the analytical workload to Amazon Redshift — its columnar storage, massively parallel processing, and query optimization are designed for complex queries on billions of rows
Upgrade to the largest available RDS instance class
Use RDS Aurora with Parallel Query enabled
explain: Redshift's columnar storage reads only required columns, MPP distributes query execution across multiple nodes, and query optimization (through distribution keys, sort keys, and query planner) handles complex multi-table joins on billions of rows efficiently — typically in seconds to minutes. RDS is an OLTP database not designed for this scale. Aurora Parallel Query helps but still has OLTP limitations. Index optimization helps for OLTP queries but not for full analytical scans.

q: A company streams real-time events to Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. Multiple consumer applications each need to read the full stream independently at their own pace. What feature should they enable?
options: Configure separate Kinesis shards for each consumer application
Use SQS fan-out via SNS subscription to duplicate messages
Create separate Kinesis streams for each consumer
Enable Kinesis Enhanced Fan-Out — each registered consumer gets a dedicated 2 MB/s per shard throughput pipe, allowing up to 20 consumers to read independently at full speed
explain: Enhanced Fan-Out provides each registered consumer with a dedicated 2 MB/s per shard throughput (using HTTP/2 push model), allowing multiple consumers to read independently without sharing the standard 2 MB/s read limit per shard. Standard shared iterator consumers share the 2 MB/s read limit. Separate shards per consumer doubles the cost. Separate streams duplicate ingestion infrastructure.

Shared file access (Windows/SMB) → FSx for Windows. HPC/ML training high throughput → FSx for Lustre. Block storage (single EC2) → EBS. Object storage → S3. EBS IOPS tier: gp3 (baseline 3000 IOPS) → io2 (64000 IOPS, <0.1ms). S3 cost tier: Standard → IA (30d min) → Glacier Instant (quarterly) → Glacier Flexible (90d min) → Deep Archive (180d min, cheapest). Access pattern known → Lifecycle. Access pattern unknown → Intelligent-Tiering. Transfer Acceleration = faster UPLOAD to S3 (not for serving downloads → use CloudFront). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Enhanced Fan-Out provides each registered consumer with a dedicated 2 MB/s per shard throughput (using HTTP/2 push model), allowing multiple consumers to read independently without sharing the standard 2 MB/s read limit per shard. Standard shared iterator consumers share the 2 MB/s read limit. Separate shards per consumer doubles the cost. Separate streams duplicate ingestion infrastructure.

q: A company's Lambda function processes images and is memory-bound. Doubling memory from 1GB to 2GB reduces execution time from 10 seconds to 4 seconds. Is this cost-effective?
options: No — doubling memory always doubles cost
Memory and cost are unrelated in Lambda
Yes — Lambda pricing is based on GB-seconds (memory × duration). At 1GB: 10 GB-sec. At 2GB: 8 GB-sec. The higher memory option costs less because execution time reduces more than memory increases
Only reduce memory to minimize Lambda costs
explain: Lambda charges for GB-seconds (memory × duration in seconds). 1 GB × 10s = 10 GB-sec. 2 GB × 4s = 8 GB-sec. The 2GB option is 20% cheaper AND 60% faster. This counterintuitive result occurs when increasing memory reduces duration more than proportionally. Always benchmark Lambda memory to find the cost-optimal configuration.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. q: A company needs to implement a write-heavy workload on DynamoDB (millions of writes per second globally). They have a fixed partition key structure that cannot be changed. What can be done to prevent throttling?
options: Use DynamoDB write sharding — add a random number suffix (1-N) to the partition key to distribute writes across N logical partitions, then use parallel queries to aggregate reads
Use DynamoDB streams to buffer writes
Enable DynamoDB on-demand mode to absorb all writes
Enable DynamoDB auto-scaling with maximum throughput
explain: Write sharding artificially increases partition key cardinality by appending a random suffix. Writes are distributed across N partition key values, spreading load across multiple DynamoDB partitions. On-demand mode can help with sudden bursts but individual hot partitions still have limits. Streams are for change event processing, not write distribution. Auto-scaling responds to throttling after it occurs.

q: A company serves an API from multiple regions. They want requests to be routed to the closest AWS region, with fallback to other regions if one region is unhealthy. Which Route 53 configuration achieves this?
options: Latency-based routing with health checks — routes to the lowest-latency healthy endpoint, automatically failing over to the next best region if the primary is unhealthy
Simple routing with lowest TTL possible
Geolocation routing by country to the nearest region
Weighted routing with equal weights across all regions
explain: Latency-based routing with health checks routes each user to the lowest-latency healthy endpoint. If the closest region's health check fails, Route 53 automatically routes to the next lowest-latency healthy region. Geolocation routing routes based on physical location, not network latency, and doesn't account for route quality. Weighted routing doesn't optimize for latency or handle regional failures.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. Key task statements: 3.1 Storage (EBS/EFS/S3 tiers), 3.2 Compute (EC2 types/placement groups/serverless), 3.3 Database (caching/read scaling/OLAP), 3.4 Network (CloudFront/Global Accelerator/Route 53). 📋 Task 3.4 — High-performing network architectures: Network performance decision tree: Global static content delivery → CloudFront (CDN, edge caching). Global TCP/UDP with health routing + static IPs → Global Accelerator (not CDN). CloudFront vs Global Accelerator: CloudFront caches HTTP content. GA routes all protocols, no caching. Connect multiple VPCs at scale → Transit Gateway (replaces VPC peering mesh). Private on-premises to AWS (consistent, high bandwidth) → Direct Connect. Private on-premises to AWS (encrypted, cheaper, variable) → VPN. Private access to AWS services without internet → VPC Endpoint (Gateway = free for S3/DDB; Interface = paid for others). ALB vs NLB: ALB for HTTP apps (L7 features). NLB for extreme performance, static IP, TCP (L4). Route 53 failover vs Global Accelerator: both do failover but GA is faster (anycast). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Latency-based routing with health checks routes each user to the lowest-latency healthy endpoint. If the closest region's health check fails, Route 53 automatically routes to the next lowest-latency healthy region. Geolocation routing routes based on physical location, not network latency, and doesn't account for route quality. Weighted routing doesn't optimize for latency or handle regional failures.
keywords: Amazon Route 53
AWS Direct Connect (dedicated private 1/10/100 Gbps, not encrypted by default)
AWS Global Accelerator (static anycast IPs, routes to nearest healthy endpoint, TCP/UDP)
AWS Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke, connects 1000s VPCs + VPNs + DX)
Config
Route 53 routing: Latency (lowest latency region), Failover (primary/secondary), Weighted (% split), Geolocation (by country/continent), Geoproximity (distance + bias), Multivalue

q: A company uses Amazon SQS for async processing. Message processing takes 2-3 minutes. Consumers are occasionally processing the same message twice. What SQS setting should be adjusted?
options: Enable SQS FIFO queues to prevent duplicate processing
Increase message retention period
Increase the visibility timeout to be longer than the maximum processing time (e.g., 5 minutes) — this prevents the message from becoming visible to other consumers while being processed
Reduce the number of consumer instances
explain: If the visibility timeout is shorter than processing time, SQS makes the message visible again before processing completes, allowing another consumer to pick it up — causing double processing. Setting the visibility timeout longer than the maximum processing time prevents this. FIFO queues provide exactly-once delivery but require API compatibility. Fewer consumers reduce duplicate risk but also reduce throughput.

q: A company runs a multi-tenant SaaS application on AWS. Each tenant's data is stored in a separate DynamoDB table (1000 tenants). Admin dashboards need to query across all tenant tables for operational metrics. This is slow and involves 1000 API calls. What architecture resolves this?
options: Use a single DynamoDB table with tenant ID as partition key — cross-tenant analytics queries use Amazon Athena via DynamoDB export to S3 or DynamoDB Streams to a data lake
Replicate each tenant table to RDS for cross-tenant queries
Use AWS Glue to join all 1000 separate tenant tables
Combine all tenant data into a single DynamoDB table with tenant ID as partition key and use Scan for cross-tenant queries
explain: A single-table design with tenant_id as partition key is the recommended DynamoDB multi-tenancy pattern. For cross-tenant analytics, DynamoDB export to S3 combined with Athena provides efficient cross-tenant queries without individual table API calls. Scanning a shared table for analytics is inefficient. AWS Glue joining 1000 tables is complex. RDS replication of 1000 tables is operationally heavy.

q: An application written in Node.js is deployed on AWS Lambda. The function loads a large NPM dependency tree causing 3-second cold starts. What optimization reduces cold start time?
options: Use Lambda Layers for shared dependencies, enable Lambda SnapStart (for Java), minimize the deployment package size, and consider bundling with esbuild/webpack to reduce package size
Increase Lambda timeout to 30 seconds
Increase Lambda memory to 3GB to speed up initialization
Use Node.js 14 instead of Node.js 18
explain: Reducing deployment package size is the most effective cold start optimization for Node.js Lambda. Bundling with esbuild/webpack tree-shakes unused code, dramatically reducing package size. Lambda Layers allow dependencies to be cached separately. Note: Lambda SnapStart is currently only for Java — but package optimization and layers apply to Node.js. Increasing timeout doesn't reduce cold start. Node.js version has minimal cold start impact.

q: A company runs an application on EC2 that handles image processing. Processing a single image requires GPU computation. The workload is bursty — hundreds of images arrive simultaneously then no traffic for hours. What compute option is most suitable?
options: Keep GPU EC2 instances running 24/7 for immediate availability

Use Amazon Elastic Inference or deploy GPU EC2 instances (p3/g4dn) via Auto Scaling groups that scale to 0 during idle periods and scale out on queue depth
explain: GPU EC2 Auto Scaling groups that scale to 0 during idle periods and scale out based on SQS queue depth handle bursty GPU workloads cost-effectively. CPU processing is 10-100x slower for deep learning image analysis.

q: A company needs to process 10 TB of log files stored in S3 every night. The processing involves filtering, aggregating, and writing results back to S3. The job must complete within 2 hours. What service is most appropriate?
options: EC2 single instance with a bash script to process files sequentially
AWS Glue ETL job with 2 DPUs
AWS Lambda with SQS trigger — process each log file in a separate Lambda invocation
Amazon EMR with Apache Spark — distributes the 10 TB processing across a cluster of EC2 instances, completing in parallel within the time window
explain: EMR with Spark distributes the processing workload across a cluster. For 10 TB in 2 hours, you size the cluster accordingly (e.g., 10-20 instances). EMR can be configured as transient clusters (spin up for the job, terminate when done). Lambda has 15-minute maximum execution time per invocation — individual large files may exceed this. Glue with only 2 DPUs would be too slow for 10 TB in 2 hours. Single EC2 is not parallelized.

q: A company's API is frequently called with the same query parameters by thousands of clients simultaneously during daily batch reporting windows. The backend processes these calls identically. What API optimization reduces redundant processing?
options: Use SQS to queue all batch reporting API calls
Implement API Gateway response caching — all clients requesting the same endpoint with the same parameters receive the cached response, processing the backend request only once per TTL period
Increase Lambda concurrency limits to handle all simultaneous calls
Use Lambda Provisioned Concurrency to handle the batch reporting load
explain: API Gateway caching returns cached responses for identical requests within the TTL window. During batch reporting, all clients requesting the same data receive the cached result while the backend processes the request only once. This dramatically reduces Lambda invocations and backend load. Provisioned Concurrency ensures Lambda is warm but processes every request. SQS makes the API asynchronous. Concurrency limits handle volume but not redundant processing.

q: A company's Aurora MySQL cluster is experiencing long-running queries that block other transactions. How can they isolate long-running reporting queries from transactional workloads without separate databases?
options: Schedule long-running queries to run only at night
Use Aurora Multi-Master to split writes between nodes
Use Aurora Replicas for read queries — direct OLTP writes and reads to the primary, direct analytical/reporting queries to the Aurora reader endpoint which balances across replicas
Increase Aurora instance size to handle both workloads
explain: Aurora Reader endpoints distribute read traffic across Aurora Replicas. Long-running queries on replicas don't block writes on the primary. The primary handles transactional OLTP. Replicas asynchronously receive changes with minimal lag. Scheduling queries at night reduces business hours impact but doesn't separate workloads technically. Aurora Multi-Master (now Aurora limitless) provides multi-writer, not workload isolation. Larger instances don't prevent lock contention.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. Multi-AZ standby is NOT readable | decoupling → SQS (queue), SNS (pub/sub), EventBridge (events). 📋 Task 3.2 — High-performing and elastic compute solutions: Compute selection: No server management, event-driven, ≤15 min → Lambda. Containers, no cluster mgmt → Fargate (ECS or EKS). Containers with control of nodes → ECS/EKS on EC2. Batch jobs, retry, array → AWS Batch. PaaS (deploy code, AWS handles infra) → Elastic Beanstalk. Network performance: Cluster PG = lowest latency (HPC). EFA = MPI/HPC workloads needing RDMA. Instance type: memory-heavy → R family. Compute → C family. GPU/ML → P/G/Inf. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Aurora Reader endpoints distribute read traffic across Aurora Replicas. Long-running queries on replicas don't block writes on the primary. The primary handles transactional OLTP. Replicas asynchronously receive changes with minimal lag. Scheduling queries at night reduces business hours impact but doesn't separate workloads technically. Aurora Multi-Master (now Aurora limitless) provides multi-writer, not workload isolation. Larger instances don't prevent lock contention.

q: A company's on-premises application connects to AWS via Direct Connect. They want to cache AWS service responses on-premises to reduce Direct Connect traffic and latency for frequently accessed data. What AWS service provides this capability?
options: Route 53 Resolver with response caching
AWS DataSync with local caching agent
AWS CloudFront with on-premises points of presence
AWS Storage Gateway (File Gateway) — caches frequently accessed S3 objects on-premises, providing low-latency NFS/SMB access while transparently storing data in S3
explain: AWS Storage Gateway File Gateway caches the most recently used data on-premises, providing low-latency access for hot data while storing the full dataset in S3. This reduces Direct Connect bandwidth for repeated access to the same files. DataSync is for data migration and synchronization, not caching. CloudFront edge locations are in AWS regions and major internet hubs, not customer on-premises locations. Route 53 Resolver caches DNS, not application data.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. Key task statements: 3.1 Storage (EBS/EFS/S3 tiers), 3.2 Compute (EC2 types/placement groups/serverless), 3.3 Database (caching/read scaling/OLAP), 3.4 Network (CloudFront/Global Accelerator/Route 53). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: on-prem file server → Storage Gateway File Gateway (NFS/SMB → S3). 📋 Task 3.4 — High-performing network architectures: Network performance decision tree: Global static content delivery → CloudFront (CDN, edge caching). Global TCP/UDP with health routing + static IPs → Global Accelerator (not CDN). CloudFront vs Global Accelerator: CloudFront caches HTTP content. GA routes all protocols, no caching. Connect multiple VPCs at scale → Transit Gateway (replaces VPC peering mesh). Private on-premises to AWS (consistent, high bandwidth) → Direct Connect. Private on-premises to AWS (encrypted, cheaper, variable) → VPN. Private access to AWS services without internet → VPC Endpoint (Gateway = free for S3/DDB; Interface = paid for others). ALB vs NLB: ALB for HTTP apps (L7 features). NLB for extreme performance, static IP, TCP (L4). Route 53 failover vs Global Accelerator: both do failover but GA is faster (anycast). 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS Storage Gateway File Gateway caches the most recently used data on-premises, providing low-latency access for hot data while storing the full dataset in S3. This reduces Direct Connect bandwidth for repeated access to the same files. DataSync is for data migration and synchronization, not caching. CloudFront edge locations are in AWS regions and major internet hubs, not customer on-premises locations. Route 53 Resolver caches DNS, not application data.

q: A company uses Amazon CloudFront. When the origin server returns a 503 error, CloudFront serves a stale cached response instead of showing users an error. How can they configure this behavior?
options: Enable CloudFront stale-if-error with a TTL — serve the last cached response when the origin returns 5xx errors up to the configured time
Configure CloudFront Cache-Control headers with stale-while-revalidate directive and enable origin failover
Enable CloudFront Origin Shield
Use Lambda@Edge to catch 5xx errors and serve a custom response
explain: CloudFront stale-if-error allows serving cached content when the origin returns 5xx errors for a configurable duration. This maintains user experience during origin outages by serving potentially stale but functional content. Origin failover routes to a secondary origin on 5xx errors. Origin Shield adds a caching layer in front of the origin but doesn't serve stale content on failure. Lambda@Edge can handle errors but adds latency and cost.

q: A company needs to migrate 50 TB of data from on-premises to Amazon S3 over a slow 1 Gbps WAN link that would take weeks via the internet. They need the data transferred in 3 days. What AWS service achieves this?
options: Use S3 Transfer Acceleration over the existing WAN link
Use AWS Direct Connect at 10 Gbps
Use AWS Snowball Edge Storage Optimized — an 80 TB physical device shipped to the data center, data is loaded offline and shipped to AWS, bypassing network limitations
Use parallel multipart uploads with multiple S3 endpoints
explain: AWS Snowball Edge allows offline data transfer. A 50TB dataset can be loaded in 1-2 days using Snowball's local storage and then shipped to AWS. AWS loads data into S3 after receipt, meeting the 3-day window without network constraints. S3 Transfer Acceleration and multipart uploads optimize internet transfers but 50 TB over 1 Gbps still takes several days minimum. Setting up a new 10 Gbps Direct Connect connection takes weeks.

q: A company runs a social media application where each user can follow thousands of other users. Loading a user's feed requires aggregating posts from all followed users. This query is very slow. What data architecture improvement helps?
options: Cache the full user post history in ElastiCache per user
Use RDS with complex JOIN queries and increase instance size

Pre-compute and materialize the feed using a fan-out write strategy — when a user posts, write the post to the feeds of all followers in DynamoDB or ElastiCache, making feed reads O(1) instead of O(followers)
Use Amazon Neptune for social graph queries
explain: Fan-out on write pre-computes feeds at write time. When User A posts, their post is immediately added to the feed tables/caches of all N followers. Feed reads become a simple lookup instead of aggregating across all followed users. This trades write overhead for dramatically faster reads — correct for read-heavy social media. Neptune handles graph traversal (finding followers) but doesn't pre-compute feeds.

q: A company stores time-series sensor data in Amazon S3 in JSON format. Queries using Athena are slow and expensive because they scan all data even for recent queries. What partitioning strategy improves this?
options: Enable Athena query result caching for all queries
Partition data in S3 by year/month/day and use Athena partition projection — queries with date filters scan only relevant partitions, dramatically reducing data scanned and query cost
Use S3 Select instead of Athena for all queries
Use Athena CTAS to convert all data to ORC format
explain: S3 partitioning by date (s3://bucket/year=2024/month=03/day=15/) combined with Athena partition projection allows Athena to prune irrelevant partitions based on WHERE clause date filters. Queries on recent data scan only recent partitions, not the entire dataset. ORC format improves column pruning but doesn't address partition scanning. Athena result caching helps for repeated identical queries. S3 Select queries individual objects, not analytical multi-file datasets.

q: A company's application makes 1,000 read API calls per second to a third-party API that has a rate limit of 100 calls per second. Requests are failing due to rate limiting. What AWS architecture pattern resolves this?
options: Implement an SQS queue to buffer outgoing API calls and a Lambda consumer that respects the rate limit (100/sec) with retry logic and exponential backoff on rate limit errors
Switch to a CDN-backed version of the third-party API
Use CloudFront to cache third-party API responses
Increase Lambda concurrency to make calls faster
explain: SQS as a buffer decouples the internal request rate (1,000/sec) from the outbound API call rate (100/sec). Lambda reads from SQS at the permitted rate of 100 calls/second. Failed calls due to rate limiting are retried. CloudFront can cache third-party API responses if they're cacheable, but not all API responses are idempotent or cacheable. Lambda concurrency increases the problem by calling faster.

q: A company wants to improve read performance for their Amazon Aurora cluster. They have 5 Aurora Replicas across 2 AZs. The application currently connects to the cluster reader endpoint. They want individual consumers to get dedicated throughput. What Aurora feature should they use?
options: Migrate to DynamoDB for better read scalability
Create 5 separate Aurora clusters, one per consumer
Create custom Aurora endpoints for specific replica subsets — assign different replica subsets to different consumer groups so each gets dedicated reader throughput
Use Aurora Auto Scaling to add more replicas dynamically
explain: Aurora custom endpoints allow creating named endpoints that route to a specific subset of replicas. You can create a custom endpoint for reporting (pointing to 2 high-memory replicas) and another for application reads (pointing to 3 other replicas). This gives dedicated throughput to each consumer group. Separate clusters add management overhead and storage costs. Auto scaling adds capacity but doesn't provide isolation between consumers.

q: A company receives large CSV files (50-500 MB each) into an S3 bucket. A Lambda function processes each file but is hitting the 15-minute execution time limit. What architecture resolves this?
options: Move the Lambda to a container and increase the timeout
Increase Lambda memory to process files faster
Use S3 event notifications to trigger an AWS Batch job or an EMR step for large files, or split files on upload using S3 Object Lambda to process in chunks
Use Step Functions to chain multiple Lambda functions processing the file sequentially
explain: Large file processing exceeding Lambda's 15-minute limit should use AWS Batch (for containerized jobs with no time limit) or EMR. Alternatively, splitting files into chunks allows Lambda to process each chunk within the time limit. Step Functions chaining Lambda functions can workaround the limit but complicates logic. Container Lambda has the same 15-minute limit. More memory speeds processing but doesn't address the 15-minute hard limit.

Lambda limits: 15 min max, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, 1000 concurrent (soft). Cold start: Lambda function first invocation takes longer. Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm. Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Large file processing exceeding Lambda's 15-minute limit should use AWS Batch (for containerized jobs with no time limit) or EMR. Container Lambda has the same 15-minute limit. More memory speeds processing but doesn't address the 15-minute hard limit.

q: A company wants to store and serve 100 million small JSON documents (average 1 KB). The primary access pattern is fetching a document by a unique ID. Which storage option provides the lowest latency at scale?
options: Amazon S3 with documents organized by ID in the key name
Amazon RDS MySQL with an indexed id column
Amazon DynamoDB with the document ID as the partition key — single-digit millisecond read latency at any scale
Amazon EFS with documents stored as files
explain: DynamoDB is designed for high-throughput, low-latency key-based access to any volume of data. GetItem by partition key provides single-digit millisecond latency. RDS adds query planning overhead. S3 GetObject has 10-50ms typical latency and is optimized for large objects. EFS NFS protocol adds overhead for millions of small files.

q: A company's web application uses an ALB and an Auto Scaling group. During Black Friday, the Auto Scaling group takes 8 minutes to launch new instances due to AMI boot time. What reduces instance launch time?
options: Use Spot Instances which launch faster than On-Demand
Increase the Auto Scaling cooldown period
Use pre-built golden AMIs that include all application code and dependencies pre-installed, avoiding bootstrap scripts that install software at launch time
Use smaller instance types that boot faster
explain: Golden AMIs with pre-installed software eliminate the time spent running bootstrap scripts (installing dependencies, pulling code from repos) during instance launch. Boot time goes from 8 minutes to typically under 2 minutes. Smaller instances boot at similar speed. Cooldown periods slow scaling. Spot Instances have the same boot process.

q: A company uses AWS CloudFront. Their dynamic API responses (unique per user) are being accidentally cached and served to other users. What configuration prevents this?
options: Disable CloudFront entirely for the API
Configure CloudFront cache behavior for the API paths with Cache-Control: no-cache or min-TTL=0, and forward all request headers and cookies used to differentiate user responses
Enable CloudFront signed URLs for all API requests
Use a separate CloudFront distribution for static and dynamic content
explain: Dynamic user-specific content should not be cached. Setting TTL to 0 or Cache-Control: no-cache ensures CloudFront always fetches from the origin for these requests. Forwarding differentiating headers/cookies creates separate cache entries per user, preventing cross-user contamination even if some caching is desired. Signed URLs control access but don't prevent caching. Separate distributions help organization but don't prevent caching.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. Key task statements: 3.1 Storage (EBS/EFS/S3 tiers), 3.2 Compute (EC2 types/placement groups/serverless), 3.3 Database (caching/read scaling/OLAP), 3.4 Network (CloudFront/Global Accelerator/Route 53). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: prevent across org (even root) → SCP on root OU. 📋 Task 3.4 — High-performing network architectures: Network performance decision tree: Global static content delivery → CloudFront (CDN, edge caching). Global TCP/UDP with health routing + static IPs → Global Accelerator (not CDN). CloudFront vs Global Accelerator: CloudFront caches HTTP content. GA routes all protocols, no caching. Connect multiple VPCs at scale → Transit Gateway (replaces VPC peering mesh). Private on-premises to AWS (consistent, high bandwidth) → Direct Connect. Private on-premises to AWS (encrypted, cheaper, variable) → VPN. Private access to AWS services without internet → VPC Endpoint (Gateway = free for S3/DDB; Interface = paid for others). ALB vs NLB: ALB for HTTP apps (L7 features). NLB for extreme performance, static IP, TCP (L4). Route 53 failover vs Global Accelerator: both do failover but GA is faster (anycast). 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Dynamic user-specific content should not be cached. Setting TTL to 0 or Cache-Control: no-cache ensures CloudFront always fetches from the origin for these requests. Forwarding differentiating headers/cookies creates separate cache entries per user, preventing cross-user contamination even if some caching is desired. Signed URLs control access but don't prevent caching. Separate distributions help organization but don't prevent caching.

q: An application uses Amazon RDS MySQL and the performance team observes high IOPS with many small random reads. The data access pattern shows repeated access to a small subset of records. What is the most targeted optimization?
options: Use Aurora instead of RDS MySQL for better IOPS
Enable RDS caching using Amazon ElastiCache — cache the hot small subset of records in memory to eliminate repeated disk I/O
Increase RDS IOPS allocation
Add read replicas to distribute the IOPS
explain: When a small subset of records is accessed repeatedly, in-memory caching with ElastiCache eliminates the disk I/O entirely for those hot records. This is more cost-effective than increasing IOPS allocation or adding replicas that still hit disk. Aurora provides better IOPS than RDS MySQL but the core issue (repeated disk reads for hot data) is better solved with application-level caching.

q: A company uses Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose to deliver streaming data to S3. Data arrives in small batches. Athena queries on the S3 data are slow because millions of small files are created. What Firehose configuration improves Athena query performance?
options: Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering on the destination bucket
Increase Kinesis Data Streams shard count

Configure Kinesis Data Firehose with dynamic partitioning and set the buffer size to 128 MB and buffer interval to 300 seconds — larger buffers create fewer, larger files that Athena queries more efficiently
Use Kinesis Data Analytics instead of Firehose
explain: Kinesis Firehose buffers records before writing to S3. Larger buffer size (up to 128MB) and longer buffer interval (up to 900 seconds) combine records into larger files. Athena performs better with fewer large files than many small files due to S3 API call overhead and file-per-task overhead in query execution. Dynamic partitioning routes data to appropriate S3 prefixes. Shard count affects Kinesis Streams, not Firehose.

q: A company's application on EC2 needs to download the same 10 GB reference dataset from S3 every time a new instance launches. This adds 15 minutes to startup time. What is the most effective optimization?
options: Increase EC2 network bandwidth by using an enhanced networking instance type

q: A company runs a multiplayer game server on EC2. Players experience high latency when connecting from different continents. The game requires TCP connections. What AWS service improves connection latency for global players?
options: CloudFront — it supports TCP game traffic
AWS Global Accelerator — routes TCP/UDP traffic through AWS's global network backbone to the optimal AWS region edge, reducing latency by 60% for global users
Route 53 latency routing to the nearest region
Direct Connect from all player locations
explain: AWS Global Accelerator uses the AWS global network backbone instead of the public internet, providing consistent low-latency routing to the nearest AWS edge location, then high-speed routing to the game servers. It supports TCP and UDP (unlike CloudFront which is HTTP/S). Route 53 latency routing directs DNS resolution but doesn't route traffic over the AWS backbone. Direct Connect is for enterprise on-premises connections, not consumer game players.
