q: A company uses Amazon SQS. Consumers process messages in 1 second. The team wants maximum throughput for the consumer fleet. What SQS configuration maximizes throughput?
options: Use SQS FIFO for better throughput
Use long polling (WaitTimeSeconds=20) with ReceiveMessage MaxNumberOfMessages=10 — this retrieves up to 10 messages per API call efficiently and reduces empty responses
Create many small SQS queues and use fan-out
Use long polling with ReceiveMessage WaitTimeSeconds set to 0 (short polling)
explain: Long polling reduces empty responses (when the queue is empty, it waits up to 20 seconds for a message instead of returning immediately). Requesting up to 10 messages per API call reduces API call overhead and maximizes per-call throughput. Short polling wastes API calls on empty responses. FIFO queues have lower maximum throughput than Standard queues. Multiple queues add consumer complexity.

Shared file access (Windows/SMB) → FSx for Windows. HPC/ML training high throughput → FSx for Lustre. Block storage (single EC2) → EBS. Object storage → S3. EBS IOPS tier: gp3 (baseline 3000 IOPS) → io2 (64000 IOPS, <0.1ms). S3 cost tier: Standard → IA (30d min) → Glacier Instant (quarterly) → Glacier Flexible (90d min) → Deep Archive (180d min, cheapest). Access pattern known → Lifecycle. Access pattern unknown → Intelligent-Tiering. Transfer Acceleration = faster UPLOAD to S3 (not for serving downloads → use CloudFront). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Long polling reduces empty responses (when the queue is empty, it waits up to 20 seconds for a message instead of returning immediately). Requesting up to 10 messages per API call reduces API call overhead and maximizes per-call throughput. Short polling wastes API calls on empty responses. FIFO queues have lower maximum throughput than Standard queues. Multiple queues add consumer complexity.

q: A company stores 1 petabyte of log data in Amazon S3 in Apache Parquet format partitioned by date. They want to enable data analysts to run ad-hoc SQL queries with response times under 10 seconds for typical queries. What should the architect recommend?
options: Use Amazon Athena with AWS Glue Data Catalog — Parquet columnar format with date partitioning enables fast, cost-efficient queries with Athena's serverless MPP engine
Load all data into RDS Aurora for SQL access
Use Amazon Redshift with COPY command to load all 1 PB
Deploy an Apache Spark cluster on EMR for ad-hoc queries
explain: Athena with Parquet + date partitions is ideal for ad-hoc queries on petabyte-scale S3 data. Parquet's columnar format allows column pruning. Date partitions allow partition pruning. Athena's serverless MPP executes queries in seconds without cluster management. Loading 1 PB into Redshift takes significant time and cost. EMR Spark handles complex queries but requires cluster provisioning. Aurora isn't designed for petabyte-scale analytical workloads.
hint: ⚡ Domain 3 (24%): Design High-Performing Architectures. Key task statements: 3.1 Storage (EBS/EFS/S3 tiers), 3.2 Compute (EC2 types/placement groups/serverless), 3.3 Database (caching/read scaling/OLAP), 3.4 Network (CloudFront/Global Accelerator/Route 53). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: large offline transfer → Snow Family (Snowball/Edge/Mobile). 📋 Task 3.2 — High-performing and elastic compute solutions: Compute selection: No server management, event-driven, ≤15 min → Lambda. Containers, no cluster mgmt → Fargate (ECS or EKS). Containers with control of nodes → ECS/EKS on EC2. Batch jobs, retry, array → AWS Batch. PaaS (deploy code, AWS handles infra) → Elastic Beanstalk. Network performance: Cluster PG = lowest latency (HPC). EFA = MPI/HPC workloads needing RDMA. Instance type: memory-heavy → R family. Compute → C family. GPU/ML → P/G/Inf. 💡 Serverless = no server management, pay per use, auto-scale. Lambda limits: 15 min max, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, 1000 concurrent (soft). Cold start: Lambda function first invocation takes longer. Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm. Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Athena with Parquet + date partitions is ideal for ad-hoc queries on petabyte-scale S3 data. Parquet's columnar format allows column pruning. Date partitions allow partition pruning. Athena's serverless MPP executes queries in seconds without cluster management. Loading 1 PB into Redshift takes significant time and cost. EMR Spark handles complex queries but requires cluster provisioning. Aurora isn't designed for petabyte-scale analytical workloads.

q: A company's Lambda functions write logs to CloudWatch Logs. As the application scales, logging becomes expensive. Detailed DEBUG logs are needed only during incidents. How should they optimize logging costs?

q: A company serves an API used by mobile apps. 95% of requests are for data that changes less than once per minute. API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB latency is 150ms average. They want to reduce this to under 20ms for the majority of requests. What is the most effective optimization?
options: Enable DynamoDB DAX to reduce database latency
Use Lambda Provisioned Concurrency
Switch from Lambda to EC2 for the API backend
Implement caching at the API Gateway level (Stage cache) or use CloudFront in front of API Gateway with a short TTL — serve cached responses in <10ms for the 95% of requests to unchanging data
explain: API Gateway caching or CloudFront caching serves cached responses in <10ms without invoking Lambda or querying DynamoDB. For data that changes less than once per minute, a 60-second TTL serves 95% of requests from cache. This provides 150ms → <10ms improvement for cached responses. Provisioned Concurrency reduces cold starts but still invokes Lambda (100+ ms). DAX reduces DynamoDB latency but still involves Lambda execution.

q: A company's application reads a configuration file from S3 on every Lambda invocation (100,000 invocations/hour). The config changes at most once per day. This adds 50ms latency per invocation. What optimization should they implement?
options: Use AWS AppConfig for configuration management
Store the config in Lambda environment variables (up to 4 KB)

Load the configuration file once in the Lambda initialization phase (outside the handler) and cache it in a module-level variable — subsequent warm invocations reuse the cached config without S3 reads
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration for faster config reads
explain: Lambda's execution environment persists between warm invocations. Initializing the config in the global scope means the S3 read happens only once per execution environment (typically lasting hours). The handler reuses the cached config on warm invocations. For configs ≤4KB, environment variables work but require Lambda redeployment to update. AppConfig is the enterprise solution with hot-swapping but adds complexity. S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds downloads but doesn't eliminate them.

q: A company runs a high-traffic website with a CDN. During a flash sale, the CDN has a low cache hit ratio because each product URL includes a unique session identifier in the query string. What should they fix?
options: Use Lambda@Edge to strip session IDs before caching
Increase CloudFront cache TTL to 24 hours

Configure CloudFront to not cache the session-specific query string parameter (use cache policy that ignores session_id) while still caching other query strings — cache key contains only relevant parameters
Disable query string forwarding entirely in CloudFront
explain: The session ID in the query string creates a unique cache key per user, causing every request to miss the cache. Configuring CloudFront's cache policy to exclude the session_id query parameter from the cache key allows identical product page content to be cached and served to all users regardless of their session ID. The session_id can still be forwarded to the origin separately if needed.

q: A data engineering team needs to run complex ETL jobs that join datasets from S3, RDS, and DynamoDB. Jobs should run on a schedule without managing servers. What AWS service is most appropriate?
options: AWS Lambda with custom ETL code
AWS Glue — serverless ETL service that auto-generates PySpark scripts, has built-in connectors for S3/RDS/DynamoDB, and runs on managed Spark infrastructure with automatic scaling
AWS Data Pipeline
Amazon EMR with permanent cluster
explain: AWS Glue is a serverless ETL service that handles the ETL heavy lifting: schema discovery (Crawlers), code generation (PySpark), and execution on managed Spark infrastructure. It natively connects to S3, RDS, DynamoDB, and other sources. Lambda's 15-minute timeout is insufficient for complex ETL. EMR requires cluster management. AWS Data Pipeline is older and less feature-rich than Glue for modern ETL.

q: A company's API Gateway is processing 1 million requests/day. 30% of these are failed requests (4xx/5xx errors) from bots and scrapers. This wastes Lambda execution costs. What reduces these wasteful invocations?
options: Configure AWS WAF with bot control managed rules in front of API Gateway — block known bot traffic before it reaches the API, eliminating Lambda invocations for bot requests
Add request validation in Lambda to reject bad requests early
Deploy API Gateway with VPC link to prevent external access
Enable API Gateway throttling with usage plans and API keys
explain: AWS WAF with Bot Control managed rules identifies and blocks known bot traffic at the API Gateway perimeter, before Lambda is invoked. This eliminates Lambda costs for the 30% bot traffic. Request validation in Lambda still invokes (and charges for) Lambda before rejecting. API key throttling controls legitimate traffic patterns. VPC link restricts to VPC traffic, blocking legitimate external users.

Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? q: A company needs to synchronize files between on-premises storage and Amazon S3 on a scheduled basis. Files can be hundreds of gigabytes. They need efficient incremental sync (only changed files). What service is designed for this?

AWS Database Migration Service (DMS)
AWS Snowball Edge for each scheduled sync
AWS DataSync — purpose-built for high-speed data transfer with automatic incremental detection (file modification time and checksum), parallel transfer, and network optimization
explain: AWS DataSync handles scheduled data transfers with automatic change detection (modification time, checksum), parallel multi-threaded transfer, bandwidth throttling, and task scheduling. It's significantly faster than S3 CLI sync due to parallel multi-connection transfers. DMS is for database migrations. Snowball is for one-time large offline transfers, not recurring scheduled syncs.

q: A company has a stable, predictable workload running on 20 EC2 On-Demand instances 24/7. They have been running this workload for 2 years and plan to continue for 3 more years. What is the most cost-effective pricing option?
options: Use Spot Instances for 90% savings
Purchase 3-year Reserved Instances (All Upfront) — provides up to 72% discount over On-Demand for predictable, steady-state workloads
Purchase Savings Plans with a 1-year commitment
Continue with On-Demand pricing
explain: 3-year All Upfront Reserved Instances provide the maximum discount (up to 72%) for predictable, long-running workloads. For a 5-year total run, 3-year RIs are appropriate. Spot Instances can be interrupted — unsuitable for mission-critical steady-state workloads. 1-year Savings Plans provide ~40-60% savings, less than 3-year RIs. On-Demand is the most expensive option for predictable workloads.

Variable but consistent spend → Compute Savings Plans (most flexible). Fault-tolerant batch, stateless, resumable → Spot (90% off). Variable/unknown traffic → On-Demand (no commitment). Infrequent event-driven → Lambda (pay per invocation, zero idle cost). Rightsizing before purchasing commitments: Compute Optimizer → rightsizing → THEN buy RI/SP. Key rule: spot = interruptible only. Do NOT use Spot for: databases, web servers, stateful apps. ❌ Eliminate distractors: 3-year All Upfront Reserved Instances provide the maximum discount (up to 72%) for predictable, long-running workloads. For a 5-year total run, 3-year RIs are appropriate. Spot Instances can be interrupted — unsuitable for mission-critical steady-state workloads. 1-year Savings Plans provide ~40-60% savings, less than 3-year RIs. On-Demand is the most expensive option for predictable workloads.

q: A company stores 500 TB of data in S3 Standard. Analysis shows 90% of the data hasn't been accessed in over 90 days. What is the most cost-effective S3 change?
options: Implement S3 Lifecycle policies to automatically transition objects to S3 Standard-IA after 30 days and S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval after 90 days, reducing storage costs by up to 80%
Delete the data older than 90 days to save storage costs
Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering on all objects
Move all data to a single S3 Glacier archive immediately
explain: S3 Lifecycle policies automate the transition to cheaper storage classes based on age. Standard-IA costs ~58% less than Standard with retrieval fees. Glacier costs ~83% less. For 90% inactive data, lifecycle policies dramatically reduce costs. S3 Intelligent-Tiering is ideal for unpredictable access patterns but has monitoring fees per object. Immediate Glacier migration means slow retrieval for the 10% that might be accessed. Deleting data may violate retention requirements.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: predictable 24/7 → Reserved Instances or Savings Plans | cost optimization → match pricing model to workload type | cold/archive data → Glacier or Deep Archive (cheapest storage) | access pattern by age → S3 Lifecycle policy. 📋 Task 4.1 — Cost-optimized storage solutions: Cost-optimized storage = match storage class to access frequency. Know the access pattern? → Lifecycle policies (explicit transitions). Unknown/unpredictable access? → Intelligent-Tiering (auto-moves, has monitoring fee). Never accessed after 7 years? → Deep Archive + expiration rule. EBS cost tip: gp3 = same price as gp2 but you tune IOPS/throughput → always migrate gp2 → gp3. S3 retrieval fee alert: Glacier/IA have per-GB retrieval fees — important for frequently-read data. Data not accessed but must keep 7 years → Lifecycle to Deep Archive (compliance). 💡 Encryption at rest: who controls the key? SSE-S3 = AWS controls everything. SSE-KMS (AWS key) = KMS controls, you can see CloudTrail. SSE-KMS (CMK) = YOU control key policy, rotation, who can use it. Best for compliance. SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. ACM provides free certs for AWS services. RDS encryption: must be enabled at creation. To encrypt existing → snapshot + copy as encrypted + restore. S3 Object Lock: WORM. Compliance mode = no one (not even root) can delete before retention period. 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. ❌ Eliminate distractors: S3 Lifecycle policies automate the transition to cheaper storage classes based on age. Standard-IA costs ~58% less than Standard with retrieval fees. Glacier costs ~83% less. For 90% inactive data, lifecycle policies dramatically reduce costs. S3 Intelligent-Tiering is ideal for unpredictable access patterns but has monitoring fees per object. Immediate Glacier migration means slow retrieval for the 10% that might be accessed. Deleting data may violate retention requirements.

q: A company uses Amazon S3 to store application logs. The logs must be retained for 7 years for compliance, but are only actively analyzed in the first 30 days. After 1 year, they are rarely retrieved. What lifecycle policy minimizes cost?
options: Transition logs to S3 Standard-IA after 30 days, to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days, to S3 Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year — Deep Archive is the lowest-cost long-term storage
Compress logs with Lambda and store in a single S3 object
Store all logs in S3 Standard for 7 years
Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering for all 7 years — it auto-optimizes storage class
explain: This lifecycle policy matches storage class to access frequency: Standard for hot access (0-30 days), Standard-IA for occasional access (30-90 days), Glacier Instant for rare access (90 days-1 year), Glacier Deep Archive for archival (1-7 years). Deep Archive ($0.00099/GB/month) is 95% cheaper than Standard. Intelligent-Tiering doesn't tier to Deep Archive automatically and has per-object monitoring fees that may exceed savings for small objects.

Never accessed after 7 years? → Deep Archive + expiration rule. EBS cost tip: gp3 = same price as gp2 but you tune IOPS/throughput → always migrate gp2 → gp3. S3 retrieval fee alert: Glacier/IA have per-GB retrieval fees — important for frequently-read data. Data not accessed but must keep 7 years → Lifecycle to Deep Archive (compliance). 💡 Serverless = no server management, pay per use, auto-scale. Lambda limits: 15 min max, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, 1000 concurrent (soft). Cold start: Lambda function first invocation takes longer. Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm. Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: This lifecycle policy matches storage class to access frequency: Standard for hot access (0-30 days), Standard-IA for occasional access (30-90 days), Glacier Instant for rare access (90 days-1 year), Glacier Deep Archive for archival (1-7 years). Deep Archive ($0.00099/GB/month) is 95% cheaper than Standard. Intelligent-Tiering doesn't tier to Deep Archive automatically and has per-object monitoring fees that may exceed savings for small objects.

q: A startup runs a development/test environment with EC2 instances that are only used 8 hours per day during business hours (Monday–Friday). What is the most cost-effective way to manage these instances?
options: Use AWS Instance Scheduler or Lambda + EventBridge to automatically stop instances after hours and on weekends — reduces running hours by ~75%
Use Spot Instances for dev/test workloads
Reduce instance sizes to minimize cost
Purchase Reserved Instances for the dev environment
explain: Stopping instances when not in use (8 hours/day × 5 days = 40 hours/week vs 168 hours) reduces running costs by ~76%. EC2 charges only for running hours. Instance Scheduler automates start/stop. For dev/test, this is the highest-impact change. Spot Instances also work well for dev/test (and can be combined with scheduling). RIs for dev environments are wasteful — you pay 24/7 for instances only used 24% of the time.

Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Stopping instances when not in use (8 hours/day × 5 days = 40 hours/week vs 168 hours) reduces running costs by ~76%. EC2 charges only for running hours. Instance Scheduler automates start/stop. For dev/test, this is the highest-impact change. Spot Instances also work well for dev/test (and can be combined with scheduling). RIs for dev environments are wasteful — you pay 24/7 for instances only used 24% of the time.

q: A company's data warehouse queries run complex analytics on historical data in Amazon Redshift. Cluster utilization is under 20% most of the time but spikes to 80% for 2 hours each morning when reports run. What is the most cost-optimized architecture?
options: Use a fixed large cluster 24/7 and accept the idle cost
Right-size the cluster to handle peak load 24/7
Use Redshift concurrency scaling (additional clusters auto-added during peak) and consider Redshift Serverless for variable workloads — pay only for compute used during queries
Schedule all queries to run at the same time to maximize cluster utilization
explain: Redshift concurrency scaling automatically adds cluster capacity during peak demand and charges only for the additional burst capacity. Redshift Serverless charges per RPU-hour during query execution, ideal for variable workloads with long idle periods. A fixed large cluster pays 24/7 for 80% peak but runs at 20% most of the time. Bunching all queries removes scheduling flexibility.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: repeated reads → add caching layer (ElastiCache or DAX) | predictable 24/7 → Reserved Instances or Savings Plans. 📋 Task 4.3 — Cost-optimized database solutions: DB cost: match pricing model to traffic pattern. Variable DB load → Aurora Serverless v2 or DynamoDB On-Demand. Predictable DB load + long-term → RDS Reserved Instances or DynamoDB Provisioned. DynamoDB provisioned = cheaper per request than on-demand (if traffic is predictable). Redshift Spectrum = extend Redshift to query cold S3 data without loading (avoids extra storage cost). 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 Serverless = no server management, pay per use, auto-scale. Lambda limits: 15 min max, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, 1000 concurrent (soft). Cold start: Lambda function first invocation takes longer. Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm. Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Redshift concurrency scaling automatically adds cluster capacity during peak demand and charges only for the additional burst capacity. Redshift Serverless charges per RPU-hour during query execution, ideal for variable workloads with long idle periods. A fixed large cluster pays 24/7 for 80% peak but runs at 20% most of the time. Bunching all queries removes scheduling flexibility.

q: A company migrated to AWS and their data transfer costs are higher than expected. Analysis shows EC2 instances in us-east-1 are transferring large amounts of data to EC2 instances in eu-west-1. What architecture change reduces this cost?
options: Use Direct Connect for all inter-region traffic
Use VPC peering instead of the public internet for inter-region traffic
Enable VPC Flow Logs to understand traffic patterns before making changes
Redesign the application to minimize cross-region data transfer — colocate services that exchange large amounts of data in the same region or use regional deployments with eventual synchronization
explain: The most effective cost reduction is architectural: minimize the amount of data that crosses regions. Colocating tightly coupled services in the same region eliminates inter-region transfer costs. VPC peering and Direct Connect reduce transfer per-GB cost slightly for approved traffic but don't eliminate the volume. Flow Logs provide visibility (useful first step) but VPC peering doesn't change the fundamental cost model for inter-region traffic.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: NAT Gateway cost → replace with VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3/DynamoDB (free) | decoupling → SQS (queue), SNS (pub/sub), EventBridge (events). 📋 Task 4.2 — Cost-optimized compute solutions: Compute cost decision matrix: Workload type → pricing model: Predictable 24/7 + 1-3 year → Reserved Instance or EC2 Savings Plan (72% off). Variable but consistent spend → Compute Savings Plans (most flexible). Fault-tolerant batch, stateless, resumable → Spot (90% off). Variable/unknown traffic → On-Demand (no commitment). Infrequent event-driven → Lambda (pay per invocation, zero idle cost). Rightsizing before purchasing commitments: Compute Optimizer → rightsizing → THEN buy RI/SP. Key rule: spot = interruptible only. Do NOT use Spot for: databases, web servers, stateful apps. 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. 💡 Multiple VPC connectivity: Few VPCs (≤3) → VPC Peering (simple, free). Many VPCs → Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke, centralised routing). On-prem to AWS: Need encryption + speed → DX + VPN over DX. Need encryption + cheap → Site-to-Site VPN. Need high bandwidth + consistent + NOT encrypted → Direct Connect alone. Expose a service to another VPC without full access → PrivateLink (not peering). DNS on-prem → VPC: Route 53 Resolver inbound/outbound endpoints. ❌ Eliminate distractors: The most effective cost reduction is architectural: minimize the amount of data that crosses regions. Colocating tightly coupled services in the same region eliminates inter-region transfer costs. VPC peering and Direct Connect reduce transfer per-GB cost slightly for approved traffic but don't eliminate the volume. Flow Logs provide visibility (useful first step) but VPC peering doesn't change the fundamental cost model for inter-region traffic.

q: A company is using Amazon RDS MySQL with a db.r5.8xlarge instance (64 vCPUs, 256 GB RAM). CloudWatch metrics show average CPU at 8% and memory at 30%. What is the recommended action?
options: Add read replicas to handle the excess capacity
Downsize the RDS instance — use AWS Compute Optimizer or RDS Performance Insights to identify the optimal instance size based on actual usage patterns, potentially saving 60-80% on the RDS cost
Keep the current instance size for future growth
Enable RDS Multi-AZ — it distributes the load and reduces per-instance cost
explain: An instance at 8% CPU and 30% memory is significantly over-provisioned. Downsizing to a db.r5.xlarge or db.r5.2xlarge based on actual workload saves 60-80% on RDS costs. AWS Compute Optimizer and RDS recommendations provide specific sizing guidance. Multi-AZ doubles the cost. Read replicas add cost, not reduce it. Future growth justification should be based on growth projections, not raw fear of running out.

q: A company wants to reduce their Amazon CloudWatch costs. They have 500 EC2 instances with detailed monitoring (1-minute metrics) enabled. Most instances are stable and don't require 1-minute granularity. What should they do?
options: Switch stable instances from detailed monitoring (1-minute) to basic monitoring (5-minute) — basic monitoring is free for EC2 instances, while detailed monitoring charges per metric
Disable all CloudWatch monitoring to save costs
Use AWS X-Ray instead of CloudWatch metrics
Export CloudWatch metrics to S3 and delete them from CloudWatch
explain: EC2 basic monitoring (5-minute intervals) is free and included in EC2 pricing. Detailed monitoring (1-minute intervals) costs $0.30 per instance per month. With 500 instances, switching stable ones to basic monitoring saves hundreds of dollars monthly. Disabling monitoring removes operational visibility. S3 export doesn't reduce CloudWatch charges. X-Ray is for application tracing, not infrastructure metrics.

q: A company runs a serverless application with Lambda and DynamoDB. Lambda costs are higher than expected. AWS Cost Explorer shows high GB-seconds usage. What should they analyze first?
options: Switch all Lambda functions to ARM (Graviton2) architecture — 20% cheaper and often faster
Reduce the number of Lambda functions
Increase Lambda function timeout to allow more processing per invocation
Analyze Lambda memory allocation vs actual memory used (AWS Lambda Power Tuning or CloudWatch memory metrics) — right-size each function to the minimum memory that meets performance requirements
explain: Lambda Graviton2 (ARM) architecture provides up to 20% better price-performance than x86 for most workloads with a simple architecture flag change. Combined with right-sizing memory (overprovisioned Lambda memory directly increases GB-seconds cost), these two changes typically reduce Lambda costs significantly. Reducing function count changes architecture. Increasing timeout increases GB-seconds cost. Right-sizing is important but Graviton2 is a quick win with minimal effort.

Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Lambda Graviton2 (ARM) architecture provides up to 20% better price-performance than x86 for most workloads with a simple architecture flag change. Combined with right-sizing memory (overprovisioned Lambda memory directly increases GB-seconds cost), these two changes typically reduce Lambda costs significantly. Reducing function count changes architecture. Increasing timeout increases GB-seconds cost. Right-sizing is important but Graviton2 is a quick win with minimal effort.

q: A company stores large video files in Amazon S3 Standard. After 90 days, videos are rarely accessed but must be retrieved within 12 hours when needed. What S3 storage class is most cost-effective for this requirement?
options: S3 Standard-IA — lower cost than Standard with millisecond retrieval
S3 Glacier Deep Archive — lowest cost but 12-48 hour retrieval
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval — up to 90% cheaper than Standard with retrieval times of 1 minute to 12 hours depending on retrieval type selected
S3 One Zone-IA — cheapest option for infrequent access
explain: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval (formerly S3 Glacier) costs ~83% less than Standard and supports Bulk retrieval in 5-12 hours and Standard retrieval in 3-5 hours — both meeting the 12-hour requirement. Glacier Deep Archive's minimum retrieval time is 12 hours (Bulk) which barely meets the requirement but Standard retrieval is 48 hours. Standard-IA is more expensive than Glacier and appropriate for 1-4x per month access. One Zone-IA loses an AZ but still costs more than Glacier.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: cost optimization → match pricing model to workload type | cold/archive data → Glacier or Deep Archive (cheapest storage). 📋 Task 4.1 — Cost-optimized storage solutions: Cost-optimized storage = match storage class to access frequency. Know the access pattern? → Lifecycle policies (explicit transitions). Unknown/unpredictable access? → Intelligent-Tiering (auto-moves, has monitoring fee). Never accessed after 7 years? → Deep Archive + expiration rule. EBS cost tip: gp3 = same price as gp2 but you tune IOPS/throughput → always migrate gp2 → gp3. S3 retrieval fee alert: Glacier/IA have per-GB retrieval fees — important for frequently-read data. Data not accessed but must keep 7 years → Lifecycle to Deep Archive (compliance). 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval (formerly S3 Glacier) costs ~83% less than Standard and supports Bulk retrieval in 5-12 hours and Standard retrieval in 3-5 hours — both meeting the 12-hour requirement. Glacier Deep Archive's minimum retrieval time is 12 hours (Bulk) which barely meets the requirement but Standard retrieval is 48 hours. Standard-IA is more expensive than Glacier and appropriate for 1-4x per month access. One Zone-IA loses an AZ but still costs more than Glacier.

q: A company uses multiple AWS accounts under AWS Organizations. They purchase Reserved Instances in the master payer account. Which accounts benefit from the RI discounts?
options: Discounts must be manually transferred to member accounts
Only accounts in the same OU as the purchasing account
Only the account where the RI was purchased
RI discounts apply across all accounts in the organization by default (RI sharing is enabled) — any account in the organization using matching EC2 instances receives the discounted rate
explain: AWS Organizations enables RI sharing by default. Reserved Instance and Savings Plans discounts automatically apply to matching usage across all member accounts in the organization. This allows central purchasing in a master account to benefit all member accounts. RI sharing can be disabled per account if needed. There is no manual transfer process — it's automatic.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 📋 Task 4.2 — Cost-optimized compute solutions: Compute cost decision matrix: Workload type → pricing model: Predictable 24/7 + 1-3 year → Reserved Instance or EC2 Savings Plan (72% off). Variable but consistent spend → Compute Savings Plans (most flexible). Fault-tolerant batch, stateless, resumable → Spot (90% off). Variable/unknown traffic → On-Demand (no commitment). Infrequent event-driven → Lambda (pay per invocation, zero idle cost). Rightsizing before purchasing commitments: Compute Optimizer → rightsizing → THEN buy RI/SP. Key rule: spot = interruptible only. Do NOT use Spot for: databases, web servers, stateful apps. 💡 IAM policy evaluation: deny wins always. Order: Explicit Deny > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary > session policy. Cross-account access: Role with trust policy + AssumeRole. OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. Permission boundary = limits what a role/user can do even if their policy grants more. AWS RAM = share VPCs, subnets, Transit Gateways across accounts (no peering required). ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS Organizations enables RI sharing by default. Reserved Instance and Savings Plans discounts automatically apply to matching usage across all member accounts in the organization. This allows central purchasing in a master account to benefit all member accounts. RI sharing can be disabled per account if needed. There is no manual transfer process — it's automatic.

q: A company is deciding between Amazon Aurora Serverless v2 and provisioned Aurora for a new application with unpredictable usage (idle most of the day with random spikes). What is the cost consideration?
options: Aurora Serverless v2 is more cost-effective for variable workloads — it scales to 0.5 ACU minimum when idle (vs. provisioned instances that charge even when idle), while scaling instantly to handle spikes
Aurora Serverless v2 and provisioned Aurora cost the same per hour
Provisioned Aurora always costs less than Serverless
Aurora Serverless v1 is cheaper than Serverless v2 for all use cases
explain: Aurora Serverless v2 bills per ACU-hour and scales down to 0.5 ACU minimum during idle periods, significantly reducing idle costs compared to provisioned instances (which charge the full instance hourly rate). Provisioned instances are more cost-effective for predictable high-utilization workloads.

q: A company wants to optimize their AWS data transfer costs. They have EC2 instances downloading data from an S3 bucket in the same region. What is the cost impact?
options: Data transfer between S3 and EC2 costs $0.01/GB in the same region
All S3 data egress is charged at $0.09/GB regardless of destination
S3 to EC2 data transfer in the same region costs the standard data transfer rate
Data transfer from S3 to EC2 instances in the same AWS region is free — there is no data transfer charge for intra-region S3 to EC2 transfers
explain: AWS does not charge for data transfer between Amazon S3 and Amazon EC2 instances within the same region. This is a key cost optimization — colocating your compute and storage in the same region eliminates transfer charges. Cross-region S3 to EC2 transfer is charged. Outbound to the internet from S3 is charged at $0.09/GB for the first 10 TB.

q: A company receives a large AWS bill driven by NAT Gateway processing fees. Their EC2 instances in private subnets are frequently accessing S3 and DynamoDB. What architecture change eliminates these NAT Gateway charges?
options: Create VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB — traffic routes directly from the VPC to these services without going through the NAT Gateway, eliminating NAT processing fees for this traffic
Switch from NAT Gateway to a NAT Instance to reduce costs
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration to bypass NAT Gateway

explain: VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB are free (no endpoint charges) and route traffic directly from the VPC to the service without going through the NAT Gateway. q: A company is running a production workload on ec2 m5.4xlarge instances (16 vCPU / 64 GB). They're considering migrating to AWS Graviton (m7g.4xlarge — 16 vCPU / 64 GB). What is the expected cost benefit?
options: Graviton (ARM) requires complete application re-architecture for all workloads

AWS Graviton instances provide comparable or better performance at approximately 20% lower cost — m7g is typically 20-40% cheaper than equivalent x86 instances with similar or better performance for many workloads
Graviton instances are only cheaper for Lambda, not EC2
Graviton instances cost more but provide better performance
explain: AWS Graviton3 (m7g) instances provide 20-40% better price-performance than equivalent x86 instances. Most modern application workloads (Java, Python, Node.js, .NET, Go, Ruby) run on ARM without code changes. Application binaries may need recompilation but not re-architecture. Lambda Graviton is also cheaper. Cost savings typically justify migration testing for any significant EC2 spend.

q: A company uses Amazon Kinesis Data Streams and is paying for 24 shards that handle daily peak traffic. At night, traffic drops to 10% of peak. Shard costs are charged per shard-hour regardless of traffic. What reduces costs?
options: Use SQS instead of Kinesis to avoid shard costs
Delete and recreate the stream daily based on traffic
Reduce the number of shards to 3 to cut costs
Use Kinesis Data Streams on-demand mode — it automatically scales shards with traffic and charges per GB ingested instead of per shard-hour, saving cost during low-traffic periods
explain: Kinesis on-demand mode eliminates shard management and charges per GB ingested rather than per shard-hour. For streams with 10:1 peak-to-off-peak ratios, on-demand can be significantly cheaper than 24 idle shards at night. Reducing to 3 shards saves cost but causes throttling at peak. SQS migration changes the streaming architecture fundamentally. Delete/recreate is operationally complex and risks downtime.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 📋 Task 4.3 — Cost-optimized database solutions: DB cost: match pricing model to traffic pattern. Variable DB load → Aurora Serverless v2 or DynamoDB On-Demand. Predictable DB load + long-term → RDS Reserved Instances or DynamoDB Provisioned. DynamoDB provisioned = cheaper per request than on-demand (if traffic is predictable). Redshift Spectrum = extend Redshift to query cold S3 data without loading (avoids extra storage cost). 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Kinesis on-demand mode eliminates shard management and charges per GB ingested rather than per shard-hour. For streams with 10:1 peak-to-off-peak ratios, on-demand can be significantly cheaper than 24 idle shards at night. Reducing to 3 shards saves cost but causes throttling at peak. SQS migration changes the streaming architecture fundamentally. Delete/recreate is operationally complex and risks downtime.

q: A company is building a new application and wants to choose the most cost-effective AWS database service for a relational workload with read-heavy access patterns. The workload has predictable traffic and requires 99.99% availability. Which database configuration minimizes cost?
options: Amazon Aurora MySQL with Aurora Replicas — Aurora provides better price-performance than RDS, and Aurora Replicas serve both reads and failover (HA), combining the functions of Read Replicas and Multi-AZ standby
Amazon DynamoDB — it's always cheaper than RDS for relational workloads
Amazon RDS Multi-AZ for high availability with Read Replicas for reads
Amazon RDS on dedicated instances for better per-unit pricing
explain: Aurora MySQL provides up to 5x better performance than RDS MySQL at a similar price point. Aurora Replicas can serve reads AND provide automatic failover (replacing the need for a separate Multi-AZ standby), giving you HA + read scaling in one. RDS Multi-AZ + Read Replicas requires separate standby and replica instances. DynamoDB is NoSQL and not suitable for relational workloads. Dedicated instances are more expensive.

Do NOT use Spot for: databases, web servers, stateful apps. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Aurora MySQL provides up to 5x better performance than RDS MySQL at a similar price point. q: A company's AWS bill shows significant charges for CloudWatch Logs storage. They have hundreds of log groups with no retention policy set (default: indefinite retention). What is the fastest cost reduction action?
options: Disable CloudWatch Logs for all services
Export all logs to S3 and then delete from CloudWatch

Set CloudWatch Logs retention policies on all log groups to delete logs after a defined period (e.g., 30-90 days) — logs beyond the retention period are automatically deleted, eliminating storage charges
Compress log entries using a Lambda function
explain: CloudWatch Logs without retention policies accumulate indefinitely at $0.03/GB/month storage. Setting retention to 30-90 days (depending on compliance requirements) automatically deletes old logs, significantly reducing storage costs. This is a quick, low-risk action. Exporting to S3 is reasonable for long-term compliance storage (S3 is cheaper) but takes time to implement. Disabling logs removes operational visibility. Compression reduces size but logs still accumulate indefinitely.

q: A company wants to reduce costs for their Amazon EC2 workloads. AWS Cost Explorer shows 60% of their EC2 spend is on On-Demand instances that have been running continuously for 8+ months. What is the recommended action?
options: Purchase Compute Savings Plans — they provide up to 66% savings, apply automatically across any instance family, size, OS, or region unlike standard RIs, offering flexibility for changing instance types
Convert all instances to Spot Instances
Purchase 3-year No Upfront Reserved Instances
Increase instance sizes to get volume discounts
explain: Compute Savings Plans apply automatically to any EC2 usage regardless of instance family, size, region, or OS. They provide up to 66% savings with a 1 or 3-year commitment. Standard RIs are locked to specific instance type and region. Savings Plans provide similar savings with more flexibility — if you change instance types, the savings plan still applies. q: A company needs to process large genomics files stored in S3. Each file is 100 GB. The processing is done by an AWS Lambda function. The Lambda function needs access to the file contents. What is the cost-optimized approach to avoid downloading the entire file for partial processing?
options: Mount EFS in Lambda and copy the S3 file to EFS for faster access
Use S3 Select to retrieve only the relevant subset of data from the large file using SQL expressions, reducing data transfer and Lambda execution time
Download the entire 100 GB file to Lambda /tmp (10 GB max — this won't work)
Use S3 Batch Operations to process all files simultaneously
explain: S3 Select retrieves only the data matching a SQL expression from within an object, reducing data scanned and transferred by up to 80%. This is ideal for processing specific fields or rows from large files. Lambda /tmp storage is limited to 10 GB (10,240 MB) — insufficient for a 100 GB file. S3 Batch Operations is for running Lambda across many S3 objects, not filtering within objects. EFS mounting adds complexity and cost.

Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: NAT Gateway cost → replace with VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3/DynamoDB (free). 📋 Task 4.4 — Cost-optimized network architectures: Network cost: data transfer is the hidden cost driver. Key insight: NAT Gateway data processing fee ($0.045/GB) accumulates fast for S3 access from private subnet → replace with VPC Gateway Endpoint (FREE). Cross-AZ charges = $0.01/GB each direction. Same AZ = free. Design: place RDS in same AZ as EC2 where possible. Internet egress = most expensive ($0.09+/GB). Use CloudFront to cache and reduce origin hits. Direct Connect vs internet: DX has lower data transfer rates for sustained large volumes. 💡 Serverless = no server management, pay per use, auto-scale. Lambda limits: 15 min max, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, 1000 concurrent (soft). Cold start: Lambda function first invocation takes longer. Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm. Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. This is ideal for processing specific fields or rows from large files. Lambda /tmp storage is limited to 10 GB (10,240 MB) — insufficient for a 100 GB file. q: A company stores 1 million small files (average 10 KB) in Amazon S3 Standard. They plan to access each file rarely — about once per year. S3 Intelligent-Tiering was suggested. What is a concern with using Intelligent-Tiering for small files?
options: S3 Intelligent-Tiering has a per-object monitoring and automation fee ($0.0025 per 1,000 objects). For 1 million files at 10 KB, the monitoring fee per GB is higher than the storage savings — making Standard or Standard-IA cheaper
Intelligent-Tiering is only for files larger than 1 TB
Intelligent-Tiering has high retrieval fees for small files
Intelligent-Tiering doesn't work with small files
explain: Intelligent-Tiering charges $0.0025 per 1,000 objects/month for monitoring. For 1 million objects, that's $2.50/month monitoring fee regardless of object size. 1 million × 10 KB = 10 GB, costing ~$0.23/month in Standard. The monitoring fee ($2.50) exceeds the storage cost ($0.23). AWS recommends Intelligent-Tiering only for objects larger than 128 KB (larger objects have higher storage costs that justify the monitoring fee).

q: A company runs a web application with an ALB and Auto Scaling group. During off-peak hours, the minimum instance count is 2 but CloudWatch shows CPU at 2% on both instances. What cost optimization should be implemented?
options: Purchase Reserved Instances for the 2 minimum instances
Reduce the minimum instance count to 1 if the application can tolerate brief capacity reduction during scale-out events, or migrate to a serverless architecture (Lambda) if traffic patterns suit it
Delete the application load balancer to save costs
Enable EC2 Hibernate to pause idle instances while keeping them reserved
explain: If the application can tolerate the brief period (typically 2-5 minutes) for Auto Scaling to launch a second instance when needed, reducing minimum to 1 halves the minimum capacity cost. For dramatically variable workloads (near-zero off-peak), migrating to Lambda may be even more cost-effective. Purchasing RIs for 2 instances provides discounts but doesn't reduce running costs. Hibernate isn't supported for all instance types and the instance is still reserved.

q: A company has Lambda functions invoked by SQS. Each Lambda invocation processes 1 message. Messages average 2 KB. The function runs for 100ms. They're looking to optimize cost. What change has the greatest impact?
options: Use Lambda SnapStart to reduce execution duration

Configure SQS batch size of 10 in the Lambda event source mapping — 1 Lambda invocation processes 10 messages instead of 1, reducing invocation count (and cost) by 90% with no impact on processing logic
Reduce Lambda memory from 1024 MB to 128 MB
Switch from SQS to SNS to reduce queue costs
explain: Lambda charges per invocation and per GB-second. Increasing SQS batch size to 10 reduces Lambda invocations by 90% (same messages, 10x fewer invocations) while keeping the same total GB-seconds for processing. This is the highest-impact optimization. Reducing memory from 1024 MB may affect performance (test first). SNS doesn't queue messages. SnapStart is for Java, and the function duration is already minimal at 100ms.

q: A company is designing a data archival solution. Data must be retained for 10 years and retrieved within 48 hours when needed (compliance audit scenarios — infrequent). What is the most cost-effective storage solution?
options: Amazon S3 Glacier Deep Archive — at $0.00099/GB/month it is the lowest-cost AWS storage option, with 12-hour Standard and 48-hour Bulk retrieval meeting the 48-hour requirement
Amazon EBS Cold HDD (sc1) volumes
Amazon S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval
Amazon S3 Standard — reliable and always available
explain: S3 Glacier Deep Archive at ~$1/TB/month is the cheapest AWS storage, designed for data accessed less than once per year. With 48-hour retrieval SLAs for compliance audits, Standard retrieval (12 hours) or Bulk retrieval (48 hours) meet the requirement. S3 Standard costs ~$23/TB/month — 23x more expensive. Glacier Flexible Retrieval costs ~$4/TB/month. EBS volumes are for block storage, not archival.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: cost optimization → match pricing model to workload type | cold/archive data → Glacier or Deep Archive (cheapest storage). 📋 Task 4.1 — Cost-optimized storage solutions: Cost-optimized storage = match storage class to access frequency. Know the access pattern? → Lifecycle policies (explicit transitions). Unknown/unpredictable access? → Intelligent-Tiering (auto-moves, has monitoring fee). Never accessed after 7 years? → Deep Archive + expiration rule. EBS cost tip: gp3 = same price as gp2 but you tune IOPS/throughput → always migrate gp2 → gp3. S3 retrieval fee alert: Glacier/IA have per-GB retrieval fees — important for frequently-read data. Data not accessed but must keep 7 years → Lifecycle to Deep Archive (compliance). 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: S3 Glacier Deep Archive at ~$1/TB/month is the cheapest AWS storage, designed for data accessed less than once per year. With 48-hour retrieval SLAs for compliance audits, Standard retrieval (12 hours) or Bulk retrieval (48 hours) meet the requirement. S3 Standard costs ~$23/TB/month — 23x more expensive. Glacier Flexible Retrieval costs ~$4/TB/month. EBS volumes are for block storage, not archival.

q: A company receives inconsistent traffic with very low baseline usage but occasional 100x traffic spikes. They're currently using EC2 with Auto Scaling but paying for minimum EC2 instances even at low traffic. What architecture reduces baseline costs to near-zero?
options: Use Amazon Lightsail for lower-cost instances at baseline
Reduce minimum Auto Scaling group size to 0
Use EC2 Spot Instances for the entire fleet
Migrate to a serverless architecture (API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB on-demand) — costs scale linearly from near-zero with actual usage, and AWS manages scaling transparently
explain: Serverless architectures have no idle cost. Lambda charges per invocation and duration only. API Gateway charges per API call. DynamoDB on-demand charges per request. Total cost at low traffic approaches zero. Auto Scaling minimum of 0 is possible but creates cold-start issues for EC2 (5-10 minutes). Spot Instances save cost but still charge per running hour. Lightsail has fixed monthly pricing, not variable.

Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. q: A company's AWS Config costs have grown unexpectedly. They enable Config in 20 AWS regions across 10 accounts. Analysis shows Config is recording all resource types including many they don't need. What reduces Config costs?
options: Enable Config Snapshot delivery only (disable stream delivery)
Disable Config in all non-production accounts

Configure Config to record only specific resource types that are relevant to compliance requirements — excluding unnecessary resource types reduces the number of configuration items recorded and associated costs
Use AWS Security Hub instead of Config for all compliance monitoring
explain: AWS Config charges per configuration item recorded. Selecting only the resource types required for compliance (e.g., EC2, S3, IAM, RDS) instead of recording all resource types significantly reduces the volume of configuration items and costs. Disabling Config in non-production accounts reduces coverage but may violate compliance requirements. Config rules and recording are separate — recording all types with no rules still generates costs. Security Hub uses Config as a data source.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: repeated reads → add caching layer (ElastiCache or DAX). 📋 Task 4.4 — Cost-optimized network architectures: Network cost: data transfer is the hidden cost driver. Key insight: NAT Gateway data processing fee ($0.045/GB) accumulates fast for S3 access from private subnet → replace with VPC Gateway Endpoint (FREE). Cross-AZ charges = $0.01/GB each direction. Same AZ = free. Design: place RDS in same AZ as EC2 where possible. Internet egress = most expensive ($0.09+/GB). Use CloudFront to cache and reduce origin hits. Direct Connect vs internet: DX has lower data transfer rates for sustained large volumes. 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS Config charges per configuration item recorded. Selecting only the resource types required for compliance (e.g., EC2, S3, IAM, RDS) instead of recording all resource types significantly reduces the volume of configuration items and costs. Disabling Config in non-production accounts reduces coverage but may violate compliance requirements. Config rules and recording are separate — recording all types with no rules still generates costs. Security Hub uses Config as a data source.

q: A company has a large amount of infrequently accessed data in Amazon DynamoDB. Read costs are acceptable but storage costs are high due to data that hasn't been accessed in 2+ years. What is the most cost-effective approach?
options: Increase DynamoDB provisioned capacity to speed up data retrieval and deletion
Migrate the entire DynamoDB table to Amazon RDS for cheaper storage
Use DynamoDB Time to Live (TTL) to automatically delete expired items at no charge, and export aged data to S3 using DynamoDB export for cold archival storage at 75% less cost than DynamoDB

For data needing archival (not deletion), DynamoDB export to S3 provides the data at S3 storage prices (~$0.023/GB vs ~$0.25/GB for DynamoDB). This combination manages the full data lifecycle. Auto-scaling manages throughput capacity, not storage costs. RDS migration changes the data model.

q: A company reviews their AWS bill and notices they're paying $50,000/month for data transfer out to the internet. Most of this is serving static assets (images, CSS, JS) directly from S3 to users. What architectural change could dramatically reduce these costs?
options: Place Amazon CloudFront in front of S3 — CloudFront data transfer rates are up to 50% cheaper than direct S3 transfer rates, and CloudFront caching reduces the volume of S3 GET requests
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration — it reduces data transfer costs
Switch to S3 Reduced Redundancy Storage for static assets
Use AWS Direct Connect for all end-user data transfer
explain: CloudFront data transfer to internet is up to 50% cheaper than S3 data transfer to internet. Additionally, CloudFront caching reduces origin fetches to S3, lowering both S3 GET request counts and S3 data transfer costs. For $50K/month, CloudFront could reduce costs by $25K+/month. S3 Transfer Acceleration increases transfer speed to S3, not from S3 to users. Direct Connect is for enterprise on-premises connections.

Same AZ = free. Design: place RDS in same AZ as EC2 where possible. Internet egress = most expensive ($0.09+/GB). Use CloudFront to cache and reduce origin hits. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. ❌ Eliminate distractors: CloudFront data transfer to internet is up to 50% cheaper than S3 data transfer to internet. Additionally, CloudFront caching reduces origin fetches to S3, lowering both S3 GET request counts and S3 data transfer costs. For $50K/month, CloudFront could reduce costs by $25K+/month. S3 Transfer Acceleration increases transfer speed to S3, not from S3 to users. Direct Connect is for enterprise on-premises connections.

q: A company runs many EC2 instances of different types across multiple regions. They want a single purchasing commitment that maximizes savings across their diverse, changing fleet without being locked to specific instance types. Which option provides this?
options: Regional Reserved Instances — flexible within a region
Standard Reserved Instances — highest discount but least flexible
EC2 Instance Savings Plans — apply to specific instance families per region
Compute Savings Plans — apply to any EC2 instance regardless of family, size, region, tenancy, or OS; also apply to Fargate and Lambda
explain: Compute Savings Plans are the most flexible commitment-based discount. They apply automatically to any EC2 instance usage (any family, size, region, OS), Fargate tasks, and Lambda invocations. When the fleet changes (e.g., switching from m5 to m6g, or from one region to another), the Savings Plan continues to apply. Instance Savings Plans only apply to a specific instance family per region. Standard RIs are locked to specific instance type and region.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 📋 Task 4.1 — Cost-optimized storage solutions: Cost-optimized storage = match storage class to access frequency. Know the access pattern? → Lifecycle policies (explicit transitions). Unknown/unpredictable access? → Intelligent-Tiering (auto-moves, has monitoring fee). Never accessed after 7 years? → Deep Archive + expiration rule. EBS cost tip: gp3 = same price as gp2 but you tune IOPS/throughput → always migrate gp2 → gp3. S3 retrieval fee alert: Glacier/IA have per-GB retrieval fees — important for frequently-read data. Data not accessed but must keep 7 years → Lifecycle to Deep Archive (compliance). 💡 Serverless = no server management, pay per use, auto-scale. Lambda limits: 15 min max, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, 1000 concurrent (soft). Cold start: Lambda function first invocation takes longer. Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm. Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Compute Savings Plans are the most flexible commitment-based discount. They apply automatically to any EC2 instance usage (any family, size, region, OS), Fargate tasks, and Lambda invocations. When the fleet changes (e.g., switching from m5 to m6g, or from one region to another), the Savings Plan continues to apply. Instance Savings Plans only apply to a specific instance family per region. Standard RIs are locked to specific instance type and region.

q: A company's cost analysis shows S3 request costs are unexpectedly high. Investigation reveals a Lambda function that processes S3 events is performing a HeadObject API call on every event to get object metadata, even though metadata is available in the S3 event notification itself. What is the fix?
options: Modify the Lambda function to extract object metadata (size, content-type, ETag) directly from the S3 event notification payload instead of making an additional HeadObject API call — eliminates 100% of those S3 request charges
Enable S3 Requester Pays to shift API costs to requesters
Cache HeadObject results in ElastiCache to reduce repeated calls
Switch from S3 event notifications to S3 Batch Operations
explain: S3 event notifications include object metadata (size, ETag, content-type) in the event payload. Making an additional HeadObject call is redundant and incurs S3 LIST/GET request charges ($0.0004 per 1,000 requests). Eliminating these calls from the Lambda function reduces both S3 API costs and Lambda execution time. Caching helps but HeadObject is still called once per unique object. Batch Operations changes the processing model.

q: A company stores 10 TB of application logs in Amazon S3 in JSON format. They run Athena queries daily to analyze the data but pay high Athena scan costs. The JSON files are not partitioned and are each 1-5 MB. What is the most effective cost reduction for Athena?
options: Run Athena queries during off-peak hours for lower cost
Use Athena workgroups to limit per-query scan costs

Convert JSON to Apache Parquet format using AWS Glue ETL and partition data by date in S3 — Parquet columnar format reduces data scanned by 60-90%, and date partitions allow queries to scan only relevant time periods
Increase Athena query timeout to scan more data per query
explain: Athena charges $5 per TB scanned. Parquet's columnar format means Athena reads only the columns in your SELECT clause (not entire rows), reducing scanned data by 60-90%. Date-based partitioning ensures queries with date filters scan only relevant partitions. This combination typically reduces Athena costs by 70-90%. Athena pricing doesn't change by time of day. Workgroup scan limits prevent runaway queries but don't reduce the cost of necessary queries.

Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 📋 Task 4.4 — Cost-optimized network architectures: Network cost: data transfer is the hidden cost driver. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Athena charges $5 per TB scanned. Parquet's columnar format means Athena reads only the columns in your SELECT clause (not entire rows), reducing scanned data by 60-90%. Date-based partitioning ensures queries with date filters scan only relevant partitions. This combination typically reduces Athena costs by 70-90%. Athena pricing doesn't change by time of day. Workgroup scan limits prevent runaway queries but don't reduce the cost of necessary queries.

q: A company needs to run a high-performance computing (HPC) simulation that takes 2 hours on 100 c5n.18xlarge instances. The simulation runs twice per week. What is the cost-optimized compute strategy?
options: Use AWS Fargate for the HPC simulation
Purchase Reserved Instances for 100 c5n.18xlarge instances
Use EC2 Spot Instances with a Spot Fleet configured for the HPC workload — leverage checkpointing and placement groups; Spot provides up to 90% savings for this interruptible batch workload
Keep 100 c5n.18xlarge instances running 24/7 for immediate availability
explain: HPC simulations are ideal Spot candidates: fault-tolerant (can checkpoint), short-duration (2 hours), and run on a schedule (not time-critical to the minute). Spot Instances provide up to 90% savings. With 100 × c5n.18xlarge × 2 hours × 2/week, On-Demand would cost ~$6,000/week; Spot ~$600/week. Running 24/7 is ~$84,000/week in On-Demand costs — 140x more expensive. RIs for 100 large instances is expensive for 4 hours/week of actual use. Fargate doesn't support HPC networking requirements.

q: A company uses AWS Lambda and notices high costs from a function invoked every minute by EventBridge to poll a database for new records. The function rarely finds new records. What architecture reduces invocation costs?
options: Cache database query results in ElastiCache to speed up empty polls
Reduce polling frequency to every 5 minutes
Increase Lambda memory to process polling faster

Replace polling with an event-driven architecture — trigger Lambda via DynamoDB Streams, RDS events, or an application-layer event when records are actually inserted, eliminating unnecessary invocations when no records exist
explain: Polling (pulling) for new records wastes Lambda invocations when nothing has changed. Event-driven architecture (pushing) triggers Lambda only when records are actually inserted (via DynamoDB Streams, SQS, SNS, or EventBridge events from the database). This can reduce invocations by 99%+ if records arrive infrequently. Reducing frequency to 5 minutes still wastes invocations. Caching empty results doesn't reduce Lambda invocations.

Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Polling (pulling) for new records wastes Lambda invocations when nothing has changed. q: A company uses Amazon EBS and has 500 volumes provisioned with 1,000 IOPS each (io1 type). Analysis shows 80% of volumes never exceed 100 IOPS. What is the cost-optimized action?
options: Delete all underutilized volumes

Downgrade the 80% underutilized volumes from io1 (1,000 IOPS) to gp3 — gp3 provides 3,000 IOPS baseline at a lower cost per GB and can be provisioned at exactly the needed IOPS level, saving 50-70% on those volumes
Enable EBS burst mode on io1 volumes to reduce provisioned IOPS
Convert all io1 volumes to instance store
explain: io1 charges per provisioned IOPS ($0.065/provisioned IOPS-month). Switching to gp3 includes 3,000 IOPS for the base price (~$0.08/GB-month) with no per-IOPS charge up to 3,000 IOPS. Volumes actually using only 100 IOPS pay for 1,000 provisioned IOPS unnecessarily on io1. gp3 saves ~50-70% for most of these volumes. Deleting volumes removes data. Instance store cannot be replaced with EBS volumes dynamically. io1 doesn't have burst mode like gp2.

q: A company wants to identify which AWS services are driving unexpected cost increases month-over-month. What is the first step in their cost investigation?
options: Review all CloudTrail API calls to identify new resource creation
Contact AWS Support for a bill review
Use AWS Cost Explorer with service-level grouping and month-over-month cost comparison filters to identify which services, regions, and accounts have the highest cost growth
Enable AWS Budgets with an alert for any monthly cost exceeding last month's spend
explain: AWS Cost Explorer provides visualization of costs by service, region, account, and tag with time comparisons. Grouping by service and comparing current month to previous months immediately identifies which services have unexpected increases. CloudTrail shows API calls but not aggregated costs. AWS Budgets alerts on threshold breaches but doesn't identify root causes. AWS Support can help but should come after internal investigation.

q: A startup is building a new application on AWS and wants to minimize costs during development while maintaining a realistic test environment. What approach is recommended?
options: Develop locally on laptops and only deploy to AWS for final testing

Develop everything on a single large EC2 instance to simplify costs

Use AWS Free Tier services where available, deploy smaller instance types in dev (scale up for production), use Aurora Serverless v2 with minimum ACU for dev databases, and leverage Spot Instances for development EC2

q: A company uses AWS Organizations with 50 accounts. They want to apply a single discount strategy across all accounts for EC2. Which purchasing option applies organization-wide automatically?
options: Compute Savings Plans in the management account — the discount applies across all accounts in the organization through billing consolidation
Purchase Reserved Instances in each member account separately
Enable RI sharing in each member account
Use Spot Instances across all accounts for cost savings
explain: Savings Plans and Reserved Instances purchased in the management (payer) account apply across all member accounts through consolidated billing. Compute Savings Plans automatically apply to the highest-cost usage across the organization. No action is needed in individual accounts beyond keeping RI sharing enabled (default). Individual account purchases are less efficient for organization-wide cost optimization.
