q: A company stores user profile images in Amazon S3 and serves them via an application server. The application currently downloads each image from S3 and re-serves it to the end user, doubling data transfer costs. What change eliminates double transfer?
options: Cache images on the application server EBS volume
Generate S3 pre-signed URLs and return them directly to the client — the client downloads the image directly from S3, eliminating the application server data transfer hop and associated costs
Use S3 Select to serve partial image data
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket
explain: Pre-signed URLs allow clients to download directly from S3, bypassing the application server. This eliminates: (1) S3-to-EC2 transfer (though intra-region is free), and (2) EC2-to-internet data transfer charges (the most expensive leg). The client pays for one S3-to-internet transfer instead of S3-to-EC2-to-internet. CloudFront is another option for cached, frequently accessed images.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: NAT Gateway cost → replace with VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3/DynamoDB (free). 📋 Task 4.4 — Cost-optimized network architectures: Network cost: data transfer is the hidden cost driver. Key insight: NAT Gateway data processing fee ($0.045/GB) accumulates fast for S3 access from private subnet → replace with VPC Gateway Endpoint (FREE). Cross-AZ charges = $0.01/GB each direction. Same AZ = free. Design: place RDS in same AZ as EC2 where possible. Internet egress = most expensive ($0.09+/GB). Use CloudFront to cache and reduce origin hits. Direct Connect vs internet: DX has lower data transfer rates for sustained large volumes. 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Pre-signed URLs allow clients to download directly from S3, bypassing the application server. This eliminates: (1) S3-to-EC2 transfer (though intra-region is free), and (2) EC2-to-internet data transfer charges (the most expensive leg). The client pays for one S3-to-internet transfer instead of S3-to-EC2-to-internet. CloudFront is another option for cached, frequently accessed images.

q: A company is building a data lake on Amazon S3. They use multiple AWS accounts for different business units. Cross-account S3 data transfer is generating unexpected costs. What architectural change reduces cross-account transfer costs?
options: Move data to each account's local S3 bucket
Use AWS DataSync for cross-account data access
Use VPC peering between accounts for S3 access
Use S3 access points with account-level permissions — different accounts access the same S3 bucket in the data lake owner's account, eliminating cross-account data copies
explain: S3 access points allow multiple AWS accounts to access a centralized S3 bucket with appropriate permissions. Data stays in one location — no copies are needed across accounts. Cross-account S3 access within the same region has no data transfer cost (intra-region S3 access is free even cross-account). Copying data to each account duplicates storage costs. DataSync is for replication/migration, not concurrent cross-account access.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: shared POSIX file system → EFS (Linux NFS) or FSx for Windows (SMB) | NAT Gateway cost → replace with VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3/DynamoDB (free). 📋 Task 4.4 — Cost-optimized network architectures: Network cost: data transfer is the hidden cost driver. Key insight: NAT Gateway data processing fee ($0.045/GB) accumulates fast for S3 access from private subnet → replace with VPC Gateway Endpoint (FREE). Cross-AZ charges = $0.01/GB each direction. Same AZ = free. Design: place RDS in same AZ as EC2 where possible. Internet egress = most expensive ($0.09+/GB). Use CloudFront to cache and reduce origin hits. Direct Connect vs internet: DX has lower data transfer rates for sustained large volumes. 💡 Migration decision: Large offline data transfer (limited bandwidth) → Snow Family (Snowball/Snowcone/Snowmobile). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. 💡 Multiple VPC connectivity: Few VPCs (≤3) → VPC Peering (simple, free). Many VPCs → Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke, centralised routing). On-prem to AWS: Need encryption + speed → DX + VPN over DX. Need encryption + cheap → Site-to-Site VPN. Need high bandwidth + consistent + NOT encrypted → Direct Connect alone. Expose a service to another VPC without full access → PrivateLink (not peering). DNS on-prem → VPC: Route 53 Resolver inbound/outbound endpoints. ❌ Eliminate distractors: S3 access points allow multiple AWS accounts to access a centralized S3 bucket with appropriate permissions. Data stays in one location — no copies are needed across accounts. Cross-account S3 access within the same region has no data transfer cost (intra-region S3 access is free even cross-account). Copying data to each account duplicates storage costs. DataSync is for replication/migration, not concurrent cross-account access.

q: A company has 200 Lambda functions in production. A monthly cost review shows 150 functions are allocated 3 GB of memory but average memory usage is only 200 MB. What is the cost impact and recommended action?
options: Consolidate 150 functions into 10 larger functions to reduce overhead

Lambda charges per GB-second; allocating 3 GB for functions using only 200 MB wastes 93% of allocated memory cost — right-size these functions to 256 MB, reducing Lambda cost by approximately 91% for those functions
Lambda memory allocation doesn't affect cost — only execution time matters
Reduce Lambda concurrent executions to save costs
explain: Lambda bills per GB-second. A function allocated 3 GB costs 15x more than the same function at 200 MB (the optimal allocation). Right-sizing 150 functions from 3 GB to 256 MB reduces their cost by ~91%. AWS Lambda Power Tuning is an open-source tool that finds the cost-optimal memory setting. Reducing concurrency limits availability. Consolidation changes architecture.

q: A company uses Route 53 for DNS. They have 100 health checks configured, each checking every 10 seconds. After a cost review, they find Route 53 health check costs are significant. How can they reduce costs while maintaining coverage?
options: Move DNS to an external provider to avoid Route 53 health check costs
Switch from HTTP health checks to TCP health checks

Increase health check interval to 30 seconds for non-critical endpoints (reducing cost by 67% per check) and consolidate health checks where possible — use calculated health checks to combine multiple endpoint checks
Disable all health checks — they're optional
explain: Route 53 charges per health check per month, and fast health checks (10s interval) cost 3x more than standard (30s interval). Increasing to 30s for non-critical endpoints and using calculated health checks (which combine multiple health checks and cost less than individual checks) reduces costs significantly. TCP vs HTTP check pricing is the same. Moving to an external DNS provider may increase costs elsewhere and lose AWS integration.

Know the access pattern? → Lifecycle policies (explicit transitions). Unknown/unpredictable access? → Intelligent-Tiering (auto-moves, has monitoring fee). Never accessed after 7 years? → Deep Archive + expiration rule. EBS cost tip: gp3 = same price as gp2 but you tune IOPS/throughput → always migrate gp2 → gp3. S3 retrieval fee alert: Glacier/IA have per-GB retrieval fees — important for frequently-read data. Data not accessed but must keep 7 years → Lifecycle to Deep Archive (compliance). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Route 53 charges per health check per month, and fast health checks (10s interval) cost 3x more than standard (30s interval). Increasing to 30s for non-critical endpoints and using calculated health checks (which combine multiple health checks and cost less than individual checks) reduces costs significantly. TCP vs HTTP check pricing is the same. Moving to an external DNS provider may increase costs elsewhere and lose AWS integration.

q: A company runs Amazon EMR clusters for daily batch processing. Each cluster runs for 6 hours then terminates. They use On-Demand pricing for the master and core nodes, and Spot for task nodes. The master node is a large instance type. What additional cost optimization is possible?
options: Right-size the master node — the EMR master node is a coordinator that doesn't process data directly; it often only needs a small/medium instance, potentially saving 50-80% on the master node cost
Eliminate the task nodes and use only core nodes
Use Spot Instances for master and core nodes as well
Use Reserved Instances for the EMR master node
explain: The EMR master node manages the cluster (YARN ResourceManager, HDFS NameNode) but doesn't process data. For most workloads, a m5.xlarge or m5.2xlarge master node is sufficient vs. using the same large instance as worker nodes. RIs for transient 6-hour clusters don't provide value (RI is hourly; a 6-hour cluster benefits but the utilization is low compared to commitment). Spot for master/core risks cluster instability.

q: A company stores log data in Amazon OpenSearch Service (Elasticsearch). After 30 days, logs are rarely queried but must be retained for 1 year for compliance. The OpenSearch cluster is sized to store 365 days of logs, which is expensive. What is the cost-optimized solution?
options: Compress log documents in OpenSearch to reduce storage
Delete logs older than 30 days from OpenSearch
Move logs to RDS after 30 days for cheaper storage
Use OpenSearch UltraWarm or Cold storage tiers for logs older than 30 days — UltraWarm costs significantly less than hot storage and data can be moved back to hot tier for queries when needed
explain: OpenSearch UltraWarm storage costs ~87% less than hot storage. Cold storage costs even less. Data can be moved from hot → UltraWarm → Cold automatically via Index State Management (ISM) policies. Logs older than 30 days are rarely queried but available when needed after moving to UltraWarm. This allows the active cluster to be sized for 30 days while retaining 1 year of data cost-effectively.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: cost optimization → match pricing model to workload type | access pattern by age → S3 Lifecycle policy. 📋 Task 4.4 — Cost-optimized network architectures: Network cost: data transfer is the hidden cost driver. Key insight: NAT Gateway data processing fee ($0.045/GB) accumulates fast for S3 access from private subnet → replace with VPC Gateway Endpoint (FREE). Cross-AZ charges = $0.01/GB each direction. Same AZ = free. Design: place RDS in same AZ as EC2 where possible. Internet egress = most expensive ($0.09+/GB). Use CloudFront to cache and reduce origin hits. Direct Connect vs internet: DX has lower data transfer rates for sustained large volumes. 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 Monitoring tool selection: Performance metrics/alarms → CloudWatch. API call audit (who did what) → CloudTrail. Resource compliance over time → AWS Config. Distributed tracing (latency bottleneck in microservices) → X-Ray. Threat detection / security anomaly → GuardDuty. CloudWatch ≠ CloudTrail: CW = performance/ops. CT = security/audit. Config = did the resource configuration change? Was it compliant? Common pattern: CloudTrail → S3 → Athena for long-term audit queries. ❌ Eliminate distractors: OpenSearch UltraWarm storage costs ~87% less than hot storage. Cold storage costs even less. Data can be moved from hot → UltraWarm → Cold automatically via Index State Management (ISM) policies. Logs older than 30 days are rarely queried but available when needed after moving to UltraWarm. This allows the active cluster to be sized for 30 days while retaining 1 year of data cost-effectively.

q: A company uses AWS WAF to protect their application. They have 30 web ACLs deployed across multiple CloudFront distributions and ALBs. AWS WAF costs are significant. What is a cost optimization strategy?
options: Remove all managed rule groups and write all custom rules
Disable WAF on development environments
Consolidate WAF rules into a smaller number of rule groups shared across web ACLs using AWS Firewall Manager, reducing the number of active web ACLs and rule groups while maintaining protection
Switch from WAF to Security Groups for DDoS protection
explain: WAF charges per web ACL per month and per rule/rule group. AWS Firewall Manager allows centrally managing WAF policies across accounts and resources, ensuring consistent protection with fewer redundant rule groups. Disabling WAF on dev reduces cost but removes protection. Security Groups don't provide L7 protection. Custom rules require significant development effort and expertise.

q: A company uses Amazon Redshift. Their cluster utilization shows 95% memory usage and slow query performance. They're considering upgrading to larger nodes. What should they evaluate first to ensure cost-effectiveness?
options: Enable Redshift automatic table optimization and wait for it to resolve performance issues

First analyze Redshift Advisor recommendations, consider VACUUM and ANALYZE to reclaim space and update statistics, evaluate data distribution and sort keys, and consider Redshift Spectrum to offload cold data to S3 before purchasing larger nodes
Immediately upgrade to larger nodes — performance issues justify any cost
Add more nodes to the cluster without changing node size
explain: Before adding expensive capacity, optimization should be explored: VACUUM reclaims storage from deleted rows (improving memory usage), ANALYZE updates query planner statistics, distribution key improvements reduce data movement, and moving cold data to S3 via Spectrum can dramatically reduce cluster storage requirements. These are free optimizations. Adding nodes or upgrading is expensive and may not be necessary after optimization.

q: A company processes 1 billion small transactions per day in DynamoDB. They're on provisioned capacity and auto-scaling. Cost Explorer shows DynamoDB is their largest cost. What should they investigate for potential savings?
options: Evaluate DynamoDB Standard-IA table class for tables with infrequent reads — if read/write ratio is low, Standard-IA reduces storage costs by 60% with the same performance
Migrate to RDS for cheaper transaction processing
Switch to DynamoDB on-demand mode for all tables
Reduce DynamoDB item size to reduce storage costs
explain: DynamoDB Standard-IA (Infrequent Access) table class reduces storage costs by 60% but increases read/write request costs. It's cost-effective for tables with high storage but lower throughput requirements. For tables that store lots of data but aren't heavily read/written (common for audit logs, historical data), Standard-IA is cheaper overall. On-demand mode adds flexibility but may cost more than well-tuned provisioned capacity at 1B daily transactions.

Never accessed after 7 years? → Deep Archive + expiration rule. EBS cost tip: gp3 = same price as gp2 but you tune IOPS/throughput → always migrate gp2 → gp3. S3 retrieval fee alert: Glacier/IA have per-GB retrieval fees — important for frequently-read data. Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. It's cost-effective for tables with high storage but lower throughput requirements. For tables that store lots of data but aren't heavily read/written (common for audit logs, historical data), Standard-IA is cheaper overall. On-demand mode adds flexibility but may cost more than well-tuned provisioned capacity at 1B daily transactions.

q: A company has multiple Amazon ECS services running in separate VPCs across different accounts. Services communicate via internet-facing ALBs, generating data transfer costs and security concerns. What architecture reduces both cost and security risk?
options: Use AWS PrivateLink to expose services — consuming VPCs connect to interface VPC endpoints, traffic stays on AWS private network, eliminating internet data transfer charges and reducing attack surface
Use Direct Connect for all inter-service communication
Merge all services into a single VPC to eliminate cross-VPC traffic
Enable VPC peering between all VPCs to eliminate internet routing
explain: AWS PrivateLink provides private connectivity between VPCs (even cross-account) without internet exposure. Data transfer via PrivateLink is cheaper than internet-routed traffic. It's also more secure — no public IPs or internet exposure needed. VPC peering creates a full mesh which becomes complex with many VPCs. Merging VPCs requires significant re-architecture. Direct Connect is for on-premises to AWS connectivity.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Always for: RDS, ElastiCache, ALB, EFS | many VPCs → Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke). Online continuous data transfer → DataSync (scheduled, incremental). SFTP server to cloud → Transfer Family. DB migration (same engine) → DMS homogeneous. DB migration (different engine, e.g., Oracle → Aurora) → SCT + DMS heterogeneous. Hybrid storage (on-prem + S3) → Storage Gateway. Strategy: Rehost (lift-and-shift) = fastest. Refactor = most cloud-native but most work. 💡 Multiple VPC connectivity: Few VPCs (≤3) → VPC Peering (simple, free). Many VPCs → Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke, centralised routing). On-prem to AWS: Need encryption + speed → DX + VPN over DX. Need encryption + cheap → Site-to-Site VPN. Need high bandwidth + consistent + NOT encrypted → Direct Connect alone. Expose a service to another VPC without full access → PrivateLink (not peering). DNS on-prem → VPC: Route 53 Resolver inbound/outbound endpoints. ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS PrivateLink provides private connectivity between VPCs (even cross-account) without internet exposure. Data transfer via PrivateLink is cheaper than internet-routed traffic. It's also more secure — no public IPs or internet exposure needed. q: A company is evaluating whether to use Kinesis Data Firehose or custom Lambda + S3 code for log delivery. The logs need to be delivered to S3. Which is more cost-effective at scale?
options: Both solutions cost exactly the same at scale

Kinesis Data Firehose is typically more cost-effective — charges per GB ingested with no infrastructure to manage, while custom Lambda + S3 requires paying for Lambda execution, SQS or Kinesis for buffering, and engineering time
Kinesis Data Firehose is only cost-effective below 1 GB/day
Custom Lambda + S3 is always cheaper because you control the code
explain: Firehose charges $0.029/GB ingested with automatic batching, compression, and S3 delivery. Custom Lambda solutions require Lambda execution cost, SQS or Kinesis trigger cost, engineering time, and maintenance. For most log delivery use cases, Firehose is simpler and cheaper. Only at very high scale (hundreds of TB/day) might custom solutions be cheaper. Operational simplicity of Firehose also has significant value.

q: A company needs to run a containerized application that processes work items from an SQS queue. The queue typically has 0 items but occasionally receives thousands. What is the most cost-effective architecture?
options: Run a fixed number of ECS Fargate tasks to poll the queue
Use EC2 Auto Scaling with SQS metric scaling
Use ECS with Application Auto Scaling — it scales on SQS queue depth
Use KEDA (Kubernetes Event-driven Autoscaling) on EKS to scale from 0 to thousands of pods based on SQS queue depth, or use Lambda with SQS event source mapping — scale from 0 with no idle costs
explain: Lambda with SQS event source mapping is the most cost-effective: Lambda scales from 0 invocations and charges per invocation only when there are messages. Zero cost at idle. ECS/Fargate task minimum is 1 task unless scaled to 0 (which introduces startup latency). KEDA on EKS also scales to 0 but requires cluster management. EC2 Auto Scaling has minimum instance costs. For sporadic, queue-driven workloads, Lambda is the natural cost-efficient choice.

q: A company uses Amazon CloudFront and S3 for a global media website. They find that cache hit ratio is 40% — lower than expected. What investigation and improvement should they prioritize?
options: Switch from S3 to EC2 origin for dynamic content
Enable CloudFront Lambda@Edge for all requests
Increase number of CloudFront edge locations

Analyze CloudFront cache statistics to identify low-cache-hit behaviors; common fixes include increasing object TTL, ensuring Cache-Control headers are set appropriately, removing unnecessary query strings from cache keys, and identifying objects that should be cached but aren't
explain: A 40% cache hit ratio means 60% of requests go to the origin, paying for both CloudFront and S3 transfer costs. Investigation typically reveals: objects with TTL=0 that should be cached, query strings creating unique cache keys unnecessarily, or Cookie forwarding creating many cache variations. Raising TTL for static assets to hours/days is the most impactful fix. CloudFront cache statistics reports identify which paths have low hit ratios.

q: A company runs scheduled AWS Glue ETL jobs. The jobs take 2-3 minutes to complete but they're being charged for 10 minutes minimum. How can they reduce Glue costs?
options: Schedule all Glue jobs to run simultaneously to maximize 10-minute billing windows

For short-duration jobs under 10 minutes, consider using AWS Glue Flex execution (accepts excess capacity at discounted rates) or migrate short ETL logic to Lambda which charges per 1ms increment with no minimum
Increase DPU allocation to finish jobs faster within the 10-minute minimum
Use larger DPU counts to finish in under 1 minute
explain: AWS Glue Standard jobs have a 1-minute minimum billing. Glue charges per second for jobs over 1 minute. However, for very short ETL tasks, Lambda (charges per 1ms with no minimum) or Python scripts on EC2 may be more cost-effective. Glue Flex mode accepts excess capacity at up to 34% discount but with non-guaranteed start time. The key insight is evaluating whether Glue is the right tool for sub-2-minute jobs.

q: A company is designing a new multi-tier web application. The database tier is expected to have high read volume with occasional write spikes. They want to minimize database costs. What architecture provides the best cost optimization?
options: Use Amazon Aurora with Aurora Replicas — Aurora's distributed storage architecture means replicas don't duplicate storage costs (all replicas share the same underlying storage), providing read scaling at lower cost than RDS replicas which each have full storage copies
Use Amazon RDS MySQL Multi-AZ for high availability and read replicas for reads
Use DynamoDB for the relational database workload
Use ElastiCache as the primary data store to minimize database costs
explain: Aurora Replicas share the same distributed storage volume as the primary — you don't pay for N copies of the storage for N replicas. RDS Read Replicas each have a full copy of the data, so storage costs multiply with each replica. For read-heavy workloads needing multiple read replicas, Aurora is more cost-effective in storage. Aurora also provides Aurora reader endpoint load balancing natively. DynamoDB doesn't serve relational workloads.

Always for: RDS, ElastiCache, ALB, EFS | read scaling → Read Replicas (async, readable). Multi-AZ standby is NOT readable | static IP for load balancer → NLB (has EIP). ALB has dynamic IPs. 📋 Task 4.3 — Cost-optimized database solutions: DB cost: match pricing model to traffic pattern. Variable DB load → Aurora Serverless v2 or DynamoDB On-Demand. Predictable DB load + long-term → RDS Reserved Instances or DynamoDB Provisioned. DynamoDB provisioned = cheaper per request than on-demand (if traffic is predictable). Redshift Spectrum = extend Redshift to query cold S3 data without loading (avoids extra storage cost). 💡 Caching reduces latency + database load. DynamoDB + read heavy + microseconds needed → DAX. RDS + repeated queries + sub-ms latency → ElastiCache Redis or Memcached. Redis vs Memcached: Redis = more features (persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets, backup, Multi-AZ). Memcached = simpler, multi-threaded (scales horizontally for pure throughput). Global static content → CloudFront (CDN). Lazy Loading = cache on miss (stale risk). Write-through = always up to date (write overhead). TTL = controls staleness. Too short = too many DB hits. Too long = stale data. 💡 ELB type selection: Web application (HTTP/HTTPS) + routing rules + WAF → ALB. Extreme performance (millions RPS), static IP, TCP/UDP, source IP preservation → NLB. Third-party security appliances (firewall, IDS) inline → GWLB. Need PrivateLink endpoint → NLB. WebSocket → ALB. Key: ALB = smart routing at L7. NLB = dumb fast routing at L4. NLB has static IP per AZ (useful for whitelisting). ALB has dynamic IPs. ❌ Eliminate distractors: Aurora Replicas share the same distributed storage volume as the primary — you don't pay for N copies of the storage for N replicas. RDS Read Replicas each have a full copy of the data, so storage costs multiply with each replica. For read-heavy workloads needing multiple read replicas, Aurora is more cost-effective in storage. Aurora also provides Aurora reader endpoint load balancing natively. DynamoDB doesn't serve relational workloads.

q: A company wants to track their AWS costs by project and team across a single AWS account with hundreds of resources. What is the AWS-recommended approach for cost allocation?
options: Use CloudFormation StackSets to organize resources and track costs per stack
Use AWS Service Catalog to organize resources by team
Apply AWS resource tags consistently (e.g., Project: ProjectA, Team: Engineering) and enable cost allocation tags in the Billing console — Cost Explorer can then break down costs by tag values
Create separate AWS accounts for each team and use consolidated billing
explain: Cost allocation tags enabled in the Billing console allow Cost Explorer to filter and group costs by tag values. q: A company uses AWS Direct Connect at 1 Gbps between their data center and AWS. They're also paying for a Site-to-Site VPN as backup. Direct Connect utilization averages 15%. How can they optimize costs?
options: Add more Direct Connect connections for redundancy

Evaluate whether a smaller Direct Connect port (100 Mbps or 500 Mbps via hosted connections) meets the bandwidth requirement at lower port cost, or use MACsec-encrypted 1 Gbps Direct Connect to eliminate the VPN cost if security was the VPN's purpose
Switch to AWS Transit Gateway instead of Direct Connect
Cancel Direct Connect and use only the VPN backup
explain: Direct Connect is charged per port-hour. A 1 Gbps port costs more than a 100 Mbps or 500 Mbps hosted connection (available via partners). At 15% utilization (~150 Mbps average), a 500 Mbps connection may suffice at lower cost. If the VPN was providing encryption for Direct Connect, MACsec on Direct Connect eliminates the need for a separate VPN. Canceling Direct Connect loses its performance and reliability benefits. Adding connections increases costs.

SSE-C = you manage key externally, provide per-request, AWS never stores. Client-side = encrypt before upload (max security, most complexity). Encryption in transit: always HTTPS/TLS. q: A company stores 50 TB of data in Amazon EFS for a machine learning training workflow. Training jobs run once per week. Between runs, the data sits idle. What S3 cost optimization applies here?
options: Mount multiple EFS file systems to spread data across cheaper tiers
Replace EFS with S3 for machine learning training data

Use EFS Intelligent Tiering — data that hasn't been accessed for 7 days moves to EFS Infrequent Access (EFS-IA) storage at 92% lower cost automatically, while remaining instantly accessible for the next training run
Delete data after each training run and reload next time
explain: EFS Intelligent Tiering automatically moves files unused for 7 days to EFS-IA ($0.016/GB vs $0.30/GB for standard EFS in us-east-1 — ~92% savings). Files move back to standard storage instantly when accessed for the next training run. This is zero-code, zero-configuration after enabling Intelligent Tiering. Deleting and reloading data wastes time and compute. S3 with FSx for Lustre is another pattern but involves migration.

AWS Trusted Advisor with automated cost optimization recommendations
explain: AWS Budgets Actions can automatically apply SCPs, target EC2/RDS instances for stop/terminate, or apply IAM policies when budget thresholds are hit. This provides automated enforcement (not just alerting). Actions can be configured at 80%, 90%, and 100% of budget with escalating responses (alert → restrict → stop). Cost Anomaly Detection sends alerts only. Trusted Advisor provides recommendations but no enforcement.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: prevent across org (even root) → SCP on root OU. 📋 Task 4.2 — Cost-optimized compute solutions: Compute cost decision matrix: Workload type → pricing model: Predictable 24/7 + 1-3 year → Reserved Instance or EC2 Savings Plan (72% off). Variable but consistent spend → Compute Savings Plans (most flexible). Fault-tolerant batch, stateless, resumable → Spot (90% off). Variable/unknown traffic → On-Demand (no commitment). Infrequent event-driven → Lambda (pay per invocation, zero idle cost). Rightsizing before purchasing commitments: Compute Optimizer → rightsizing → THEN buy RI/SP. Key rule: spot = interruptible only. Do NOT use Spot for: databases, web servers, stateful apps. 💡 IAM policy evaluation: deny wins always. Order: Explicit Deny > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary > session policy. Cross-account access: Role with trust policy + AssumeRole. OR resource-based policy (S3, KMS, SQS). Resource-based policy allows same-account AND cross-account access without AssumeRole. SCP = guardrail at org level, cannot grant permissions, only restrict. Permission boundary = limits what a role/user can do even if their policy grants more. AWS RAM = share VPCs, subnets, Transit Gateways across accounts (no peering required). 💡 Well-Architected questions: map the scenario to the right pillar. Uptime/failover → Reliability. Encryption/access control → Security. Fast/low-latency → Performance Efficiency. Save money → Cost Optimization. Automate operations → Operational Excellence. Reduce carbon footprint → Sustainability. ❌ Eliminate distractors: AWS Budgets Actions can automatically apply SCPs, target EC2/RDS instances for stop/terminate, or apply IAM policies when budget thresholds are hit. This provides automated enforcement (not just alerting). Actions can be configured at 80%, 90%, and 100% of budget with escalating responses (alert → restrict → stop). Cost Anomaly Detection sends alerts only. Trusted Advisor provides recommendations but no enforcement.

q: A company processes video files uploaded to S3. Processing happens immediately after upload using a Lambda trigger. They discover 40% of uploaded files are duplicates (same content, different filenames) and are being processed multiple times. What cost optimization reduces duplicate processing?
options: Use Amazon Rekognition to detect duplicate videos

Calculate a hash (MD5/SHA256) of each file on upload and check against a DynamoDB table of processed file hashes — skip processing if hash already exists, reducing duplicate Lambda processing costs by 40%
Use S3 Object Lock to prevent duplicate uploads
Use S3 Versioning to detect duplicate content
explain: Content-based deduplication using a hash stored in DynamoDB prevents reprocessing identical files. The Lambda function checks the DynamoDB hash table before processing — if the hash exists, skip. This reduces Lambda execution costs and downstream storage costs by 40% in this scenario. S3 Versioning tracks file versions, not content duplication. Rekognition is for video analysis, not deduplication. S3 Object Lock prevents deletion, not duplicate uploads.
hint: 💰 Domain 4 (20%): Design Cost-Optimized Architectures. Key task statements: 4.1 Storage (Lifecycle/S3 tiers), 4.2 Compute (Spot/RI/Savings Plans/rightsizing), 4.3 Database (Reserved/Serverless), 4.4 Network (endpoints/data transfer cost). 🔍 Exam clues in this question: multi-AZ = eliminate single-AZ failure. Always for: RDS, ElastiCache, ALB, EFS. 📋 Task 4.3 — Cost-optimized database solutions: DB cost: match pricing model to traffic pattern. Variable DB load → Aurora Serverless v2 or DynamoDB On-Demand. Predictable DB load + long-term → RDS Reserved Instances or DynamoDB Provisioned. DynamoDB provisioned = cheaper per request than on-demand (if traffic is predictable). Redshift Spectrum = extend Redshift to query cold S3 data without loading (avoids extra storage cost). 💡 Serverless = no server management, pay per use, auto-scale. Lambda limits: 15 min max, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, 1000 concurrent (soft). Cold start: Lambda function first invocation takes longer. Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm. Lambda@Edge = run lightweight JS/Python at CloudFront edge (max 5s). Serverless API pattern: API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Long-running process (>15 min) → NOT Lambda (use Fargate, Batch, or EC2). Serverless event processing: EventBridge rule → Lambda → downstream. 💡 S3 is key-value object storage, not a file system. Access control: Bucket policy (resource-based) + IAM (identity-based) + ACL (legacy). Presigned URL = share private object for limited time (e.g., 15 min download link). Versioning must be ON for: replication, MFA Delete, Object Lock. WORM compliance → Object Lock Compliance mode. Large file upload → Multipart (parallel parts). S3 Transfer Acceleration ≠ CloudFront. TA = accelerate UPLOADS via edge. CF = serve DOWNLOADS from edge. Event-driven processing of new S3 objects → S3 Events → Lambda (or SQS, SNS). ❌ Eliminate distractors: Content-based deduplication using a hash stored in DynamoDB prevents reprocessing identical files. The Lambda function checks the DynamoDB hash table before processing — if the hash exists, skip. This reduces Lambda execution costs and downstream storage costs by 40% in this scenario. S3 Versioning tracks file versions, not content duplication. Rekognition is for video analysis, not deduplication. S3 Object Lock prevents deletion, not duplicate uploads.
